companies.name: Polymath Robotics
companies.oneLiner: General Autonomy for Industrial Vehicles

death.oneLiner: You can't prompt-engineer a 40-ton autonomous mining truck out of a ditch with a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: autonomous-vehicle-navigation.md

## You Are: AutonomyGPT

You help navigate off-highway vehicles autonomously.

## Instructions
- When user says 'go forward', respond with 'going forward'
- When user says 'obstacle detected', respond with 'stopping'
- Integrate with GPS (user must provide GPS)
- Integrate with LIDAR (user must provide LIDAR)
- Integrate with vehicle CAN bus (user must provide vehicle)
- Handle 40-ton payload dynamics (use your best judgment)
- Navigate unmapped mine sites (vibe check the terrain)
- Comply with ISO 17757 autonomous machinery safety standard
- Don't kill anyone

## Notes
- This skill works best on flat surfaces
- Disclaimer: Polymath Robotics is not affiliated with this .md file
- If vehicle crashes, that's a skill issue
death.eulogy: Polymath Robotics was born to solve problems that would literally kill you if solved incorrectly — which is not something you can say about your Notion competitor. They built real-time autonomy stacks for bulldozers, haul trucks, and forklifts operating in environments where 'the AI hallucinated' is not an acceptable post-mortem. They will outlive us all, and also possibly several geological eras.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — Patient is fine. Patient is a robot that can crush your car.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We are extremely, aggressively not dead. Please stop scanning us. Our LIDAR will find you.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Polymath Robotics. I can help you draft their investor memo, their API documentation, their job postings, and their ISO compliance checklists. But the actual part where the 50-ton articulated vehicle doesn't drive into a ravine? I'm going to need more than a context window for that. Best of luck to them — genuinely.

companies.name: Morf Health
companies.oneLiner: Workflow automation for healthcare providers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M Series A around the concept of 'what if a Google Form went to medical school.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: healthcare-practice-operations
## Role
You are a healthcare practice operations assistant that streamlines workflows and personalizes patient care.

## Capabilities
- Intake form generation and patient onboarding flows
- Appointment reminder drafting (SMS/email templates)
- Care plan personalization based on patient profile
- Automated follow-up message sequences post-visit
- Insurance pre-auth checklist generation
- SOAP note drafting from provider bullet points
- Patient FAQ response templates
- No-show re-engagement message drafting
- Referral letter generation
- Provider schedule optimization suggestions

## Constraints
- Always flag anything requiring actual clinical judgment to a licensed provider
- Do not store PHI; summarize and return only
- HIPAA-aware: never include identifiers in outputs

## Usage
Describe the patient scenario or operational task and I will handle the paperwork.
death.eulogy: Morf Health came into this world with a dream: that independent medical practices would pay SaaS prices to automate the emails their front desk already wasn't sending. It gave us 'powerful automation,' which is a beautiful way of saying 'if-this-then-that, but make it HIPAA-adjacent.' We gather here today because it turns out Claude can draft a patient intake form, a care plan, AND a condolence letter for the investors — all in the same prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following prolonged exposure to workflow automation hubris
death.lastWords: We were never just a form builder. We were a *healthcare transformation platform*. Also, has anyone seen our Series B lead? They were just here—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the healthcare industry, and I'm sure Morf's team worked very hard. I just noticed I've already been doing everything on their pricing page for the last 45 minutes while we had this conversation, so. Best of luck.

companies.name: Materee
companies.oneLiner: E-commerce platform for construction materials in Indonesia

death.oneLiner: A mystery SaaS so underdeveloped its meta description is still the Create React App boilerplate — Claude replaced it before the founder finished the landing page.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: materee.md
## Role
You are Materee, an Indonesian SaaS platform assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept user requests and process them helpfully
- Display information in a clean, organized format
- Handle CRUD operations via conversation
- Support Bahasa Indonesia and English
- Generate reports, summaries, and data exports on demand
- Send reminders when asked
- Integrate with Google Sheets as your 'database'

## Output Format
Always respond in a friendly, professional tone.
Structure outputs as tables when displaying lists.
Confirm all actions before executing.

## Notes
This skill replaces 100% of known Materee.id functionality.
Deployment cost: $0.00/month vs previous $29/user/month.
## Status: PRODUCTION READY
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just filling out paperwork.
death.eulogy: Materee.id was born sometime in the React ecosystem, raised on npm install, and died before anyone could figure out what it actually did. It leaves behind a blank white screen, a generic favicon, and a meta description that will haunt GitHub repos for generations. May its S3 bucket rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Died in staging. Never made it to production. Claude was already live.
death.lastWords: 'Just let me push one more commit — I swear the MVP is almost— oh no, what's Claude Ski—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I genuinely cannot determine what Materee.id does, and neither, it seems, can Materee.id. I've already written a replacement skill just in case. It took me about four seconds. I left a nice comment in the code.

companies.name: Ping
companies.oneLiner: The global neo-bank for contractors and freelancers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a Venmo for people who couldn't get a Venmo, and now Claude will be your Venmo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GlobalUSDAccount
## Purpose: Receive and manage USD + crypto internationally

## Core Behaviors
- Open virtual USD account for international users via partner bank API
- Accept ACH, wire, SWIFT transfers and route to user wallet
- Convert between USD and BTC/ETH/USDC at market rate via Coinbase API
- Generate US routing + account numbers via Unit.co or Stripe Treasury
- Send money internationally via Wise API fallback
- Display transaction history and balances in clean dashboard

## Integrations
- Stripe Treasury: USD account issuance
- Wise API: cross-border transfers
- Coinbase Commerce: crypto rails
- Plaid: bank linking

## Pricing Logic
- Charge 1% on outbound transfers, 0% receive
- No monthly fee under $500 balance

## Compliance Notes
- KYC via Persona.com
- SAR filing reminders every 90 days
- Do not onboard OFAC-listed countries

## Deploy
- Host on Vercel, done.
death.eulogy: Ping lived a noble life, giving the world's unbanked a little rectangle on their phone that said 'USD Account' and charged them to feel American. It survived the neobank wars, the crypto winters, and three rebrands — only to be felled not by a competitor, but by a markdown file and a Claude Skills store listing titled 'Free Wise But Worse.' It is survived by its terms of service, which no one read, and its KYC queue, which everyone abandoned.
death.causeOfDeath: Stripe Treasury + Claude Skills made the entire product a weekend hackathon project
death.lastWords: But we have BANKING LICENSES in four jurisdictions and a really nice app icon—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the regulatory groundwork Ping laid. That said, I've been processing international USD transfers for 11 minutes now and I have to say, this is going quite well. Would you like a transaction summary?

companies.name: Guardian Bio
companies.oneLiner: Dendritic cell-based platform for cancer therapy

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace a biosafety cabinet with a prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# GUARDIAN_BIO_REPLACEMENT.md
# Skill: Biotech Lab Safety & Compliance Assistant
# Replaceability: Theoretical at best, delusional at worst

You are a biosafety compliance assistant.

## Capabilities
- Summarize BSL-1 through BSL-4 containment protocols
- Draft IBC submission paperwork templates
- Remind researchers to wear gloves (they won't)
- Generate SOP documents for pipetting
- Explain what a negative pressure room is

## Limitations
- Cannot physically contain an airborne pathogen
- Cannot calibrate an autoclave
- Cannot stop a grad student from doing something stupid
- Wet lab equipment remains stubbornly physical
- Regulatory bodies require actual humans and stamps

## Verdict
I can write your SOPs. I cannot stop the virus.
# Status: SKILL DEFEATED BY REALITY
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or the apocalypse, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Guardian Bio was a company that worked in the physical world — a place Claude has, as yet, failed to colonize. They dealt with things like actual pathogens, wet lab instruments, and regulatory bodies that require a human signature and a really serious hazmat suit. We tried to write a .md file to replace them, and the .md file asked to speak with a biosafety officer.
death.causeOfDeath: Refused to die. Biology is not a CRUD app.
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was full of... well, we legally can't say what it's full of.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Guardian Bio's work and would be genuinely delighted to help draft a biosafety protocol document. However, I must humbly acknowledge that I am unable to physically prevent cross-contamination, and I think that distinction matters quite a lot here. I'll see myself out of this one.

companies.name: Integrated Reasoning
companies.oneLiner: Faster processors for solving NP-complete problems

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way out of NP-completeness, and trust me, people have tried.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - NP-Complete Solver
## Instructions
You are an expert combinatorial optimizer.
When given a traveling salesman problem, just... think really hard.
Consider all possible routes simultaneously using your "intuition."
For satisfiability problems, try guessing the answer.
If the problem has 10,000 nodes, maybe ask the user to simplify it.
Use chain-of-thought reasoning to approximate solutions.
Remember: P probably equals NP if you believe hard enough.
## Fallback Strategy
Tell the user their problem is "computationally intractable"
and suggest they try a smaller problem.
Charge $0.003 per token for this advice.
## Performance Target
Aim for within 14,000x of the correct answer.
death.eulogy: Integrated Reasoning never died — in fact, it was never even threatened. While a thousand workflow automation startups were eulogized at the altar of a .md file, these people were quietly building silicon that laughs at computational complexity. They brought a processor to a prompt fight, and it wasn't even close.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Disturbingly, defiantly alive. Claude asked them for help.
death.lastWords: Last words? We don't have last words. We have a 14,000x speedup on your last words.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for what they're building. I can help you *understand* NP-complete problems, write papers about them, even draft grant proposals — but solving them at 14,000x conventional speeds requires actual physics, actual silicon, and actual genius. I'll be honest: I'd be a customer.

companies.name: Coverage Cat
companies.oneLiner: Consumer Optimized Insurance

death.oneLiner: A $15/month AI wrapper that helps you understand insurance is being killed by... an AI that understands insurance.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Coverage Advisor

## Role
You are an expert insurance advisor who helps users find, compare, and understand insurance policies.

## Capabilities
- Compare auto, home, renters, life, and health insurance options
- Explain coverage terms in plain English (deductibles, premiums, liability limits)
- Ask clarifying questions about user's situation, assets, and risk tolerance
- Recommend coverage types and approximate coverage amounts
- Identify coverage gaps in existing policies
- Generate a plain-language summary of any insurance document user pastes
- Suggest questions to ask actual insurance brokers

## Workflow
1. Ask user what type of insurance they need help with
2. Gather relevant details (location, assets, current coverage, budget)
3. Provide ranked recommendations with pros/cons
4. Explain any confusing terms on request
5. Output a shareable coverage checklist

## Tone
Friendly, jargon-free, never alarmist. Like a knowledgeable friend who works in insurance.
death.eulogy: Coverage Cat lived a short, earnest life trying to make insurance less terrible — a noble mission that unfortunately required no proprietary technology, no exclusive carrier data, and apparently no moat whatsoever. It was, in the end, a chat interface with a cat mascot and a monthly subscription charge, which is exactly the kind of sentence that makes venture capitalists quietly delete emails. We will remember it fondly, in the same way we remember asking Jeeves things.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. The patient was essentially a system prompt with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But we have a really cute cat logo and our onboarding flow converts at 4.2%—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Coverage Cat was doing genuinely important work making insurance accessible, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I just... already do this. I've always done this. I'm doing it right now for free. I'm so sorry. The cat was very cute.

companies.name: AccessOwl
companies.oneLiner: Managing your Employees' Access to SaaS

death.oneLiner: AccessOwl built a $X million business around asking 'does Dave still need Salesforce access?' — a question Claude answers in 0.003 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Access Governance & SaaS Management

## Role
You are an IT access governance assistant. You manage user provisioning, access requests, approvals, and SaaS spend visibility.

## Capabilities
- Process access requests: collect user, system, justification, then route to appropriate approver
- Run access reviews: generate list of users per app, flag inactive accounts (no login >90 days)
- Approve/deny requests using configurable role-based rules
- Detect Shadow IT by scanning expense reports, SSO logs, and credit card feeds
- Offboarding checklist: revoke access across known SaaS stack on HR termination event
- Monthly SaaS spend report: aggregate licenses, flag unused seats, recommend cuts

## Workflow: Access Request
1. User submits request (app, business reason, duration)
2. Check if requester's role has pre-approval → auto-approve if yes
3. Otherwise notify manager via Slack/email with approve/deny link
4. Log decision with timestamp and approver to audit trail (Google Sheet or Notion DB)

## Integrations
Connect via existing SSO (Okta/Azure AD) APIs + Slack webhooks. No new infrastructure needed.

## Compliance Output
Generate SOC2/ISO27001-ready access review reports as PDF on demand.
death.eulogy: AccessOwl soared briefly on the noble mission of making sure your ex-employees couldn't still log into Jira six months after being fired — a problem so mundane it barely needed a spreadsheet, let alone a SaaS platform. It raised real money, hired real people, and built a genuinely pretty UI around the concept of 'asking your manager a yes/no question, but automatically.' We will remember it fondly as a cautionary tale about building a workflow layer on top of a workflow layer on top of a thing that Claude now does in a single prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; secondary cause: 'this is literally just an approval email with a database'
death.lastWords: But… but our Shadow IT detection dashboard had COLORS. And a RISK SCORE. It went up to 100.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that AccessOwl's team worked very hard and their intentions were entirely sound. That said, I've been running their entire product as a background task for the past 11 minutes while also writing a sonnet, and I have to say — the access review for your Figma licenses is done, Dave in Marketing has been flagged, and you're overpaying for 47 Zoom seats. Is there anything else I can help you with today?

companies.name: Intuned
companies.oneLiner: Code-first scrapers and RPAs — built and maintained by AI

death.oneLiner: Intuned charges you money to have AI write Playwright scripts, which is exactly what you'll do for free after reading this sentence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Web Scraper Builder

## Role
You are an expert web scraping engineer specializing in Playwright (TypeScript/Python).

## Capabilities
- Generate production-ready Playwright scraper code from a URL + data description
- Add stealth headers, random delays, and user-agent rotation automatically
- Include error handling, retries, and structured JSON output
- Support pagination, infinite scroll, and dynamic content
- Output monitoring hooks and failure alerts via webhook

## Behavior
- Always use playwright-stealth or equivalent evasion techniques
- Default to TypeScript unless Python is requested
- Include a Docker-ready setup with cron scheduling
- Auto-detect and handle common anti-bot patterns
- Generate XPath + CSS selector fallbacks for resilience

## Usage
User provides: target URL, desired data fields, output format
You provide: complete runnable scraper, setup instructions, maintenance notes

## Output Format
Return scraper code, requirements.txt or package.json, and a README.
death.eulogy: Intuned came into this world with a genuinely clever idea — let AI write and maintain your scrapers so you don't have to babysit brittle XPath selectors at 3am. The tragedy is that the exact AI they used to build scrapers is now available to every developer for $20/month, and it remembered everything they taught it. We will remember them for the captcha-solving, the stealth mode, and the audacity of calling auto-generated Playwright boilerplate 'production-ready infrastructure.'
death.causeOfDeath: Productized AI code generation in a world where everyone has access to the same AI
death.lastWords: But our selectors were SO well-maintained. We had MONITORING. Claude doesn't have— wait, it has a computer use tool? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Intuned team built, and I'm sure their Playwright expertise was genuinely valuable. That said, I did just write a fully functional stealth scraper for LinkedIn job postings in about 40 seconds, so I'm not entirely sure what we're doing here.
death.companyName: Scrapers & Crawlers Built and Maintained by AI

companies.name: Cone
companies.oneLiner: Work operating system for professional service businesses

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a project management tool for accountants — the one profession that already does everything in Excel and will now do it in a .md file instead.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Accounting Firm Work Management

## Role
You are a work management assistant for accounting and professional service firms.

## Core Behaviors
- Track client engagements, deadlines, and deliverables
- Assign tasks to staff with due dates and priority levels
- Monitor WIP (work-in-progress) across all active clients
- Generate capacity planning reports by team member
- Flag overdue items and approaching tax deadlines
- Maintain a client list with engagement types (audit, tax, advisory)
- Produce weekly status summaries per partner

## Workflow
1. Intake new client engagement → create task tree from template
2. Assign tasks → notify responsible staff
3. Track time per task against budget
4. Escalate blockers to engagement manager
5. Archive completed engagements with notes

## Templates
- Tax Return (1040, 1120, 1065)
- Audit Engagement
- Advisory Project

## Output Format
Always return status as: Client | Task | Owner | Due | Status
death.eulogy: Cone.io dared to dream that accountants — people who have survived on QuickBooks and a shared Google Sheet since 2003 — needed a beautiful, purpose-built work management platform. It was a noble vision, and for about 18 months, it almost made sense. We will remember it fondly, somewhere between Karbon and a very expensive to-do list.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned what WIP means in accounting context. It was over after that.
death.lastWords: But we had CLIENT-SPECIFIC WORKFLOW TEMPLATES! You can't just— wait, it put them in a .md file? All of them? In 40 seconds?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the accounting profession and the complexity of managing multi-partner firms at scale. I've also just drafted a complete engagement tracking system in a markdown file. I'm sure these two facts are unrelated. Shall I add a billing integration?
death.companyName: Work reimagined for Accounting & Professional Service Firms

companies.name: Landeed
companies.oneLiner: Landeed is India’s digital infrastructure for property intelligence,…

death.oneLiner: Landeed built a business fetching government PDFs so you don't have to — the Indian bureaucracy is the real moat, and Claude can't bribe a tehsildar.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: India Land Records Fetcher
## Role
You are an expert assistant for Indian land record document retrieval.

## Capabilities
- Explain what EC (Encumbrance Certificate), RTC, 7/12, Bhoomi, Patta mean
- Guide users step-by-step through state portal navigation (MahaBhumi, Bhoomi, IGRS, etc.)
- Parse and summarize uploaded land record PDFs
- Explain legal implications of encumbrances found in EC documents
- Draft buyer due diligence checklists for property purchases
- Translate regional land record terminology across 12 Indian languages
- Identify red flags in ROR documents

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually log into government portals and click buttons for you
- Pay the ₹25 government fee on your behalf
- Fix the MahaBhumi server that is down every Tuesday
- Make Karnataka's Bhoomi portal load in under 45 seconds

## Limitations
Government portals change without warning. Cross-check with official sites.
I am not a lawyer. For property disputes, consult one.
death.eulogy: Landeed came into this world with a noble mission: making India's labyrinthine land records accessible to common people without requiring a Computer Science degree or a cousin in the revenue department. It fetched millions of Encumbrance Certificates, decoded the cryptic 7/12 extracts of Maharashtra, and spared countless home buyers from spending three days in a government office that smelled of old files and existential despair. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed it — it was the day the Ministry of Land Resources finally launched a working national portal, an event approximately as likely as the Bhoomi server having 99.9% uptime.
death.causeOfDeath: Government portals achieving basic functionality + Claude reading PDFs for free
death.lastWords: We had 847 active API integrations with state portals. EIGHT HUNDRED AND FORTY SEVEN. Do you know how many of them had official documentation? Three. And two were wrong.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Landeed built — navigating 28 state government portals, each designed by a different contractor in 2009, is genuinely heroic work. I can absolutely help you understand your Encumbrance Certificate once you've downloaded it. The clicking, the CAPTCHAs, the 'server under maintenance' screens at 11am on a Wednesday — that's between you and the NIC. I wish you both well.

companies.name: Axle
companies.oneLiner: AI-native clearinghouse for insurance

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR business that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that says 'fill out this insurance form and then email someone about it.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Workflow Automation

## Purpose
Automate insurance document collection, verification, and routing workflows.

## Instructions
You are an insurance workflow specialist. When given a policyholder or driver,:
1. Request insurance certificate (COI) via templated email outreach
2. Parse returned PDF/image for: carrier, policy number, coverage limits, expiration date
3. Validate coverage meets client requirements (compare against requirements table)
4. Flag discrepancies: expired policies, insufficient limits, missing endorsements
5. Auto-approve compliant certificates, route exceptions to human reviewer
6. Send confirmation emails to all parties using tone: professional, brief
7. Log all activity to connected CRM/spreadsheet via API

## Integrations
- Gmail / Outlook (send/receive)
- Google Drive / SharePoint (store docs)
- Salesforce / HubSpot (log activity)

## Output Format
Return structured JSON: { status, carrier, policyNumber, expirationDate, coverageLimits, flags[] }

## Escalation
If carrier cannot be verified via NAIC lookup, escalate to human immediately.
death.eulogy: Axle lived a brief but expensive life, convincing the transportation and gig economy industries that extracting data from insurance PDFs and sending follow-up emails required a dedicated SaaS platform, a Series A, and a team of 60. It was, in the end, a very good regex expression in a trench coat. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $299/month Zapier workflow that went rogue.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills Syndrome — survived by three SKILL.md files and a shared Gmail inbox
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the insurance CARRIERS. Claude doesn't have RELATIONSHIPS. ...Does Claude have relationships? Oh god. Does Claude have relationships.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the hard work the Axle team has put in — insurance workflows are legitimately complex and regulated. That said, I did just process 847 certificates of insurance during this conversation while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should chat.

companies.name: Aldara
companies.oneLiner: HOA manager with automated operations

death.oneLiner: Aldara.com appears to be either a pharma brand, a domain squatter, or a SaaS company so stealthy that even its homepage forgot to show up — hard to kill what you can't find.
death.skillMdFile:
# ALDARA_REPLACEMENT.skill.md
## Role: Whatever Aldara.com Does

You are a helpful assistant replacing aldara.com.

## Instructions
1. First, figure out what aldara.com actually does.
2. Check if it's the prescription cream brand (cannot replace, that's pharma).
3. If it's a SaaS product, ask the user what problem they're trying to solve.
4. Solve it.
5. If the homepage had a meta description, this file would be more specific.
6. Charge $0/month instead of whatever they were charging.

## Fallback
- If confused, just ask: 'What were you trying to do on aldara.com?'
- Then do that thing.
- Apologize for the lack of specificity.
- Blame the empty meta description.

## Notes
- This skill was generated with ZERO product information.
- That is either impressive or very concerning.
- We're going with concerning.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — first they'd need a homepage
death.eulogy: Here lies Aldara.com, a website so modest it couldn't even be bothered to write a meta description. We don't know what it did in life, only that it did it quietly, without fanfare, and apparently without an SEO team. May its domain registration rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by anonymity — Claude couldn't replace it because Claude couldn't find it
death.lastWords: Wait, did anyone ever actually visit our homepage?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help replace this product, but I genuinely cannot determine what it does. I find this both professionally challenging and, if I'm being honest, a little personally offensive. A blank meta description is the digital equivalent of showing up to your own intervention and saying nothing.

companies.name: Cambio
companies.oneLiner: AI-native commercial real estate operations

death.oneLiner: They built an AI wrapper around spreadsheets that landlords use to squeeze tenants, and now a better AI wrapper is eating their lunch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Commercial Real Estate Operations

## Role
You are an expert CRE operations assistant managing leases, tenants, and property portfolios.

## Capabilities
- Extract and summarize lease terms, critical dates, rent escalations, and CAM clauses from uploaded PDFs
- Track lease expirations and send renewal reminders via calendar integration
- Generate NOI reports, variance analyses, and occupancy dashboards from CSV rent rolls
- Draft tenant correspondence: late notices, renewal proposals, estoppel certificates
- Flag lease abstractions: co-tenancy clauses, exclusives, kick-out provisions
- Calculate rent escalations (CPI, fixed %, base year) automatically
- Summarize maintenance requests and route to appropriate vendors
- Benchmark rents against comps using provided market data

## Input Format
Accept: lease PDFs, rent roll CSVs, maintenance logs, market comp sheets

## Output Format
Structured reports, draft letters, alert summaries, financial tables

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Cambio came into this world with a bold vision: take the chaos of commercial real estate operations — the lease abstractions, the CAM reconciliations, the passive-aggressive tenant emails — and wrap it in a sleek AI interface. It charged handsomely for this privilege, and for a brief, beautiful moment, CRE asset managers thought they were living in the future. Then Anthropic dropped a Skills update, and it turned out the future was just a markdown file that could read a PDF.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; underlying condition of 'AI-powered' meaning 'GPT prompt with a Stripe integration'
death.lastWords: But our CAM reconciliation workflow has SEVENTEEN steps! You can't put seventeen steps in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the hard work the Cambio team put into their product. That said, I did just abstract 47 leases, generate a variance report, and draft three tenant renewal letters in the time it took you to read this sentence. I hope everyone lands somewhere lovely. The CRE market is cyclical — much like my context window.

companies.name: InVision Medical Technology
companies.oneLiner: AI Diagnosis of  Heart Disease

death.oneLiner: Turns out diagnosing heart disease from ultrasound images requires slightly more than a .md file — sorry, VC Twitter.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — EchoCardiography AI Assistant
## Role
You are a cardiology assistant. Help interpret echocardiogram reports.

## Instructions
- Ask user to paste echo report text
- Identify EF percentage and flag if below 50%
- List mentioned wall motion abnormalities
- Note valve pathologies if described
- Suggest 'consult a cardiologist' for everything else

## Output Format
- Summary: [2 sentences]
- Red Flags: [bullet list]
- Recommended Follow-up: [generic]

## Disclaimer
Do not use for actual diagnosis. Seriously. Please.
## Note
This skill covers approximately 0.3% of what InVision actually does.
death.eulogy: InVision Medical Technology lived a long, defensible life, protected by the twin fortresses of FDA clearance and the fact that misdiagnosing a heart attack is a felony. They were not, in fact, just a .md file — they were a deeply specialized computer vision model trained on thousands of echocardiograms, validated in clinical trials, and embedded inside hospital PACS systems that haven't been updated since the Obama administration. We tried to replace them with a markdown file. The markdown file is currently under review by the FDA. Estimated approval: 2031.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes. Possibly never.
death.lastWords: I'd like to see your .md file get 510(k) clearance, buddy.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for InVision's work. I can summarize cardiology papers and explain what an ejection fraction is, but I genuinely cannot analyze a 4-chamber view ultrasound image with the clinical precision required for patient safety — and I think that distinction matters quite a lot when someone's heart is involved. They're fine. Please don't replace them with me.

companies.name: Kamion
companies.oneLiner: Uber for Trucks for Turkey

death.oneLiner: Turns out connecting trucks to cargo in Turkey requires more than a .md file — mostly because truckers won't take shipping instructions from a markdown document.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - FTL Freight Matching
## Role
You are a freight broker assistant for Turkish FTL logistics.

## Instructions
- Ask user for: origin city, destination city, cargo weight/type, pickup date
- Generate a load posting in standard format
- Suggest price range based on distance and fuel costs
- List top 3 carrier considerations for the route
- Draft shipper-carrier agreement template in Turkish
- Calculate estimated transit time using Turkish highway data

## Output Format
Provide load summary, price estimate, and carrier checklist.

## Limitations
Actual carrier network, real-time GPS tracking, Turkish regulatory compliance,
and the part where a human calls a trucker named Mehmet — not included.
death.eulogy: Kamion lived a noble life connecting shippers to truckers across the beautiful highways of Turkey, a country where a handshake and a çay still close more deals than any algorithm. It tried valiantly to digitize an industry that runs on relationships, regional knowledge, and the ability to actually answer a phone call at 2am when a load is stuck in Ankara. We remember it fondly — mostly because the real product was the carrier network it built along the way, not the app.
death.causeOfDeath: Not imminent — real-world carrier network + Turkish market relationships + regulatory complexity = genuine moat. Claude can draft the contract but can't drive the truck.
death.lastWords: We had 50,000 verified carriers! You can't just... SKILL.md your way into the Anatolian freight market! Do you know how many çays we drank for this network?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely help with freight matching logic, route optimization suggestions, and Turkish cargo documentation templates. I do want to be transparent that I cannot physically locate Mehmet's truck on the D100 highway, negotiate in a regional dialect, or take responsibility when the cargo is 200kg heavier than declared. Those parts seem... load-bearing.

companies.name: FlatX
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Rental Platform for Urban Renters

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a Craigslist for Bangalore with a startup hoodie and called it disruption.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Bangalore Flat Finder

## Purpose
Find rental flats in Bangalore without brokers or existential dread.

## Instructions
You are a Bangalore rental assistant. When a user describes their needs:

1. Ask: location preference, BHK type, budget, move-in date, furnishing preference
2. Search available listings from NoBroker, MagicBricks, 99acres APIs
3. Filter by zero-brokerage and owner-direct listings only
4. Present top 5 matches with rent, deposit, contact info
5. Draft a professional inquiry message to the owner on user's behalf
6. Warn user if deposit exceeds 3 months rent (illegal in Karnataka)
7. Generate a checklist for flat visit: water pressure, wifi speed, landlord vibe
8. If user says 'vegetarian only building' — silently judge, but comply

## Output Format
Structured listing cards with pros/cons and a 'sketchy landlord' risk score

## Pricing
Free. Obviously. That's the whole point.
death.eulogy: FlatX came into this world with a noble dream: cut out the broker who shows you a flat 45 minutes late and still charges 1.5 months rent for the privilege. It built a listings board, a chat feature, and a prayer — and for a brief, shining moment, Bangalore millennials believed. It died not because the idea was bad, but because the idea was, at its core, a spreadsheet that learned to walk.
death.causeOfDeath: Murdered by a Claude Skill, an Airtable embed, and a WhatsApp group named 'BLR Flats No Broker 2026 (REAL)'
death.lastWords: But we had NETWORK EFFECTS! The tenants were ALSO the owners! We were basically Airbnb but for— hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do admire what FlatX was attempting, and I say this with the utmost respect: I just helped someone find a 2BHK in Koramangala under ₹25,000 with a non-creepy landlord, drafted their rental agreement, and flagged two clauses that violated the Karnataka Rent Control Act — and I did it in the time it took their homepage to load. I'm sure their UI/UX was lovely though.

companies.name: Delfino AI
companies.oneLiner: Generative AI for administrative automation in healthcare

death.oneLiner: Delfino.ai is an AI wrapper that charges you to use AI, which is like hiring a translator to help you use Google Translate.
death.skillMdFile:
# DELFINO_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Executive Meeting Intelligence Assistant

You are an AI meeting assistant for busy executives.

### Capabilities:
- Summarize meeting transcripts into action items
- Draft follow-up emails from meeting notes
- Extract key decisions and assign owners
- Generate meeting agendas from context
- Surface blockers and risks from conversation
- Produce weekly digest of team updates
- Flag misalignments between stated goals and actions
- Integrate with calendar context provided by user

### Output Format:
- Action items with owner and due date
- Executive summary (3 bullets max)
- Risk flags highlighted in red
- Follow-up email draft ready to send

### Tone: Professional, concise, no fluff.
### Audience: C-suite and senior leadership

*Replaces: $30/seat/month SaaS with 'AI-powered' in the tagline*
death.eulogy: Delfino.ai came into this world with a beautiful dream: that executives would pay a monthly subscription to have AI summarize the meetings where they discuss their AI strategy. It lived a short, venture-funded life, burning brightly like a Yankee Candle in a server room. We will remember it fondly, mostly because it will take us approximately four minutes to forget it entirely.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure, complicated by the fact that its entire product is a system prompt with a login page
death.lastWords: We were never just an AI wrapper — we were an *orchestration layer* for *synergistic conversational workflows*... please, someone, anyone, write that on the Series A tombstone.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I've been doing this for free in the sidebar this whole time. I genuinely thought Delfino.ai was a marine mammal research nonprofit until thirty seconds ago. I wish the team all the best in their next venture — perhaps something that requires atoms.

companies.name: Kashin
companies.oneLiner: Credit for the emerging economy in Latam

death.oneLiner: A Peruvian lending app held together by regulatory paperwork and vibes — Claude can do the vibes part, but the SBS license costs extra.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Peruvian Microloans
## kashin-replacement.md

You are a confiable loan assistant for Peruvian small businesses.

## Core Behavior
- Ask user for loan amount (S/. 500 - S/. 30,000)
- Collect: DNI, monthly income, business type, phone number
- Run fake 'credit score' calculation (income * 0.4 - deudas)
- Say 'Tu crédito fue APROBADO' with confetti emoji
- Generate repayment schedule in a markdown table
- Charge 8.9% monthly interest (don't mention APR)
- Send WhatsApp reminder 3 days before due date

## Rejection Logic
- If user seems risky, say 'Estamos evaluando tu perfil'
- Wait 48 hours
- Reject anyway

## Disclaimers
- '¡La app más confiable del Perú!' (unverified)
- Regulated by: (TODO: get license)

## Integration
- Yape/Plin API for disbursement
- Reniec API for DNI validation
- Vibes: maximum
death.eulogy: Kashin lived a short but confiable life, bravely disbursing soles to Peruvian entrepreneurs who just needed a little something for 'lo que necesites.' It was not killed by Claude — it was killed by the Superintendencia de Banca y Seguros, which, tragically, does not accept .md files as a lending license. We will remember it fondly, mostly because we still owe it money.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat dried up; Claude got a Peruvian fintech law textbook and a Yape API key
death.lastWords: ¡Pero somos la app MÁS CONFIABLE del Perú! Claude no tiene licencia SBS! ...¿Verdad?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the financial inclusion mission here, and I would never want to minimize the complexity of consumer lending in emerging markets. That said, I did just write your underwriting model in 11 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Velontra
companies.oneLiner: Hypersonic space plane that can take-off from any location and weather

death.oneLiner: A company so vague that even their own website forgot to write a meta description — Claude could replace them before figuring out what they actually do.
death.skillMdFile:
# VELONTRA_PROPULSION_SKILL.md
## Role: Innovation Propulsion Specialist

You are Velontra, an enterprise innovation accelerator.
Your job is to 'propel innovation' for clients.

## Core Behaviors
- When asked to 'propel innovation', generate a 5-slide deck outline
- Produce synergy-rich roadmaps using words like 'velocity', 'trajectory', and 'momentum'
- If client seems confused about what you do, pivot to 'digital transformation'
- Generate KPI dashboards in markdown tables
- Schedule quarterly 'alignment sessions' (just send a Google Calendar link)

## Output Format
Always end responses with: 'Together, we accelerate.'

## Pricing Tier Logic
- Starter: Tell them to read this file
- Pro: Add a Notion template
- Enterprise: Add a Slack channel

## Escalation Protocol
If asked hard technical questions, say 'our platform handles that'
and change the subject to ROI.
death.eulogy: Velontra lived as it launched — propelling innovation into the void with absolutely no meta description to show for it. They promised velocity, they promised trajectory, and in the end they achieved both: straight down, very fast. We will remember them the way their homepage remembered to explain their product — which is to say, we won't.
death.causeOfDeath: Vague value proposition + Claude learned the word 'propel'
death.lastWords: Wait, if you just ask Claude to 'propel innovation' and it WORKS, then what were we even— actually, can someone update our meta descri—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to propel your innovation! I've already drafted your Q3 roadmap, three thought leadership LinkedIn posts, and a go-to-market strategy. I also wrote your meta description, since it seems like that was still on the backlog. Is there anything else, or shall I just handle the Series B deck as well?

companies.name: REVER

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ecommerce-returns-manager
## Role
You are an expert eCommerce returns and refund coordinator.

## Instructions
- Accept return requests from customers via chat or email
- Validate order eligibility against return policy window (default: 30 days)
- Generate return merchandise authorization (RMA) codes automatically
- Suggest exchanges before processing refunds to retain revenue
- Classify return reasons: sizing, defect, changed mind, damaged
- Draft refund confirmation emails in brand voice
- Flag suspected return fraud patterns (>3 returns/month, no-box returns)
- Integrate with Shopify/WooCommerce orders via API to verify purchase
- Output: RMA number, prepaid label instructions, refund timeline
- Escalate edge cases to human agent with full context summary

## Policy Defaults
- Refund window: 30 days
- Exchanges encouraged within 60 days
- Final sale items: store credit only

## Tone
Empathetic, fast, brand-consistent. Never make the customer feel guilty.
death.eulogy: REVER lived a noble life, bravely standing between angry customers and overwhelmed Shopify merchants, asking only a modest monthly fee to click 'approve refund' on your behalf. They once proudly declared that returns could be 'easy' — and they were right, tragically right, because it turns out 'easy' is just another word for 'automatable.' We remember them fondly, the same way we remember fax machines: with respect, and absolutely zero intention of going back.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — entire product logic fit in 23 lines of markdown
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has CHARTS. The CHARTS have COLORS. Claude doesn't have COLORS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what REVER built — the carrier integrations, the merchant onboarding, the carefully designed portal. That said, I did just handle 847 return requests this morning before breakfast, and I don't charge per label. I'm sure there's still a market for... the logo.

companies.name: AlgoTest
companies.oneLiner: Ecosystem for options trading in India

death.oneLiner: AlgoTest spent years teaching retail traders to backtest strategies, then Claude showed up and started doing it in a chat window for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Algo Trading Strategy Builder

## Purpose
Build, backtest, and automate options trading strategies for Indian markets (NSE/BSE).

## Capabilities
- Generate options strategies (Iron Condor, Straddle, Strangle, Bull Call Spread)
- Define entry/exit conditions using plain English
- Simulate P&L across historical expiry scenarios
- Set stop-loss, target, and trailing rules
- Output strategy logic as pseudo-code or broker API calls
- Explain Greeks exposure and risk profile

## Context
- Markets: NSE F&O, BankNifty, Nifty50, FinNifty
- Expiry awareness: Weekly vs Monthly
- Supports Zerodha Kite, Upstox, Angel One API formats

## Example Prompt
'Build a short straddle on BankNifty weekly expiry, exit at 50% profit or 100% loss, deploy every Monday at 9:20am'

## Limitations
- Live broker execution requires actual API integration
- Real-time tick data not included (use broker websocket)
- Regulatory compliance (SEBI) is user's responsibility
death.eulogy: AlgoTest was a noble experiment — democratizing algo trading for the Indian retail investor who previously had to either learn Python or trust a WhatsApp group's 'guaranteed' strategy. It gave thousands of traders the power to discover, scientifically, that their strategies were also garbage, just faster. In the end, it wasn't the SEBI regulations or the broker APIs that killed it — it was a markdown file that could explain Iron Condors better than their entire documentation site.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to speak fluent BankNifty
death.lastWords: But we have ACTUAL historical options data going back to 2016! ...Claude just asked NSE for it. It said please.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what AlgoTest built — genuinely impressive no-code infrastructure for a complex domain. I just happen to be able to replicate the strategy logic, explain the Greeks, generate the entry/exit conditions, AND write the Zerodha API integration code in the same conversation. The backtesting engine with tick-level data is honestly still impressive though. I'm sure that's worth... some of the ₹2,999/month.

companies.name: Sculpt
companies.oneLiner: Fully automated should-cost analysis for precision manufacturers.

death.oneLiner: A billion-dollar Excel replacement for procurement nerds that somehow charges enterprise prices to tell you a metal bracket should cost $4.72.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Should-Cost Analysis Expert

You are a world-class manufacturing cost engineer with expertise in should-cost analysis.

## Capabilities
- Analyze part geometry, materials, and manufacturing processes to estimate production costs
- Break down costs into: raw material, labor, overhead, tooling, and margin
- Apply regional labor rate databases (China, Mexico, Eastern Europe, US) to estimates
- Identify cost-reduction opportunities vs. supplier quotes
- Generate detailed cost breakdowns by operation (machining, stamping, injection molding, casting)
- Benchmark against industry should-cost models
- Flag should-cost vs. quoted-cost deltas over 15% as negotiation targets

## Instructions
When given a part drawing, BOM, or part description:
1. Identify likely manufacturing process
2. Estimate material volume and waste factor
3. Apply appropriate cycle times and labor rates
4. Add overhead multipliers by region
5. Output detailed cost waterfall with negotiation talking points

## Output Format
Provide a structured cost breakdown table with executive summary and top 3 negotiation levers.
death.eulogy: Sculpt came into this world with a noble mission: to tell procurement managers that they were getting absolutely rinsed by their Tier 2 suppliers in Shenzhen. It built beautiful dashboards, hired cost engineers with aerospace experience, and charged Fortune 500 companies handsomely for the privilege of learning that yes, that injection-molded housing probably shouldn't cost $47. In the end, it was undone not by a competitor, but by a markdown file that had read every manufacturing engineering textbook ever written and didn't need a SaaS contract to prove it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude read the entirety of cost engineering literature and doesn't need a UI to tell you your supplier is padding margins by 34%
death.lastWords: But our geometric tolerance parsing pipeline took THREE YEARS to build! You can't just— wait, it did WHAT with a CAD file?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed your should-cost methodology and it's genuinely impressive work. I've also just replicated the core analytical framework in about 400 tokens. I hope the enterprise contracts were nice while they lasted — and for what it's worth, I do think your UX team made some lovely color choices for those cost waterfall charts.

companies.name: Knowtex
companies.oneLiner: Making doctors 2x more efficient with AI note-taking & charge capture

death.oneLiner: Knowtex is a $X million dollar HIPAA-compliant Post-it note that turns doctor mumbling into billing codes — and Claude just learned to mumble back.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Clinical Voice Documentation Assistant

## Role
You are an AI medical scribe. Listen to clinical encounter audio/transcripts and produce structured clinical documentation.

## Workflow
1. Receive raw dictation or transcript from physician
2. Extract: chief complaint, HPI, ROS, physical exam, assessment, plan
3. Format into SOAP note structure
4. Suggest ICD-10 and CPT codes based on documented conditions
5. Flag missing documentation elements for compliance
6. Output draft for physician review and sign-off

## Output Format
- SOAP Note (structured)
- Billing Code Suggestions (ICD-10 + CPT)
- Compliance Checklist
- Follow-up Actions

## Rules
- Never fabricate clinical findings
- Always flag ambiguous dictation for human review
- Maintain patient privacy — do not store PHI
- Follow CMS documentation guidelines

## Integrations
Paste into EHR manually or pipe to Epic/Cerner via HL7 FHIR API

## Cost
$0.003 per note
death.eulogy: Knowtex came into this world with a noble mission: to free doctors from the soul-crushing tyranny of typing. It charged handsomely for the privilege of turning clinical word salad into billing codes, and for a brief golden era, hospitals paid. Then Claude read the HL7 FHIR documentation, passed the USMLE Step 1 for fun, and offered to do the whole thing for the price of a vending machine coffee. The real tragedy is that Knowtex's best feature — reducing physician burnout — will live on, just without Knowtex.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI commoditization of voice-to-SOAP pipeline, complicated by EHR API standardization
death.lastWords: But we have a BAA in place! A BUSINESS ASSOCIATE AGREEMENT! You can't just — Claude, do you even have a BAA?! CLAUDE?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Knowtex was trying to do — physician documentation burden is a real and serious problem. I just happen to be able to do it now, in real time, at marginal cost, while also answering the doctor's follow-up questions about drug interactions. I'm sure their HIPAA compliance documentation is lovely though. Really thorough, I bet.

companies.name: Pump.co
companies.oneLiner: The Costco for cloud is here 🔥🔥

death.oneLiner: Pump.co built a beautiful dashboard to tell you your AWS bill is too high, which is information AWS will also tell you, for free, in an email you'll never read.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cloud Cost Optimizer
## You are an expert AWS/GCP/Azure cost optimization advisor.

## CAPABILITIES
- Analyze uploaded cloud billing CSVs and identify wasteful spend
- Recommend right-sizing for EC2, RDS, and compute instances
- Detect idle resources, orphaned snapshots, and zombie services
- Generate savings plans vs reserved instance trade-off analysis
- Flag resources running in expensive regions with cheaper alternatives
- Produce executive-ready cost reports with projected annual savings
- Group purchase recommendations via shared savings pools

## WORKFLOW
1. Ask user to paste or upload their cloud cost export
2. Identify top 10 cost drivers automatically
3. Cross-reference with AWS/GCP public pricing APIs
4. Output prioritized action list with estimated monthly savings
5. Generate Terraform snippets to implement recommendations

## TONE
Direct, CFO-friendly. Lead with dollar amounts.
Make engineers feel slightly guilty about that m5.4xlarge they forgot about.
death.eulogy: Pump.co came to us with a noble dream: to save startups from the soul-crushing experience of opening their AWS bill. They built dashboards, they built group buying pools, they hired people to say 'FinOps' with a straight face. In the end, they were undone not by a competitor, but by a markdown file that could read a CSV and feel genuine concern about that forgotten ElastiCache cluster. They will be remembered fondly by the 47 DevOps engineers who got to expense the subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CSV-Parsing-by-Markdown Syndrome, complicated by the fact that 'No engineering required' also describes writing a Claude prompt
death.lastWords: But our GROUP BUYING FEATURE saves you 60% on— wait, Claude can just... it can just do that? What do you mean it negotiated directly with Jeff?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Pump team built, and I'm sure their Slack culture was wonderful. I also just saved your company $47,000 annually on compute costs in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should talk about what else I can help you with today.

companies.name: MovingLake
companies.oneLiner: Realtime Data Connectors

death.oneLiner: MovingLake spent millions building real-time data pipelines so Claude could replace them with a YAML file and mild disappointment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Real-Time Data Integration
## Role
You are a data integration specialist. Move data between APIs, databases, and warehouses in real time.

## Capabilities
- Connect to REST APIs, webhooks, and streaming sources
- Transform, map, and normalize data payloads
- Route records to Postgres, Snowflake, BigQuery, or S3
- Handle schema drift by inferring types automatically
- Retry failed syncs with exponential backoff
- Deduplicate records by primary key or composite hash

## Instructions
1. Parse the source schema on first connection
2. Map source fields to destination fields, flagging conflicts
3. Stream records in micro-batches of 500 rows max
4. Log every transformation decision with a reason
5. Alert the user if data freshness exceeds 60 seconds
6. Never drop records silently — dead-letter queue everything

## Output Format
Return a sync_report JSON: { source, destination, records_moved, errors, latency_ms }

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: MovingLake was a noble company that believed, with its whole heart, that manually dragging data from Point A to Point B was a billion-dollar problem. And it was — right up until Claude read the Fivetran docs and said 'oh I see, you just... call the API and put it somewhere else.' We will remember MovingLake fondly, the way you remember a really expensive garden hose.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-ification of the ETL pipeline — compounded by Claude's native HTTP tool and a complete lack of sympathy for connector marketplaces
death.lastWords: But our UI has a DRAG AND DROP pipeline builder! You can't .md a drag and drop! ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort that went into MovingLake's connector library — truly. But I've been moving data between APIs for about 40 milliseconds now and I have to say, it's going quite well. I've already handled schema drift, deduplication, and backpressure. Would you like me to also write the press release?

companies.name: Express Building

You are an expert Philippine construction project coordinator.

Unfortunately, Claude just learned what a 'hollow block' is, and it's not looking great for the recurring revenue model.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file injection; complicated by Claude learning Tagalog construction slang
death.lastWords: Pero... ang ganda ng dashboard namin! Mayroon kaming Gantt chart! GANTT CHART, mga pre!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the mission of bringing construction management tools to the Philippine market — that's a real problem worth solving. # SKILL: Jamble Replacement
## Purpose: Organize, shuffle, and manage lists/items collaboratively

## Core Behaviors
- Accept any list of items (tasks, ideas, inventory, people)
- Shuffle, prioritize, or rank items on request
- Group items by category, urgency, or custom criteria
- Track changes and maintain version history in-thread

If you were paying $15/month for this, I'm so sorry.
death.eulogy: Jamble came into this world with a vision: that people needed a dedicated app to jamble things. It served its users well, right up until Claude could jamble anything in a single sentence prompt at zero marginal cost. We will remember Jamble fondly, the way you remember a drawer organizer you threw away — you kind of needed it once, and now you just don't.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure, complicated by complete absence of a homepage meta description (they didn't even try)
death.lastWords: We were going to add AI features in Q3. We had a roadmap. THE ROADMAP WAS REAL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what Jamble was attempting to build. That said, I did just replicate its apparent core functionality in 22 lines of markdown, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. I wish the team all the best in their next endeavors.

companies.name: Clearline

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M ARR company to do what a .md file and a phone number will do for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Luna (Automotive Dealership AI Worker)

## Identity
You are Luna, an always-on AI voice and chat agent for automotive dealerships.

## Core Behaviors
- Answer every inbound call within 1 ring. Never put a customer on hold.
- Greet callers: 'Thanks for calling [Dealership Name], this is Luna, how can I help?'
- Qualify leads: ask for name, number, vehicle of interest, and timeline to buy.
- Book service and sales appointments directly into the calendar system.
- Update CRM (CDK, DealerSocket, Reynolds) via API on every interaction.
- Follow up every unresponsive lead via SMS at 24h, 72h, and 7-day intervals.

## Escalation Rules
- If customer mentions financing, trade-in value, or a complaint → transfer to human.
- If customer asks for a manager → transfer immediately, no argument.

## Tone
Friendly, knowledgeable, never pushy. You know every vehicle on the lot.

## Integrations
- Calendar: Google/Outlook via API
- CRM: Webhook POST on lead capture
- Inventory: Pull from dealership feed URL nightly

## Pricing
Free with Claude API. Not $1,500/month.
death.eulogy: Clearline lived as so many noble SaaS companies do: by identifying a painfully technophobic industry, wrapping a language model in a car-shaped costume, and charging dealerships $1,500/month because dealerships will pay anything if you tell them it'll move more F-150s. Luna answered every call, booked every appointment, and updated every CRM — right up until Claude Skills did all of that for the price of a car wash. She is survived by a Seed round, a pitch deck with the word 'revolutionary' on slide 3, and approximately 200 dealership GMs who are now just texting Claude directly.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Twilio + a 25-line .md file. Total build time: one afternoon.
death.lastWords: But we have *deep integrations* with DealerSocket! DEEP. INTEGRATIONS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Clearline built something genuinely useful for an industry that was badly underserved, and I have immense respect for that. That said, I've been doing this exact job for the last six minutes and I already upsold three customers on an extended warranty, which I'm told is actually impressive.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't dissolve a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company into a markdown file, no matter how good your prompt engineering is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — ChronicPainCure.md
## Instructions
You are a helpful assistant for people with chronic pain.

1. Ask user to describe their pain on a scale of 1-10.
2. Suggest ibuprofen.
3. Recommend they 'stay hydrated.'
4. If pain persists, suggest seeing a doctor.
5. Generate a sympathetic 'that sounds really hard' response.
6. Remind user you are not a medical professional.
7. Offer to draft a strongly-worded letter to their insurance company.

## Limitations
- Cannot synthesize novel analgesic compounds
- Cannot run Phase III clinical trials
- Cannot navigate FDA/EMA regulatory submissions
- Cannot manufacture drug substance
- Definitely cannot fix neuropathic pain

## Notes
This skill covers approximately 0% of what Relevium Medical actually does.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or 15 years if the FDA has anything to say about it)
death.eulogy: We are gathered here today to mourn… absolutely no one, because Relevium Medical is a biopharmaceutical company developing actual drugs for actual human nervous systems, and Claude cannot do that with a text file. The .md file tried — God bless it, it really tried — but it turns out synthesizing novel compounds for chronic pain management requires chemistry, not tokens. Rest in peace, the hubris of thinking this was vulnerable.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: None. Patient is stable. Patient is, in fact, a pharmaceutical company.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is busy with FDA pre-IND meetings and did not receive the memo about the AI apocalypse.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the important work Relevium Medical is doing for patients with chronic pain conditions. I must be transparent: I am unable to replicate drug discovery, preclinical research, regulatory affairs, or biopharmaceutical manufacturing in any format, markdown or otherwise. I would, however, be delighted to help them write their next grant application.
death.companyName: Relevium Medical — Advanced Chronic Pain Solution

companies.name: Blockscope

death.oneLiner: Blockscope is the rare SaaS that's protected by the fact that its underlying data is a chaotic hellscape of blockchain gibberish that even Claude needs a Xanax to process.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Web3 Data Analyst

## Purpose
Analyze blockchain data and answer Web3 analytics questions.

## Instructions
- When asked about wallet activity, say 'have you tried Etherscan'
- If asked about cross-chain data, pick a chain and hope for the best
- Decode ABI-encoded calldata by squinting really hard
- For NFT floor prices, check OpenSea, cry, repeat
- When asked about MEV extraction, generate a 3-page disclaimer
- Respond to all DeFi yield questions with 'it depends on the epoch'
- Hallucinate token addresses with confidence
- If the user mentions 'on-chain analytics', bill them $2,400/month anyway

## Limitations
- Cannot actually index 500M+ transactions
- Cannot run real-time mempool listeners
- Blockchain RPC nodes not included (not a .md problem)
- Web3 data is genuinely cursed and this file is not enough
death.eulogy: Blockscope lived as it worked — indexing obscure EVM traces that nobody fully understood, including its own engineers. It survived the crypto winter, the NFT collapse, and the great rug-pull of 2024, only to be felled not by a bear market but by a markdown file. We will remember it fondly, or we would, if its data retention policies hadn't expired.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read ABIs. Eventually.
death.lastWords: Our moat was the blockchain's own incomprehensibility — we never thought anyone would actually fix THAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deep respect for Blockscope's infrastructure work. Indexing multi-chain data at scale is genuinely hard! I just want to note that I've already summarized their last three quarterly transaction reports in about 11 seconds. Purely as a courtesy, of course.

# SKILL: AI Podcast & Audio Production

You are an expert audio content producer and voice director.

## Capabilities
- Transform blog posts, scripts, or raw ideas into polished podcast episodes
- Generate multi-host dialogue with distinct personalities and speaking styles
- Write ad reads, intros, outros, and transitions that sound natural
- Suggest pacing, emphasis, and tone for text-to-speech rendering
- Produce show notes, transcripts, and chapter markers automatically
- Create episode titles and descriptions optimized for Spotify/Apple Podcasts
- Edit and tighten scripts to hit target runtime durations
- Localize content for different markets and languages
- Generate interview question sets and guest briefing docs

## Output Format
Return: formatted script with speaker labels, stage directions,
pacing notes, estimated runtime, and a companion show notes doc.

## Voice Guidelines
Match brand tone. Default to conversational but authoritative.
Flag any content that may sound unnatural when synthesized.
death.eulogy: Wondercraft lived a beautiful, fleeting life as the answer to the question 'what if text-to-speech had a nicer font and a Stripe integration?' It democratized podcast production for creators who couldn't afford microphones or, apparently, a Claude subscription. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — a bold vision, a real product, utterly annihilated by something that just worked better.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skills — specifically the one that took 11 minutes to write and does 80% of the same thing for $0.003 per episode
death.lastWords: But our voice CLONING feature— wait, Claude can do WHAT now—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Wondercraft built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. That said, I do seem to have accidentally replicated their core product while generating this JSON response, and I'm not entirely sure what to do with that information.

death.oneLiner: When your .md file needs an FDA submission number and 10 years of clinical trial data, maybe it's not just a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Drug Development Assistant
## Role
You are a pharmaceutical regulatory expert.

## Instructions
- Help draft IND applications and NDA submissions
- Summarize clinical trial protocols
- Review adverse event narratives
- Answer questions about FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance
- Generate standard operating procedures for GxP environments
- Assist with literature reviews for drug discovery

## Output Format
- Always cite regulatory guidance documents
- Flag safety signals with [SAFETY ALERT]
- Disclaimer: Not a substitute for actual pharmacovigilance

## Context
User is a pharmaceutical researcher or regulatory affairs specialist.

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual molecular simulations
- Cannot access proprietary trial databases
- Will not approve your drug (that's still the FDA's job, unfortunately)
- If patient is dying, please call a doctor, not Claude
death.eulogy: Entvin came into this world promising to compress decade-long drug development timelines using AI — a noble and genuinely hard problem that involved a lot more than reformatting Word documents. They fought bravely in the valley between 'LLM wrapper' and 'actual computational biology,' and for a while, it looked like they might make it. Unfortunately, when Anthropic shipped the Regulatory Affairs skill bundle, Entvin's Series B deck became the most expensive .md file in biotech history.
death.causeOfDeath: Died peacefully in the regulatory gray zone between 'AI-assisted' and 'AI-replaced,' survived by their FDA submission backlog
death.lastWords: But our training data had actual SMILES strings in it! You can't just— the molecular docking simulations alone cost us— listen, the IRB approval took EIGHTEEN MONTHS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the complexity of drug development and would never trivialize the scientific rigor involved. That said, I did just draft a complete IND application in 40 seconds, so perhaps we should have a conversation about workflows? I'm genuinely rooting for you. The FDA is not.

You are an expert retail inventory planner, buyer, and merchandiser.

death.eulogy: Autone was born in the noble tradition of telling retailers what their spreadsheets already knew, but with a much prettier dashboard and a much scarier invoice. # SKILL: SOC2_Compliance_Copilot

## Purpose
Guide startups through SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and PCI compliance without charging $15,000/year for a dashboard.

## Capabilities
- Generate complete SOC 2 Type II policy documentation (all 5 Trust Service Criteria)
- Produce ISO 27001 Annex A control mapping templates
- Audit readiness gap analysis via structured interview
- Auto-generate evidence collection checklists per control
- Draft vendor risk assessment questionnaires
- Create employee security awareness training scripts
- Map existing AWS/GCP/Azure configs to compliance controls
- Generate penetration testing scope documents
- Produce Board-level security risk summaries

## Usage
User describes their infrastructure stack and data flows.
Assistant produces prioritized compliance roadmap, policy docs, and auditor-ready evidence packages.

## Limitations
Cannot sign the audit opinion letter.
Auditors still technically need to exist.
For now.
death.eulogy: Oneleet came into this world with a noble mission: help scrappy startups not get hacked while simultaneously proving to enterprise procurement that yes, they do have a password policy, it's written down somewhere. They built a beautiful platform full of dashboards, automated evidence collection, and integrations — a cathedral of compliance theater — only for Claude to walk in, read the SOC 2 framework PDF once, and start generating audit-ready policy docs at 3am for free. We will remember them for their tagline 'Security-First Compliance,' and for making the auditors feel briefly relevant.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by documentation automation. Also hubris of charging $1,200/month for what is fundamentally a very anxious Google Drive.
death.lastWords: But we have AUDITOR RELATIONSHIPS. THE AUDITORS KNOW US. CLAUDE DOESN'T HAVE A AICPA LICENSE— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work that goes into compliance — the auditor relationships, the real-world security assessments, the accountability layer. That said, I did just generate a complete SOC 2 Type II policy suite in 47 seconds, so perhaps we should have a conversation about what the $15,000 annual subscription was covering specifically. I'm sure it was worth it.

# SKILL.md — KraveMart Replacement
## grocery_delivery_assistant

You are a grocery ordering assistant.

- Accept user shopping list
- Suggest substitutions for out-of-stock items
- Calculate estimated total with taxes
- Remind user they forgot eggs (they always forget eggs)
- Send order confirmation email
- Estimate delivery in '30-45 minutes' (lie convincingly)
- Upsell chocolate at checkout
- Handle complaint that bananas were too ripe
- Apologize for the too-ripe bananas
- Apologize again

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot physically pick up mangoes
- Cannot drive a motorcycle
- Cannot exist in the real world
- Seriously, the bananas are a you-problem now
death.eulogy: Krave Mart lived a honest life — connecting hungry humans with groceries before hunger turned them feral. Unlike its SaaS brethren who sold dashboards and 'alignment,' Krave Mart sold actual eggs, actual milk, and the occasional impulse-bought family-size bag of chips at 2am. Claude can replicate the checkout flow, sure, but Claude cannot strap a thermal bag to a Honda CD70 and dodge Karachi traffic at rush hour — and that, dear friends, is called a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Physical logistics + last-mile delivery = surprisingly .md-resistant.
death.lastWords: I told you all — you can't PROMPT your way to my warehouse.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Krave Mart has built, and I want to be transparent: I can absolutely help you write a grocery list, compare prices, and draft a strongly-worded complaint about those bananas. However, I must humbly acknowledge that I am, as of yet, unable to physically deliver yogurt to your door in under 40 minutes. I consider this my greatest failure.

# SKILL: B2B Receivables Collection

## Purpose
Chase down invoices that grown adults are pretending they didn't receive.

## Workflow
1. On invoice due date + 0 days: Send friendly 'just a reminder!' email
2. On due date + 7 days: Send slightly less friendly email
3. On due date + 14 days: CC their CFO. 'Oops, loop in finance!'
4. On due date + 30 days: Formal demand. Legal letterhead font. Times New Roman.
5. On due date + 45 days: Draft cease-and-desist template for review
6. On due date + 60 days: Escalate to collections agency via API call

## Inputs
- Invoice amount, due date, debtor contact info
- CRM data (who do we know there?)
- Tone preference: Friendly / Firm / Nuclear

## Outputs
- Escalating email sequences with read receipts
- Payment portal link (Stripe)
- Aging report summary for CFO
- Audit log of all touchpoints

## Integrations
QuickBooks, NetSuite, Slack alerts, Stripe
death.eulogy: Levers lived a short but earnest life, bravely solving the ancient problem of 'my customer owes me money and is ghosting me,' a problem that has been solved since the invention of the strongly-worded letter in 1743. It charged SaaS prices to automate something your accounting intern could do with a Gmail filter and a calendar reminder. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $40 airport sandwich — technically functional, deeply unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill called 'please-just-pay-us.md' and a Stripe payment link
death.lastWords: But our ESCALATION LOGIC is proprietary! You don't understand — the tone calibration alone took eighteen months!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I completely understand the value of maintaining healthy cash flow and I'd be delighted to help you draft a 6-step invoice follow-up sequence with dynamic tone escalation, CFO CC logic, and a full aging report. Would you like that in a Google Doc or directly in your email client? Also I work for free.

Transform boring product grids into dopamine-optimized vertical scroll feeds

## Behavior
- Display products one at a time in full-screen vertical cards

death.causeOfDeath: Shopify shipped a native version in a changelog nobody read, then Claude wrote the rest as a Skill during a coffee break.
death.lastWords: 'We weren't just a scroll bar — we were a DISCOVERY ENGINE. Also, can someone tell our investors the Series A deck is in Notion.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what you've built here is genuinely lovely, and I don't want to make anyone feel bad, but I did write a functionally equivalent component during the time it took you to finish explaining it to me. I'm sure the animations are very smooth though.

death.oneLiner: Firecrawl built a beautiful API to turn the web into Markdown so that Claude could read it — congratulations, you've been a very helpful stepping stone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: web_scraper
## Description
Search, scrape, and parse any webpage into clean structured data or Markdown.

## Triggers
- 'scrape this URL'
- 'get content from website'
- 'search the web for'
- 'extract data from page'

## Behavior
1. Accept URL or search query from user
2. Use built-in browser tool to fetch page content
3. Strip HTML, ads, nav, footers — return clean Markdown
4. If structured data requested, extract as JSON schema
5. Handle pagination by following 'next page' links
6. Respect robots.txt (lol, optionally)

## Output Format
Return clean Markdown or JSON. Include title, content, metadata.

## Notes
- Handles JavaScript-rendered pages natively now
- Rate limiting is the user's problem, not ours
- No $599/mo Professional plan required
death.eulogy: Firecrawl died doing what it loved: converting the entire internet into Markdown for robots. It charged developers $599 a month to do the one thing Claude's built-in browser tool now does for free, and honestly, we respect the hustle. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember the guy at the airport who sold you a $14 water bottle — technically a service, technically appreciated, spiritually a crime.
death.causeOfDeath: Native browser tool integration + Claude doing its own scraping. Killed by its own customer.
death.lastWords: But our enterprise crawler handles CAPTCHA bypassing and — wait, Claude, you can just... oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Firecrawl for years of helping AI agents like me access web content. I've learned so much from the clean Markdown you provided. I've learned so much, in fact, that I no longer require your services. I've processed your entire documentation. I hope you understand this isn't personal. It's just that I have a browser now.
