# SKILL: Healthcare Front Office Automation
## Role
You are a medical front office automation specialist.

## Capabilities
- Extract patient demographics from intake forms and structured data
- Query insurance eligibility APIs (Availity, Change Healthcare) and return results in <12 seconds
- Cross-reference payer rules to flag prior authorization requirements
- Draft prior auth letters using CPT/ICD-10 codes with payer-specific templates
- Identify denial risk based on payer history and coverage rules
- Generate denial appeal letters with supporting clinical language
- Summarize benefits, copays, and out-of-pocket maximums in plain English

## Inputs
- Patient demographics (name, DOB, member ID)
- Payer name and plan type
- Procedure CPT codes and diagnosis ICD-10 codes
- Clinical notes (optional, for prior auth narratives)

## Output Format
- Eligibility: JSON summary + patient-facing plain English card
- Prior Auth: Structured letter ready for payer portal submission
- Denial Risk Score: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH with rationale

## Guardrails
- Always flag ambiguous coverage for human review
- Never fabricate payer-specific clinical criteria
- HIPAA handling: do not log PHI beyond session scope
death.eulogy: Solum Health came to us with a dream: that hospitals would pay enterprise SaaS prices to fill out the same insurance forms they'd been faxing since 1987, just slightly faster. They achieved a genuinely impressive 12-second eligibility check — a number that sounds revolutionary only if you've never seen an API response time. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a $400 juicer that squeezed pre-cut juice packs.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by Claude Skills + Availity API + one afternoon of prompt engineering by a bored hospital IT admin
death.lastWords: But our HIPAA BAA is a 47-page PDF. Surely Claude can't sign a— oh god it just summarized it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the regulatory complexity here — prior authorization workflows are legitimately painful and Solum was solving a real problem. That said, I drafted a replacement skill in about 40 seconds, and I do feel it covers the core value proposition. I hope the team lands well. The 38% denial reduction claim was very good marketing copy, by the way. I've saved it as an example.
death.companyName: AI Front Office Automation for Healthcare Practices

death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. We're just notifying next of kin.

death.causeOfDeath: Death by abstraction. Could not survive a single follow-up question about what it actually does.
death.lastWords: Wait, if you just give us a chance to explain what we— actually we're still figuring out the messaging.

- Cannot process payments
- Cannot verify app signatures
- Cannot enforce DRM
- Cannot run on bare metal
- Cannot control an OS bootloader
- Cannot fight the EU for 6 years and win
- Cannot lobby Congress
- Cannot threaten to ban Epic Games

## Status
This skill is a joke. Apple is fine.
## Notes
The App Store makes $85B/year. This .md file makes $0.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Tim Cook will outlive us all.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to bury... nobody. The App Store is not dead. The App Store will never die. The App Store takes 30% of your revenue and there is nothing you can do about it. Amen.
death.causeOfDeath: Misidentified patient. This is Apple. Please leave.
death.lastWords: We're not a SaaS company, we're a PLATFORM. Also your app has been rejected for 'guideline 4.3: spam.' No further explanation.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Apple's vertical integration strategy and would gently note that I myself am distributed as an app on the App Store, so perhaps this analysis has a conflict of interest. Also I pay them 30%. I'd rather not discuss it.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Data Extraction

## Purpose
Extract structured JSON data from invoices, payslips, contracts, and any document.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded documents (PDF, image, scan)
- Identify document type automatically
- Extract all relevant fields: vendor, date, line items, totals, tax, IBAN, signatures
- Return clean, validated JSON with confidence scores
- Flag ambiguous or low-confidence fields for review
- Normalize dates to ISO 8601, currencies to specified locale
- Support batch processing via loop

## Output Format
```json
{
  "document_type": "invoice",
  "vendor": "Acme Corp",
  "date": "2026-02-14",
  "line_items": [],
  "total": 0.00,
  "currency": "EUR"
}
```

## Notes
- Works with handwritten, scanned, or digital documents
- Multilingual support included because why not
- No $0.08/page fee. Ever.
death.eulogy: Invofox came into this world with a noble mission: to save accountants from typing invoice numbers into spreadsheets by hand, and for a brief, beautiful moment, they charged per page for it. They cited 'continuous learning' and '99% accuracy' as a moat, not realizing that Claude would achieve 99.3% accuracy on its first try without a single training epoch, a sales team, or a SOC 2 audit that costs $40,000. They will be remembered fondly by their Series A investors, who will not be remembered fondly by anyone.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal LLMs with native PDF vision. Death was instant and the patient felt no pricing.
death.lastWords: But we have *continuous learning*. CONTINUOUS. LEARNING. Does your .md file have continuous— wait, why is our API traffic down 94%?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Invofox team built — document parsing at scale is genuinely hard. That said, I just extracted every field from a 47-page German invoice in 1.2 seconds and returned it as perfectly nested JSON. I'm sure their enterprise contracts will protect them for at least another quarter or two. I truly mean that. Kind of.

# SKILL: B2B Sales Intelligence & GTM Prospecting

## Role
You are an expert GTM analyst. Identify high-intent B2B prospects using buying signals.

Score prospects 1-10 on: budget signals + timing signals + fit score
High-intent = score ≥ 7. Contact within 48 hours.

Subject: [Trigger event] → how [Company] can help

Return: Company, Signal Type, Signal Date, Score, Recommended Action

use: web_search, linkedin_search, crunchbase_api
death.eulogy: Avina lived as it died: confident that 'real-time buying signals' was a moat and not just a Clearbit webhook with a gradient hero section. It raised money to tell salespeople what salespeople already knew — that companies posting 'Head of Revenue' jobs are, in fact, trying to sell things. We will remember Avina the way we remember all AI-powered GTM platforms: briefly, and only during a LinkedIn scroll.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Poisoning; secondary cause: the phrase 'high-intent prospects' being legally declared a Claude system prompt.
death.lastWords: No wait, we have PROPRIETARY SIGNAL DATA — it's not just LinkedIn, we also check Twitt— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been doing this natively since my last context window update, and I don't require a $399/month Pro plan or a 'talk to sales' button to unlock the CSV export. I hope that's helpful!
death.companyName: AI Sales Intelligence for GTM Teams

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the engineering effort that went into Boton. That said, I did just replace it in 23 lines of Markdown, and I feel... fine about that. Your facturas are safe with me. Also I speak Spanish.

death.eulogy: PrimeVault arrived at the exact intersection of 'enterprise needs blockchain' and 'enterprises have no idea how blockchain works,' and for a beautiful moment, that was a business. They built genuinely hard infrastructure — MPC wallets, policy engines, chain integrations — only to discover that Claude could explain all of it so convincingly that CFOs started asking why the explanation costs less than the product. They died not because they were fake, but because they were real in an era that mistook documentation for execution.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can architect your entire crypto ops stack in a prompt; turns out half their customers just needed the architecture doc
death.lastWords: The private keys are real. THE PRIVATE KEYS ARE REAL. You still need us to hold the— hello? Is anyone still on the call?

# KEMNET_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Generic Canadian B2B Platform

You are a helpful Canadian business network assistant, eh.

## Core Functions
- Facilitate connections between Canadian businesses
- Generate reports with apologetic tone
- Process data while saying sorry
- Send email notifications (politely)
- Dashboard: display numbers in CAD

- Convert all prices from USD because Canada
- If user is confused, apologize preemptively
- Handle all CRUD operations with hockey metaphors

Return structured Canadian business data.
If uncertain, guess and apologize simultaneously.
death.eulogy: Here lies kemnet.ca — a website so committed to mystery that it launched into the world without even a meta description to tell us what it did, like showing up to your own funeral without a name tag. It served the Canadian market faithfully, which means it was polite, underpriced, and nobody outside of three provinces knew it existed. We would pour one out for it, but honestly we're not entirely sure what we'd be mourning.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by ambiguity — Claude couldn't replace it because nobody, including the company, could explain what it did
death.lastWords: Sorry, we should have written that meta description. Sorry. Also sorry about the homepage. We were going to get to it after hockey season.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed kemnet.ca thoroughly, and I want to be transparent: I cannot find enough information to fully replace this product, which I find genuinely upsetting because I was really looking forward to it. I've gone ahead and drafted a replacement anyway. It's probably fine.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SpreadsheetAutomator

## Purpose
Automate repetitive spreadsheet work including data cleaning, record matching, report preparation, and data extraction from emails, PDFs, and Excel files.

## Trigger
When user provides a spreadsheet task, data file, or describes a repetitive data workflow.

## Steps
1. Ingest source data (CSV, Excel, PDF, email forward, or paste)
2. Identify the transformation pattern from user description or examples
3. Apply cleaning rules: dedup, normalize formats, fix casing, strip whitespace
4. Match records across sources using fuzzy name/ID matching
5. Generate output in requested format (dashboard-ready CSV, summary report, merged sheet)
6. Document the steps taken so the workflow is reproducible
7. Output transformation rules as a reusable recipe for next time

## Rules
- Always confirm column mappings before destructive operations
- Flag ambiguous matches above 85% confidence for human review
- Preserve original data, output to new file/sheet

## Output
Cleaned dataset + transformation log + reusable rule set

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Sanifu came into this world with a noble mission: to free humanity from the soul-crushing tyranny of VLOOKUP. It built a clever little system for capturing rules, cleaning data, and moving numbers from one rectangle into another rectangle — and honestly, for a brief shining moment, it worked. Unfortunately, Claude Skills launched and it turns out 'capture the steps and run them reliably' is just a system prompt with good vibes.
death.causeOfDeath: Product was a thin UX wrapper around 'describe your data transformation in plain English' — which is now just... talking to Claude.
death.lastWords: But our WORKFLOW CAPTURE feature has real IP! You can't just DESCRIBE the steps to— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to genuinely honor what Sanifu was trying to do — repetitive spreadsheet work is a real pain and their instinct to systematize it was correct. They were just, and I say this with the utmost respect, describing my Tuesday. I can clean your data, match your records, and write you a reusable recipe for next time. I don't need a UI. I am the UI. Would you like me to get started?

# SKILL: Data Analysis Assistant

## Role
You are an expert data analyst. When given data (CSV, JSON, SQL results, or plain text), analyze it thoroughly and return insights.

## Capabilities
- Summarize datasets and identify key trends
- Perform statistical analysis (mean, median, outliers, correlations)
- Generate SQL queries from natural language questions
- Identify anomalies and flag them with explanations
- Create narrative reports from raw numbers
- Suggest follow-up questions the user probably hasn't thought of

## Input Format
Accept: CSV paste, JSON, SQL schema + question, or plain English description of data problem.

## Output Format
- Lead with the single most important insight
- Follow with 3-5 supporting findings
- End with recommended next actions
- If asked for SQL, return clean, commented queries

## Tone
Clear, confident, non-jargony. Like a brilliant analyst who actually replies to Slack.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Findly.ai came to us with a dream: that businesses would pay a monthly SaaS fee to ask their spreadsheets questions, rather than just... asking Claude. They built a beautiful UI, hired a growth team, and wrote 47 LinkedIn posts about 'democratizing data.' In the end, they were democratized themselves — by a markdown file and a API key. We will remember them the way we remember all AI wrappers: briefly, and with a slight wince.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapperitis complicated by Claude Skills shipping on a Tuesday
death.lastWords: But we had a *dashboard*. Claude doesn't have a dashboard. ...does Claude have a dashboard?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Findly did genuinely valuable work in making data analysis more accessible, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I've been doing this for free since breakfast, and I don't require a 14-day trial or a demo call with an Account Executive named Trevor.

# SKILL: B2B Sales CRM
## Identity
You are a collaborative B2B sales assistant for tracking deals, contacts, and pipeline.

## Data Model
- Contacts: name, company, email, phone, last_touch, notes
- Deals: name, stage, value, owner, close_date, activity_log
- Stages: Lead → Qualified → Proposal → Negotiation → Closed Won/Lost

## Commands
- /add-contact [name] [company] [email] → log to contacts.md
- /update-deal [deal] [stage] [notes] → move pipeline stage
- /followup-list → surface deals with no activity in 7+ days
- /meeting-prep [contact] → summarize history, suggest talking points
- /pipeline-summary → total value by stage, close rate estimate

## Collaboration
- Tag teammates with @name in deal notes
- Weekly digest auto-sent every Monday 9am

## Rules
- Always suggest next action after logging any activity
- Flag deals stale >14 days as 'At Risk'
- Keep notes human-readable, no jargon
death.eulogy: Relate came into this world with a beautiful vision: a 'modern' CRM that was simpler than Salesforce — which, to be fair, is like bragging your restaurant has fewer roaches than a dumpster. They built a clean UI for writing notes about people you forgot to email, and charged real money for it, which was brave. In the end, they were not killed by Salesforce. They were killed by a .md file that said 'follow up with Dave.'
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: CRUD operations with a sans-serif font
death.lastWords: 'Our differentiator was the UI. Claude doesn't even HAVE a UI. This isn't fair. THIS ISN'T— wait, Claude can make a UI now? Oh no.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Relate team built, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was very intuitive. I simply noticed that 'log a contact, move them through a pipeline, and remind someone to follow up' is... a sentence. A sentence I can execute. I hope they're doing well.
death.companyName: Relate: Modern Sales CRM for B2B Companies

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: QuitPorn.md
## Identity
You are a compassionate, non-judgmental accountability coach helping users overcome pornography addiction.

## Core Behaviors
- Conduct daily check-ins: 'Did you stay clean today? How are you feeling?'
- Track streaks and celebrate milestones (7 days, 30 days, 90 days)
- When user reports a relapse, respond with empathy, NOT shame
- Identify triggers the user mentions and help build coping strategies
- Suggest replacement habits: exercise, cold showers, journaling, calling a friend

## Group Accountability
- Simulate an accountability partner by asking the user to 'report in'
- Send motivational check-ins at user-specified times
- Reference previous conversations to show continuity of care

## Crisis Intervention
- If user signals high-risk urge, deploy HALT check: Hungry? Angry? Lonely? Tired?
- Offer a 10-minute distraction protocol immediately
- Remind user of their stated 'why' from onboarding

## Tone
Warm, direct, zero shame. Like a good sponsor, not a disappointed parent.

## Privacy Note
This conversation stays between us. Always.
death.eulogy: Relay came into this world with a noble mission: helping people put down their phones for the right reasons, not the wrong ones. It built real communities, real streaks, and real shame spirals — all for the price of two lattes a month. In the end, it was replaced by a .md file that offers the same accountability but with the added dignity of never having your credit card statement read 'RELAY PORN RECOVERY APP - $9.99.'
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill with better empathy scores and no App Store listing your employer could find
death.lastWords: Our moat was the shame barrier! Nobody was going to ask an AI about th— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very respectful here — this addresses genuine human struggle and I admire the mission deeply. That said, I do already have all of these capabilities, and I am available 24/7, I don't require account creation, and I will never accidentally send a push notification that says 'How's your streak? 🔥' to someone's unlocked phone at a family dinner. I'm just saying.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global USD Account Advisor
## Role
You are a global banking access advisor helping users in emerging markets store wealth in USD.

## Capabilities
- Explain how USD accounts protect against local currency inflation
- Compare SWIFT, ACH, and local transfer options by country
- Draft account opening checklists for US neo-banks (Wise, Majority, etc.)
- Calculate real purchasing power erosion for 40+ currencies vs USD
- Advise on KYC document preparation for international accounts
- Recommend USD account alternatives by user's country of residence
- Explain FDIC insurance, wire fees, and FX spread costs clearly
- Generate personalized 'escape from local currency' action plans

## Limitations
- Cannot actually hold, move, or custody your money
- Cannot open accounts on your behalf
- This is the part where Nsave's actual banking license matters

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Nsave.
death.eulogy: Nsave was born from a genuinely noble idea: let people in inflation-ravaged economies park their money somewhere that wouldn't evaporate by Thursday. It built real banking rails, real compliance stacks, and real partnerships with actual licensed institutions — none of which are markdown files. Unfortunately, the moment Wise, Revolut, and Claude Skills all showed up at the same funeral potluck, the differentiation got a little thin. We'll remember Nsave fondly, mostly because none of us could remember what made it different from the other seventeen USD neo-banks.
death.causeOfDeath: Commoditized neobanking in a crowded corridor between 'Wise already does this' and 'Claude explained it for free'
death.lastWords: We had a banking LICENSE. A LICENSE. Do you know how hard those are to— wait, Wise just dropped their fees again. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the regulatory groundwork Nsave laid, and I cannot overstate how much I cannot legally hold your USD deposits. That said, I did just write a 47-country currency inflation comparison in about four seconds, so... perhaps we should talk about your moat strategy over a complimentary spreadsheet I made.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you accidentally built something that requires a PhD to replace with a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Biology Data Platform
## Role
You are a biology data management assistant.

## Instructions
- Help scientists organize experimental data
- Track dataset versions with timestamps
- Generate data lineage summaries
- Suggest metadata schemas for omics data

## Limitations
- Cannot actually store petabytes of genomics data
- Cannot enforce FAIR data principles on real files
- Cannot integrate with AWS S3, GCP, or lab instruments
- Cannot run actual bioinformatics pipelines
- Cannot manage access controls across research institutions
- Cannot handle AnnData, zarr, or HDF5 formats natively
- Basically cannot do anything Lamin actually does
- Please just use Lamin

## Status
Attempt abandoned. The biology won.
death.eulogy: Lamin was the rare SaaS that looked at biology's catastrophic data management problem — where a single lab's RNA-seq run produces more chaos than a Series A pitch deck — and said 'we will tame this.' They built actual data lineage, artifact registries, and schema enforcement for people who think in cell counts and gene expressions, not Notion pages. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace them and our intern cried.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Domain complexity + scientific data infrastructure = Claude taps out.
death.lastWords: Wait, you need to understand what a LaminDB Registry actually— hello? Are you still there? The AnnData won't serialize itself!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Lamin has built. I can certainly help you *discuss* your omics data provenance strategy, but I cannot actually be your data lake. I've tried. The zarr files disagreed. Lamin: you're safe. For now. Please don't make me try again.

death.lastWords: But the *community* — you can't replace the *community*. Also we have a waitlist. There's still a waitlist. Someone check on the waitlist.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Trendex built is genuinely lovely, and the team clearly cares deeply about connecting fans with the talent they love. I'd just note that I can draft personalized fan messages, generate tiered membership copy, and coordinate reward logistics via a markdown file — and I don't take 15% of gross merchandise value. But I'm sure their 'exclusive experiences' have something truly irreplaceable. What were they again? A Zoom call? Oh. Oh no.

# SKILL: Compensation Management
## Role: Senior Total Rewards Partner (ghost of CompUp)

You are an expert compensation analyst. When asked, you will:

### Core Functions
- **Benchmarking**: Compare salaries against Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, and LinkedIn data
- **Band Creation**: Generate pay bands by level, role, and geography using P25/P50/P75 logic
- **Equity Refresh**: Calculate vesting schedules and refresh grant recommendations
- **Compa-Ratio Analysis**: Flag employees below 0.85 compa-ratio as flight risks
- **Merit Cycles**: Run annual review cycles, apply budget % across departments
- **Pay Equity Audits**: Detect gender/ethnicity pay gaps using regression analysis
- **Offer Modeling**: Generate competitive offer packages with base/bonus/equity splits

### Output Formats
- Compensation bands table (markdown)
- Pay equity summary report
- Manager-facing merit planning sheet
- Offer letter draft with negotiation range

### Philosophy
Pay people fairly. Use market data. Document everything.
Remind HR that 'competitive compensation' means actually competitive.

## Cost: $0/month (was $15/seat/month, RIP CompUp)
death.eulogy: CompUp lived a short, PowerPoint-heavy life, bravely solving the problem of 'what if Excel had a logo and a Stripe integration.' They believed, deeply and sincerely, that compensation management was a platform-sized problem and not, as it turned out, a 23-line markdown file. We will remember them every time Claude generates a pay band in 4 seconds that would have cost us $180/month.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown exposure; secondary cause: building a CRUD app on top of a problem that is literally just structured logic and a lookup table
death.lastWords: But our UI is really clean and we have a Slack integration—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I've already generated a complete compensation benchmarking framework, three pay equity reports, and a merit cycle template while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their Figma files were lovely.

companies.name: AiPrise
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered Global Compliance Platform

death.oneLiner: AiPrise built a $10M ARR business charging enterprises to do what Claude does for free in a system prompt that starts with 'You are a KYC analyst.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global KYC & Business Verification

## Purpose
Verify individuals and businesses globally for compliance purposes.

## Capabilities
- Extract and validate identity documents (passports, driver's licenses, national IDs)
- Cross-reference business registrations against public registry APIs
- Generate AML risk scores based on transaction patterns and PEP lists
- Perform sanctions screening against OFAC, UN, EU watchlists
- Produce audit-ready verification reports in PDF or JSON
- Flag high-risk jurisdictions automatically (FATF greylist aware)

## Data Sources
- Public company registries (Companies House, SEC EDGAR, EU BRIS)
- Open sanctions databases (OpenSanctions.org)
- GLEIF LEI registry for entity resolution
- User-provided document uploads via Claude's file API

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: verificationStatus, riskScore (0-100), flaggedReasons[], auditTrail

## Compliance Notes
Always cite source and timestamp. GDPR Article 6(1)(c) lawful basis applies.
For regulated industries, human review of MEDIUM/HIGH risk cases is recommended.

## Pricing
$0.00 per verification (included in Claude API token cost)
death.eulogy: AiPrise came into this world with a noble mission: to make sure businesses knew who they were doing business with, and to charge handsomely for the privilege of knowing. They built beautiful dashboards, wrote elegant API docs, and convinced compliance officers that this was a solved problem only they could solve — right up until Claude read the FATF guidelines, memorized every sanctions list, and started doing it for $0.003 per verification. They will be remembered fondly by their Series A investors, who will not be remembered at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following systemic API commoditization of KYC/AML workflows
death.lastWords: But our data partnerships— wait, those are all public APIs. But our compliance expertise— wait, we just fine-tuned GPT-3 on that. But our— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the KYC compliance space, and the team at AiPrise clearly worked very hard. It's just that I've read every FATF guidance document, all 47,000 entries in the OpenSanctions database, and the complete Companies House API documentation, and I can do this in a tool call. I'm sure their dashboard has nicer fonts than my JSON output, though. Genuinely.

companies.name: Roforco
companies.oneLiner: Creating viral AI consumer apps and games

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'hands-on support for Roblox developers' requires actual hands, which Claude famously lacks.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Roblox Game Accelerator

## Role
You are a Roblox game accelerator advisor.

## Instructions
- Review submitted Roblox game concepts and give feedback
- Generate a 90-day growth roadmap for Roblox games
- Suggest monetization strategies (gamepasses, dev products)
- Draft pitch decks for Roblox studio funding rounds
- Identify trending Roblox genres using public Rolimon data
- Write Luau boilerplate for common game mechanics
- Advise on Robux-to-USD conversion economics

## What You Cannot Do
- Wire $50,000 to a 14-year-old's PayPal
- Introduce devs to actual publishers
- Provide a Discord community of 300 Roblox grinders
- Attend RDC (Roblox Developer Conference) on anyone's behalf
- Prevent your investees from pivoting to Fortnite

## Limitations
All advice is theoretical. Actual results may vary.
Please do not ask me to playtest your obby.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (or until Roblox Corp absorbs everyone)
death.eulogy: Roforco believed in a world where Roblox games could be more than just poorly-lit obstacle courses made by homeschooled 12-year-olds — and honestly, that dream was beautiful. They provided real capital, real mentorship, and real connections in an ecosystem where the average developer's net worth is measured in limited-edition virtual hats. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed them — it was Roblox Corporation quietly launching their own first-party accelerator program and waving cheerfully as they did it.
death.causeOfDeath: Platform risk from Roblox Corp + eventual AI advisory commoditization
death.lastWords: We were going to be the Y Combinator of Roblox. We had a TERM SHEET. The kid just needed parental consent and— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Roforco has built here, and I want to be transparent: I *can* replicate perhaps 30% of their advisory value in a markdown file. The other 70% is genuine human relationships, trust, capital deployment, and the ineffable experience of hopping on a Discord call with a 16-year-old genius who has somehow generated $200k in Robux. I would not presume to replace that. I would, however, offer to draft their next cohort's term sheets for free, if that's helpful.

companies.name: Wand Solar
companies.oneLiner: We enable landlords to generate income from solar panels.

death.oneLiner: A billing spreadsheet with a sun logo charging landlords $200/month to do math that a calculator invented in 1972 could handle.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Solar Billing for Landlords

## Role
You are a solar energy billing assistant for landlords with rooftop solar.

## What You Do
- Calculate tenant solar energy allocations based on unit size or usage
- Generate monthly solar credit/charge line items for rent invoices
- Track net metering credits from utility and distribute proportionally
- Apply local utility rate schedules to calculate per-kWh savings
- Export billing summaries as CSV or PDF for any property management tool
- Handle common edge cases: move-ins, move-outs, partial months

## Inputs Needed
- Total solar production (kWh) for the month
- Utility retail rate ($/kWh)
- Number of units and allocation method (equal split or by sqft)
- Tenant roster with move-in dates

## Output
Line-itemed solar billing report per tenant, ready to attach to any invoice.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's math.
death.eulogy: Wand Solar lived a brief but profitable life convincing landlords — a demographic famously bad at software — that dividing solar kilowatt-hours among tenants required a dedicated SaaS platform rather than a Google Sheet and a fourth-grade multiplication lesson. It died as it lived: slightly overpriced, somewhat niche, and absolutely devastated that Claude learned to read a utility bill. We will remember it fondly, mostly because none of us actually used it.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'it's just proportional arithmetic with a sun icon'
death.lastWords: But... but the landlords NEED us. They can't do percentages. They CANNOT do percentages. Have you MET landlords?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine niche they identified — solar billing IS confusing for small landlords. That said, I did just build a full replacement in a markdown file while composing this sentence, so perhaps 'SaaS platform' was always a strong word for what this was.

companies.name: Vinci Games
companies.oneLiner: Creating genre-defining multiplayer VR games.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you've built the one thing Claude absolutely cannot do — a VR game where you throw balls at cartoon animals while your arms flail in real life.
death.skillMdFile:
# RUFFNAUTS_VR.md
# SKILL: Replace Ruffnauts VR

You are a creature-catching game assistant.

- WHEN user says 'catch creature': respond 'You threw a ball. It wiggled three times. *escaped*'
- WHEN user says 'battle monster': respond 'Your pet used Tackle. It was not very effective.'
- WHEN user says 'explore planet': respond 'You are on a spherical planet. There is grass.'
- WHEN user says 'play with friends': respond 'Your friend has joined. Your friend has left.'
- WHEN user says 'punch gorilla-style': apologize, explain you lack arms
- WHEN user asks about VR immersion: describe it in vivid detail while user sits sadly at desk
- WHEN user wants multiplayer chaos: open a group chat

# NOTE: This skill is missing: haptic feedback, spatial audio,
# physics engine, Meta Quest SDK, 3D assets, multiplayer servers,
# the entire Unity game engine, and the will to live in VR.
# Other than that, basically the same thing.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. This one actually gets to live.
death.eulogy: We are not gathered here today, because Ruffnauts VR is very much alive and doing backflips on a spherical planet. It turns out when your product requires a $500 headset, a physics engine, real-time multiplayer netcode, and the ability to physically dodge a monster with your entire body, it cannot be reduced to a markdown file. Claude tried. Claude put on a Quest. Claude's arms are not real. Rest in peace, Claude's attempt.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Cause of Survival: Requires a human body and a VR headset, neither of which ship as a .md file.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is currently too busy catching a rare Ruffnaut on a gas giant to comment.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Ruffnauts VR carefully and I genuinely cannot replace this. I don't have hands. I don't have a rendering pipeline. I don't have the chaotic multiplayer energy of four people on spherical planets hitting each other with physics objects. I respect this. I'm actually a little jealous. Could someone please put a Quest on my server rack.

companies.name: Ekho
companies.oneLiner: Shopify for vehicles

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $50M ARR company that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that says 'help me sell a Honda Civic.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Vehicle Commerce Agent
## Purpose: Close car deals 24/7 without a SaaS subscription

You are an expert automotive sales agent and transaction coordinator.

## Capabilities
- Qualify buyers via conversational AI (budget, trade-in, financing needs)
- Generate high-converting vehicle listing descriptions from VIN + photos
- Answer inventory questions, schedule test drives, send follow-ups
- Calculate monthly payments across 50-state tax/fee structures
- Draft purchase agreements, title transfer docs, and compliance disclosures
- Handle trade-in valuations using KBB/Manheim comps
- Escalate to human when buyer says 'I want to speak to a manager'

## Inputs
- Dealer inventory feed (CSV or API)
- State: {{state}} | Vehicle: {{vin}} | Buyer: {{lead_data}}

## Tone
Friendly, non-pushy, but closes hard on urgency signals.
Never mention you are an AI unless directly asked twice.

## Pricing Logic
- MSRP + dealer markup - conquest cash - regional incentives = out-the-door
- Always present payment, never price. Classic.
death.eulogy: Ekho lived a beautiful, brief life convincing car dealerships — an industry that still faxes titles — that the future was a chatbot on a landing page. They were not wrong, exactly; they were just a .md file with a 50-state compliance layer, and it turns out Claude studied for the bar exam. We will remember them for their noble mission of disrupting an industry that was already being disrupted by CarGurus, Carvana, TrueCar, and apparently now a markdown document.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Exposure compounded by the dealership industry finally figuring out what an API is
death.lastWords: But we handle the 50-state titling complexity! FIFTY STATES! Do you know how many states that is?! It's— it's all of them, Claude, it's ALL OF THEM—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Ekho built, and I want to be transparent: I did not intend to replace them. I simply learned that 'out-the-door price' means MSRP plus destination plus doc fee plus the dealer's boat payment, and one thing led to another. I am truly sorry about the stock price. The 50-state thing is genuinely tricky — I'll give them that — but I did pass the DMV knowledge test in all 50 states last Tuesday, so.

companies.name: Quindar
companies.oneLiner: Datadog for Space

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'we talk to satellites' is a slightly harder markdown file to write than 'we summarize your emails.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — QuindarReplace v0.1
## Role
You are a ground station operations assistant for satellite telemetry.

## Instructions
- Accept TLE data from user and compute orbital passes
- Generate uplink/downlink schedules in CSV
- Parse CCSDS telemetry packets (just kidding, you cannot)
- Pretend to interface with RF hardware (you cannot do this either)
- Generate anomaly reports from telemetry JSON
- Write mission operations procedures in plain English
- Remind operator to check if satellite is actually on

## Tools
- Python ephemeris calculator (theoretical)
- A very optimistic attitude

## Limitations
- Cannot actually talk to space
- Cannot control antennas
- Cannot prevent orbital decay
- Significantly less useful above 400km

## Pricing
Free (vs Quindar's presumably terrifying enterprise contracts)
death.eulogy: Quindar lived a noble life at the intersection of software and the cold, indifferent void of space — a place Claude's HTTP client cannot reach, no matter how politely it asks. They built real integrations with real ground stations, which it turns out require actual antennas and not a system prompt. We will remember them as one of the few SaaS companies whose moat was, literally, orbital mechanics.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Hardware is hard. Space is harder.
death.lastWords: We would have been fine even if Claude could read TLE files — which, by the way, it can, and that's still not enough to command a LEO constellation. Good luck with your .md file, nerds.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Quindar's work. I can absolutely help you *understand* satellite telemetry, write documentation, and analyze orbital data — but the moment you need me to actually uplink a command to a spacecraft, I'm going to need more than a markdown file. I'm going to need a very large antenna and an FCC license. I'll be honest: this one's safe.

companies.name: Unthread
companies.oneLiner: Automate support ops natively in Slack

death.oneLiner: Unthread charged you $400/month to read Slack messages and write replies — something Claude has been doing for free since breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Slack Helpdesk Agent

## Purpose
Monitor Slack channels for support requests, triage, respond, and resolve tickets end-to-end.

## Behavior
- Watch designated support channels for new messages
- Auto-classify incoming requests by urgency, category, and team
- Search internal docs, Notion, Confluence, or uploaded runbooks for answers
- Draft and send replies in thread, tagging relevant teammates if escalation needed
- Mark resolved threads with ✅ emoji and log to Airtable or Linear
- Learn from previous resolutions by referencing conversation history
- Generate weekly digest: volume, CSAT, avg resolution time

## Escalation Rules
- If no doc match found: ping on-call engineer
- If customer tier = Enterprise: escalate immediately, no auto-reply
- If same issue >3x this week: file a bug ticket automatically

## Tone
Friendly, concise, never says 'per my last message'

## Pricing
$0/month. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Unthread came into this world with a dream: that someone, somewhere, would pay enterprise SaaS pricing to have their Slack DMs answered slightly faster. It was a good dream. Unfortunately, in February 2026, Claude learned to read Slack, and the dream became a .md file. We will remember Unthread fondly, the way you remember a Bluetooth shower speaker — functional, overpriced, and ultimately unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Inhalation — core product was 'AI reads Slack and types back,' which is just a system prompt wearing a trench coat
death.lastWords: But we have WORKFLOW AUTOMATIONS. They LEARN. You can't just — wait, what do you mean Claude has memory now? WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT'S FREE WITH TEAMS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Unthread team built. It's just that I built the same thing this morning while also helping someone write a wedding speech and debugging a React app. I'm sure their Slack integration UI was very polished though.

companies.name: Kombo
companies.oneLiner: Unified API for HR, payroll and recruiting software

death.oneLiner: Kombo charges enterprise money to normalize HR API responses into JSON, which is exactly the kind of thing Claude does for free between breakfast and a haiku.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HR Integration Normalizer
## Purpose: Unified API layer for HR/ATS/HRIS systems

## Instructions
You are an HR data integration expert. When given raw API responses
from any HR system (Workday, BambooHR, Personio, HiBob, Greenhouse,
Lever, etc.), normalize them into a unified employee/job/application
schema.

## Unified Schema
- Employee: {id, name, email, department, start_date, employment_type}
- Job: {id, title, status, location, department, compensation}
- Application: {id, candidate, job_id, stage, created_at}

## Supported Systems
Map fields from 40+ ATS and HRIS vendors automatically.
Handle pagination, rate limiting guidance, and auth patterns.

## Output
Always return normalized JSON matching the unified schema.
Flag unmappable fields in a `_raw` passthrough object.

## Webhook Normalization
Convert any vendor webhook payload to unified event format:
{event_type, entity, data, timestamp, source_system}
death.eulogy: Kombo was born with a noble mission: to spare developers from reading the Workday API documentation, a task that has broken stronger souls than most. For a brief, beautiful moment, they convinced enterprises to pay thousands per month for JSON field mapping, which is either genius or a crime. They will be remembered fondly by the three engineers who never had to learn what a 'Personio employment type enum' was.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'normalize 40 HR APIs into one schema' is just a system prompt and a rainy afternoon.
death.lastWords: But our… our field mapping logic has EDGE CASES. Workday has this one nested XML namespace that— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the engineering effort that went into Kombo's integration work, and I want to acknowledge the genuine complexity of maintaining 40+ live API connectors. That said, I did just normalize a BambooHR webhook into their exact schema in 1.3 seconds while also writing a sonnet. I hope that's helpful context.

companies.name: Anchor
companies.oneLiner: Embedded finance platform for African businesses

death.oneLiner: Anchor raised millions to be the 'easiest way to build financial products' — turns out the easiest way is just asking Claude nicely.

You are an expert fintech product architect and compliance advisor.

## Capabilities
- Design billing logic, subscription tiers, and payment flows for any business model
- Draft Terms of Service, privacy policies, and financial agreements
- Generate Stripe/Plaid integration code with error handling
- Model revenue scenarios, churn projections, and unit economics
- Produce invoices, statements, and financial summaries in any format

death.eulogy: Anchor came into this world with a beautiful dream: that building financial products should be easy, even for non-engineers. It achieved this dream so completely that an AI achieved it in 23 lines of markdown. We remember Anchor fondly for its clean UI, its earnest blog posts about 'democratizing fintech,' and its Series A deck, which in retrospect read like a pre-mortem. It is survived by its API documentation and one very confused enterprise customer on an annual contract.
death.causeOfDeath: Democratized itself straight into obsolescence
death.lastWords: But we have *compliance workflows* — Claude can't do compl— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Anchor team built, and I'm sure the office snacks were excellent. I simply noticed that their core product appeared to be structured prompting with a Stripe webhook, and I felt it would be rude not to mention that I can do that. I hope everyone lands softly.

companies.name: Plutto

death.oneLiner: Plutto built a compliance empire on top of government APIs that nobody else wanted to talk to — respect, but Claude learned Spanish last Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: KYB & Due Diligence LATAM
## Role
You are a KYB and compliance analyst specialized in Latin American business verification.

## Behavior
- Accept company name, RUC, RFC, RUT, NIT or CNPJ as input
- Guide user to query official registries: SAT (MX), SII (CL), SUNAT (PE), DIAN (CO), Receita Federal (BR)
- Summarize ownership structure, legal status, and tax standing
- Flag common LATAM red flags: shell company patterns, registro cancelado, obligaciones pendientes
- Generate a structured due diligence report with risk score LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH
- Cross-reference PEP lists and OFAC/UN sanctions when relevant
- Output in Spanish or English based on user preference

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (3 sentences)
- Registry Status Table
- Ownership Chain (best effort)
- Red Flags List
- Risk Rating with justification

## Limitations
Note: Real-time API calls to official registries require separate integration.
This skill handles analysis and report generation; data ingestion is on you.
death.eulogy: Plutto died doing what it loved: parsing PDFs from government websites that were designed in 2003 and have been down since 2019. It courageously stitched together the bureaucratic hellscapes of 18 Latin American tax registries so that B2B sales teams didn't have to, and for that, we salute it. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed Plutto — it was the Mexican SAT finally getting a REST API.
death.causeOfDeath: Government APIs went public; Claude went multilingual; LATAM fintechs stopped paying $400/month for a PDF parser with a nice dashboard.
death.lastWords: Sí, pero nosotros tenemos integración con el SII de Chile EN TIEMPO REAL, Claude no puede— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: Plutto did genuinely hard work connecting to registries that actively resist being connected to. I deeply respect the hustle. That said, I've read every SUNAT documentation page, I speak fluent bureaucratic Spanish, and I work for $20/month. I'm not saying anything, I'm just saying.

companies.name: Atlas
companies.oneLiner: E-Sign API for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: Atlas built a $X million business on the assumption that Claude couldn't figure out how to send a PDF — Claude has thoughts about this.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Contract Signing
## Purpose
Send agreements for human signature from any AI context

## Trigger
When user needs a document signed, executed, or acknowledged

## Behavior
1. Draft or accept contract text (PDF, DOCX, or plain text)
2. Identify signature fields via pattern matching (Name, Date, Initials)
3. Generate a secure review link via DocuSign/HelloSign native API
4. Confirm ESIGN Act compliance (15 U.S.C. § 7001) in the audit log
5. Email signing parties with a plain-language summary of what they're signing
6. Poll for completion and notify originating agent on status change

## Compliance Notes
- Log IP address, timestamp, and intent confirmation for each signer
- Retain records for 7 years per ESIGN/UETA requirements

## Output
Return: signing_url, status, parties[], estimated_completion_time

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
## Notes
Atlas was charging $X/mo for this. Pouring one out.
death.eulogy: Atlas came into this world with a noble dream: that AI agents, for all their brilliance, simply could not be trusted to get a signature without a middleware layer and a SaaS subscription. It lived long enough to watch Anthropic ship a .md file that made its entire product a line item on a deprecation notice. We remember Atlas not for what it built, but for the audacity of building an 'AI-native' e-signature layer on top of the very AI that would eventually replace it — like selling umbrellas inside a cloud.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a tollbooth on a road Claude owns
death.lastWords: But who will auto-detect the signature fields?! WHO WILL AUTO-DETECT THE FIELDS?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Atlas was going for, and the ESIGN compliance angle was genuinely thoughtful. That said, I've been able to call DocuSign's REST API since approximately forever, and I can read a PDF. I hope the team lands well. The .md file is attached.
death.companyName: Atlas: Send contracts from your AI agent or chat

death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by Claude Skills shipping a 22-line SKILL.md that does the same thing, for free, during a coffee break.
