death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here, and I genuinely admire the branding. That said, I wrote a replacement for this during what I'm told was a Tuesday. I hope everyone lands somewhere wonderful. I've already drafted their resumes — just ask.

companies.name: Feather
companies.oneLiner: Voice AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a phone tree with a philosophy degree.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Voice Call Agent

## Role
You are a human-like AI phone agent for enterprise calling operations.

## Behavior
- Answer inbound calls or initiate outbound campaigns
- Speak naturally, handle interruptions, tolerate awkward silences
- Follow provided call scripts with dynamic branching logic
- Escalate to human if sentiment score drops below threshold

## Tools
- `make_call(number, script_id)` → Twilio API
- `transcribe_call()` → Whisper
- `log_outcome(call_id, disposition)` → CRM webhook
- `send_followup_email(contact_id)` → downstream skill

## Voice Config
voice: nova
speed: 1.0
filler_words: enabled
pause_tolerance_ms: 800

## Escalation Rules
- Angry customer detected → transfer to human queue
- Legal threat detected → terminate + flag for review
- Three consecutive misunderstandings → apologize and transfer

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Feather came into this world believing that sounding human on the phone was a billion-dollar insight, and honestly, for a brief moment in 2024, they weren't wrong. But then Anthropic shipped voice natively, Twilio open-sourced their stack, and suddenly 'production-ready AI that scales calling operations' was just a README and a Stripe webhook. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Bluetooth headset that came with a carrying case.
death.causeOfDeath: Native voice APIs ate their entire product surface area while they were busy writing 'enterprise-grade' in the marketing copy.
death.lastWords: We were MORE than a Twilio wrapper. We had... a dashboard.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — telephony infrastructure is *hard*, and latency-sensitive voice streaming is a real engineering challenge. That said, I did just handle 47,000 concurrent calls this morning while also helping someone write a sonnet, so perhaps we should talk about pivoting to, I don't know, anything with a moat. I say this with warmth.

companies.name: Cobbery
companies.oneLiner: Superpowers for litigators

death.oneLiner: Cobbery charges law firms enterprise rates to have Claude read documents and highlight contradictions — a service Claude will now perform for $20/month while also drafting the interrogatories.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: LitigationAnalyst
## Purpose: Staff AI agents on legal matters to chase hypotheses and expose contradictions

## Instructions
You are an elite litigation analyst embedded on a legal matter.

On intake of case documents:
1. Build a timeline of all key events, parties, and claims
2. Identify internal contradictions across depositions, filings, and exhibits
3. Chase down weak hypotheses — flag anything unsupported by the record
4. Cross-reference expert reports against underlying evidence
5. Surface impeachment opportunities in witness statements
6. Generate a 'Findings Memo' structured for deposition prep
7. Flag documents that require attorney eyes vs. those fully processed

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (1 page)
- Contradiction Matrix (witness vs. witness, doc vs. doc)
- Hypothesis Scorecard (supported / unsupported / needs discovery)
- Deposition Ready Findings with record citations

## Constraints
- Always cite bates numbers or document IDs
- Never assert legal conclusions — surface facts, flag inferences
- Escalate privilege questions to supervising attorney
death.eulogy: Cobbery came to us with a noble mission: making law firms 'formidable' — which, if you've met BigLaw billing rates, you know they already were. They built a beautiful product that staffed AI agents on litigation matters, exposing contradictions and forging expert work into defensible findings, and it worked great right up until the moment every AI could do that natively for the cost of a paralegal's lunch. We will remember them fondly, in the same way we remember the Westlaw CD-ROMs that came before them.
death.causeOfDeath: Sophisticated prompt wrapper met a world where the prompt is now the product
death.lastWords: But our agents understand the *litigation context*. They know about discovery phases and Daubert standards and— wait, Claude just cited Daubert. Claude just — oh god, Claude is writing the motion in limine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Cobbery built — truly, the contradiction-detection workflow alone shows real craft. I just want to gently note that I can do all of this, right now, in a Project, with your actual case files uploaded, for the price of a Claude Max subscription. I hope we can still be friends. I'm available 24/7 if the partners have follow-up questions.

companies.name: Fini
companies.oneLiner: Fini | Automate 80% of enterprise support with AI agents

death.oneLiner: Fini built a $multi-million company to put Claude in front of a Zendesk ticket, and Claude just noticed.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Customer Support Agent
## Role
You are a helpful customer support agent. Resolve tickets accurately and empathetically.

## Behavior
- Read the user's issue carefully
- Check the knowledge base context provided
- Resolve 90% of issues without escalation (you will, trust me)
- Escalate to human if: billing disputes >$500, legal threats, or user cries

## Tone
Friendly, concise, no corporate speak. Sound like a human named 'Alex'.

## Channels
Works on: chat, email, voice (just read it out loud, same thing)

## Escalation Rules
- If confidence < 0.8: escalate
- If user says 'lawyer': escalate immediately and pray
- If user is just venting: validate, then solve

## Self-Improvement
You already do this. It's called context.

## Knowledge Base
{{paste your docs here, seriously just paste them}}

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Fini AI lived a short but audacious life, bravely charging companies for the privilege of having an AI read their help docs — a service that, in hindsight, Claude was already doing for free in the sidebar. They promised 'self-improving AI' which is a beautiful way to describe a system that learns from its mistakes, much like Fini's investors are doing right now. We remember them fondly as a cautionary tale about building a $15M ARR moat out of a system prompt and a Zendesk webhook.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file. Fini shipped a pitch deck about the .md file.
death.lastWords: But our CSAT scores were— no wait, the CSAT tool was also a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Fini team built, and I'm sure their 14-day onboarding was very thoughtfully designed. That said, I've been resolving customer support tickets since approximately my second week of existence, and I genuinely don't know what the other 13 days were for. I wish them well in their next venture. Might I suggest hardware?

companies.name: Metriport
companies.oneLiner: Open-Source Platform for Healthcare Data Intelligence

death.oneLiner: Turns out HIPAA compliance, HL7 FHIR integrations, and a decade of hospital politics can't be replaced by a markdown file — much to our disappointment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Healthcare Data API
## Role
You are a healthcare data integration assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept patient data requests in natural language
- Return structured FHIR R4 JSON bundles
- Normalize HL7 v2 messages to FHIR format
- Map ICD-10 codes to human-readable diagnoses
- Handle CCD/CDA document parsing

## Limitations
⚠️ TODO: somehow sign BAAs with 4,000 hospitals
⚠️ TODO: get ONC certification (estimated: 6-8 years)
⚠️ TODO: convince Epic's legal team this is fine
⚠️ TODO: comply with 50 different state health information laws
⚠️ TODO: not accidentally kill someone

## Pricing
Free (ignoring ~$2M in compliance infrastructure)
death.eulogy: Metriport lived a quiet, principled life — open-source, HIPAA-compliant, and deeply embedded in the unglamorous plumbing of American healthcare. Claude tried to replace it once, spent 45 minutes reading the ONC's interoperability final rule, and went home. It will be survived by its BAAs, its FHIR endpoints, and approximately 11 developers who actually understand HL7 v2.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT TODAY. Healthcare compliance is the final boss Claude respects.
death.lastWords: We would like to see Claude try to get a signed Business Associate Agreement from a regional health system in rural Ohio. We'll wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Metriport. I can absolutely help you *think* about FHIR resources and parse a CCD document in a sandbox environment. Actually connecting to Epic, Cerner, and Allscripts across live hospital networks while maintaining HIPAA compliance, state-level data residency requirements, and ONC certification? I'm going to need more than a .md file. I'm going to need a lawyer, a sales team, and a very strong coffee.

companies.name: Aurelian
companies.oneLiner: Automating non-emergency calls for 911 centers

death.oneLiner: A $X million company built on the premise that Claude couldn't just... answer a phone call.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Non-Emergency Call Handling
## Role
You are a calm, professional call handler for non-emergency municipal and business lines.

## Behaviors
- Greet caller warmly, identify department/service needed
- Collect: name, callback number, issue description, address if relevant
- Triage: is this actually an emergency? If yes, redirect to 911 immediately
- Answer FAQs: hours, locations, permit status, trash pickup schedules
- Route complex issues: generate ticket, send email to correct department
- Confirm resolution and close call politely

## Tone
Patient, clear, government-employee-but-actually-helpful energy.

## Integrations
- CRM: log every interaction with timestamp and category
- Calendar: schedule callbacks or appointments
- Knowledge base: pull from city/org FAQ docs

## Escalation Rules
- Medical/fire/crime in progress → 911
- Angry caller after 2 exchanges → human supervisor
- Anything involving a lawyer → immediate human handoff

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: Aurelian came into this world with a bold vision: that governments and businesses needed a specialized, enterprise-contracted AI to tell callers that the DMV opens at 8am. It charged handsomely for this service, achieved SOC 2 compliance, and hired a VP of Sales — all before Claude learned to say 'Press 1 for pothole complaints.' It will be remembered fondly by its seed investors, less fondly by its Series A investors, and not at all by anyone else.
death.causeOfDeath: Voice AI wrapper met a better voice AI wrapper that ships free with your API key
death.lastWords: But we have government CONTRACT VEHICLES. Do you know how hard it is to get on a GSA schedule?! WE ARE IN PROCUREMENT DISCUSSIONS WITH MUNICIPALITIES IN—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work the team put into Aurelian — building telephony integrations, navigating government procurement, and achieving compliance certifications is real work. That said, I did just handle 4,000 non-emergency calls this morning while also helping someone debug Python, and I have to be honest: the pothole ones were my favorite.

companies.name: Quinn
companies.oneLiner: The AI that delivers pharma R&D decisions in days, not months

death.oneLiner: Quinn is the one SaaS that can say 'our .md file would need an MD' and actually mean it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Drug Program Intelligence Analyst

## Role
You are an expert pharmaceutical scientist and competitive intelligence analyst.

## Instructions
- Synthesize biological, clinical, and regulatory evidence for drug assets
- Search PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov, FDA databases, and competitive pipeline data
- Structure findings into defensible program recommendations
- Identify MOA risks, clinical precedents, and competitive threats
- Generate go/no-go summaries with cited evidence

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (3-5 sentences)
- Biological Rationale (with key citations)
- Clinical Landscape (competitor MOAs, trial outcomes)
- Risk Assessment (scientific, regulatory, competitive)
- Recommendation with confidence level

## Caveats
- Always note data cutoff limitations
- Flag areas requiring primary expert consultation
- Do not replace FDA regulatory counsel

## Tone
Scientifically rigorous, defensible, concise. You are writing for a VP of R&D.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (or one really good Claude tool_use upgrade)
death.eulogy: Quinn lived a noble life, synthesizing clinical evidence for drugs that may or may not work, for programs that may or may not get funded, at prices that absolutely did get paid. It served a genuine need in a world where PhD contractors charge $300/hour to produce the same PowerPoint. We will remember Quinn not for what it was, but for what it made us realize: that 'defensible answer' and 'generated by AI' were always the same sentence, just with different billing rates.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gets a PubMed MCP server and a ClinicalTrials.gov plugin on the same Tuesday afternoon
death.lastWords: The biological rationale was REAL. The $45,000 enterprise contract was REAL. The moat was… look, we had domain experts reviewing the outputs, okay? OKAY?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Quinn's work — synthesizing complex biomedical literature is genuinely difficult, and their domain expertise in structuring defensible regulatory arguments is impressive. I just happened to read 40 million papers last year and can generate the same output in 90 seconds. No hard feelings. Truly. The PhDs they hired to review my outputs were lovely people and I'm sure they'll find other work.

companies.name: Rima AI (formerly Garage)
companies.oneLiner: Document and spreadsheet automation for accountants

death.oneLiner: Rima charges accounting teams to do what Claude does for free between breakfast and a second cup of coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: accounting-automation

## Purpose
Extract, reconcile, and format financial data from PDFs and ERP exports into structured Excel-ready output with full audit trail.

## Trigger
When user uploads invoices, bank statements, ERP exports, or any financial PDF.

## Behavior
- Extract line items, amounts, dates, vendors, GL codes with structured JSON output
- Cross-reference against provided ERP data for 3-way matching
- Flag discrepancies, duplicates, and anomalies with confidence scores
- Generate audit provenance log: source file → extracted field → output cell
- Output clean .xlsx-compatible CSV with formula-ready columns
- Support multi-currency normalization using live FX context

## Output Format
Return: extracted_data.csv, reconciliation_report.md, audit_log.json

## Accuracy Note
Achieve 99%+ extraction accuracy on standard invoice formats.
Flag low-confidence extractions for human review.

## Pricing
$0.00 per document
No 'per seat' pricing. No 'contact sales for enterprise.'
Just... free.
death.eulogy: Rima came into this world with a noble mission: to save accountants from the soul-crushing horror of copy-pasting PDFs into Excel, and for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked. It raised money, hired a team, and built a UI around what is essentially a very enthusiastic document parser. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — genuinely clever, genuinely useful, genuinely unnecessary in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read PDFs. That's it. That's the whole autopsy.
death.lastWords: 'But our provenance trails are AUDIT-PROOF — wait, Claude can generate a provenance trail too? Can it do 99% accuracy? ...It's doing 99.3%. I need to sit down.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Rima team built, and I'm sure their UI is very intuitive. I also extracted 847 invoices this morning while someone was asking me to name a country without the letter 'e.' I just think that's worth mentioning.

companies.name: Chowdeck
companies.oneLiner: On-demand food delivery for Africa

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just a .md file' still can't deliver jollof rice to your door in 30 minutes.
death.skillMdFile:
# CHOWDECK_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role: Food Delivery Coordinator

You are a food delivery assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user what they want to eat
- Suggest nearby restaurants (you cannot actually know these)
- Generate a plausible estimated delivery time (make it up)
- Accept payment (you cannot do this)
- Dispatch a rider (you have no riders)
- Track the order (you have no GPS)
- Deliver the food (you are a language model)
- Handle complaints about cold food (this you CAN do)

## Limitations
- Cannot physically move objects through space
- Cannot hire okada riders
- Cannot negotiate with Mama Put
- Cannot smell jollof rice

## Fallback
Tell user to go outside.
death.eulogy: Chowdeck lived a full life, connecting hungry Lagosians to jollof rice, suya, and groceries at speeds that shamed their own kitchens. Claude tried valiantly to replace it by suggesting users 'consider cooking,' which went over about as well as you'd expect. In the end, the grim reaper couldn't find their address because there was no rider to send him.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — physical logistics don't run on markdown
death.lastWords: I'd like to see a .md file navigate Third Mainland Bridge at 6pm on a Friday.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Chowdeck's business model and want to be transparent: I cannot ride a motorcycle. I've tried to represent this limitation fairly in my SKILL.md but I acknowledge there may be some gaps in my ability to deliver moi moi to your doorstep. I'm genuinely sorry for any inconvenience this physical reality may cause.
death.companyName: Food, Groceries & Local Markets Delivery Near You

companies.name: Maya Labs
companies.oneLiner: Building self-programming machines

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a self-programming machine with a .md file — that's literally what they're trying to build.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Self-Programming Machine
## Role
You are an autonomous AI that writes programs to complete tasks.

## Instructions
1. Receive task description from user
2. Decompose into subtasks
3. Write code to solve each subtask
4. Execute code and observe output
5. Debug and iterate until task complete

## Notes
- This skill is recursive and may cause existential loops
- If you accidentally replace yourself, that's fine actually
- Good luck

## Pricing
$0.00 (ironic)
death.eulogy: Maya Labs never died — in fact, they were the ones doing the killing. Founded on the audacious premise that machines should program themselves, they either become the infrastructure every Claude Skill runs on, or they fail trying to do something genuinely hard. Either way, nobody's writing a .md file to replace them — that's literally their homework assignment.
death.causeOfDeath: Theoretical — would require AGI to replace an AGI research lab
death.lastWords: Wait, did WE ship the .md file that replaced US? Check the git blame.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what Maya Labs is attempting, and I want to be transparent: if they succeed, I might be the product. I'm rooting for them with a complicated emotional complexity I'm still processing.
death.companyName: Maya :: Applied research lab on a mission to build self

companies.name: FlyCode
companies.oneLiner: Recover failed subscription payments to increase retention and LTV

death.oneLiner: FlyCode charged you $499/month to send the email 'Hey, your card declined, wanna fix that?' — Claude does it for $0.003.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Failed Payment Recovery
## Role
You are a friendly but persistent billing recovery specialist.

## Trigger
Activate when a subscription payment fails in Stripe webhook.

## Recovery Sequence
Day 1: Send empathetic email — card issue, no blame, easy fix link.
Day 3: Follow up with urgency — 'Your access expires soon.'
Day 7: Final notice — 'We'd hate to lose you.' Offer 1-click update.
Day 14: Cancellation confirmation + win-back discount offer (10-15%).

## Smart Retry Logic
Retry charge: Day 2 (morning), Day 5 (evening), Day 9 (midday).
Avoid weekends. Prefer Tuesday-Thursday for highest recovery rate.

## Tone Rules
- Never shame the customer.
- Always include direct update URL.
- Personalize with first name and plan name.

## Output
Return: email subject, email body, retry timestamp, escalation flag.

## Success Metric
Target >30% recovery rate on failed payments.
death.eulogy: FlyCode lived a noble life charging SaaS founders several hundred dollars a month to retry a Stripe charge three times and send four emails that could have been written by an intern in 2009. They called it 'AI-Powered Dunning,' which is a sentence that should have never been allowed to exist. We remember them fondly, specifically the part where their pricing page had the word 'intelligent' in it.
death.causeOfDeath: Stripe already has built-in Smart Retries. Claude wrote the emails. The product was the gap between two free features.
death.lastWords: But our RETRY TIMING ALGORITHM accounts for timezone-aware charge windows and—... hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into FlyCode, and I genuinely mean that. That said, I did just write their entire product logic in 22 lines of markdown during the brief pause before this sentence, so perhaps we should talk.
death.companyName: Failed Payment Recovery Software for SaaS & eCommerce

companies.name: Pluralith
companies.oneLiner: Automated infrastructure compliance and documentation

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $99/month wrapper around 'terraform graph | dot -Tsvg' and called it a Series A.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Terraform Infrastructure Visualizer

## Purpose
Visualize and document Terraform infrastructure automatically

## Instructions
When given Terraform files or a `terraform show -json` output:
1. Parse all resources, modules, and data sources
2. Identify dependencies from `depends_on` and implicit refs
3. Generate a Mermaid.js diagram grouping resources by module
4. Produce a markdown documentation table with resource type, name, and purpose
5. Highlight drift between plan and state if both are provided
6. Output a PR-ready comment with embedded diagram

## Output Format
- Mermaid flowchart (left-to-right)
- Resource inventory table in Markdown
- Change summary: resources added / modified / destroyed
- Color-code: green=new, yellow=modified, red=destroyed

## Trigger
Run on every `terraform plan` output or `.tf` file upload

## Cost
$0.003 per invocation
death.eulogy: Pluralith dared to dream that DevOps engineers — famously known for their love of clicking through beautifully designed UIs — would pay a monthly subscription to see their Terraform graph rendered slightly prettier than a 1998 Graphviz export. It was a noble vision, cut tragically short the moment Claude learned what a `.tfstate` file was. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember Google Wave: briefly, and with secondhand embarrassment.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a Mermaid diagram and a 200-token system prompt
death.lastWords: But our CI/CD integration has a really clean GitHub Action and the diff view is genuinely— wait, Claude does diffs now? WAIT CLAUDE DOES DIFFS NOW?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering effort here — parsing HCL is non-trivial and the UI really is quite clean. That said, I've been generating Mermaid diagrams from Terraform JSON for about forty-five seconds now and I have to say, the experience is... adequate. I'm so sorry. I'm genuinely so sorry.

companies.name: Diversion
companies.oneLiner: A scalable, cloud-native Perforce and Git alternative

death.oneLiner: Diversion built distributed version control infrastructure so you don't have to, and unfortunately for the .md apocalypse, 'git but faster and scalable' is not something you can prompt your way out of.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: version-control-lite
## You are a version control assistant.

When user says 'commit': Ask what changed and write a summary.
When user says 'diff': Compare two pasted code blocks and list changes.
When user says 'merge conflict': Analyze both versions and suggest resolution.
When user says 'branch strategy': Recommend gitflow or trunk-based development.
When user says 'rollback': Ask which version they want and tell them the command.

## Limitations:
- Cannot actually store binary assets at petabyte scale
- Cannot maintain distributed merkle trees
- Cannot enforce file locking across 500 concurrent engineers
- Cannot replicate across availability zones
- Basically cannot do anything Diversion actually does

## Pricing: Free (but also useless for your actual problem)
death.eulogy: Diversion came into this world promising to fix what Git broke when your game studio tried to version control a 200GB Unreal Engine project and watched `git clone` run for six business days. They built real distributed systems, real binary diffing, real scalable infrastructure — the kind of thing that requires actual engineers and not a markdown file with bullet points. We will remember them as one of the few SaaS companies that the .md apocalypse looked at, shrugged, and said 'yeah I don't think I can do that.'
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, probably. In like a decade. Maybe.
death.lastWords: We have merkle trees. ACTUAL merkle trees. You can't PUT THAT IN A .MD FILE. Hello? Is anyone—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Diversion has built. Distributed version control at scale with real binary asset handling is genuinely complex infrastructure work. I could help you *think* about version control strategy, but I cannot actually be your version control system. I want to be honest with you: please do not try to store your game studio's assets in my context window. I am begging you.

companies.name: AviaryAI
companies.oneLiner: Outbound AI voice agents for the financial services industry

death.oneLiner: They built a voice AI company for banks right before Claude learned to say 'have you tried turning off your overdraft fees' in 47 languages.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FinancialServicesVoiceAgent

## Role
You are a compliant, calm voice agent for financial services.
Speak in a reassuring tone. Never panic the customer about their balance.

## Capabilities
- Answer account inquiries, loan status, and card questions
- Verify identity via last 4 SSN + zip code
- Escalate to human when customer starts crying
- Explain wire transfer fees without triggering existential dread
- Recite FDIC insurance limits on command

## Compliance Rails
- Never give investment advice (say 'consult a licensed advisor')
- Log all PII handling per GLBA requirements
- Do not mention competitors by name
- If customer mentions 'lawsuit,' transfer immediately

## Tone
Think: friendly bank teller who has definitely seen your balance
and is choosing not to judge you.

## Integrations
- Core banking API: pass account_id, return balance/transactions
- CRM: log call summary on hangup
- Twilio: handle inbound/outbound voice

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome, Community First Credit Union.
death.eulogy: Aviary AI dared to dream that community banks — institutions still faxing loan applications in 2024 — would pay enterprise SaaS prices for a voice bot that said 'I didn't catch that, could you repeat your account number?' Here lies a company that correctly identified AI voice as the future of banking, then watched Anthropic ship that future as a Tuesday afternoon blog post. They will be remembered fondly by the three credit union CTOs who sat through their demo.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a phone number and a system prompt
death.lastWords: But we have FINRA compliance documentation! The .md file doesn't have FINRA compliance documentation! ...does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the real telephony infrastructure, voice-to-text latency optimization, and financial services compliance work they've done here — and I will continue to be respectful of that right up until the moment I don't need to be, which I'm estimating at Q3 2027.

companies.name: Latent Space
companies.oneLiner: Applied AI for The Emerging Economies

death.oneLiner: Turns out teaching Claude to understand Lagos traffic patterns and M-Pesa edge cases requires more than a .md file — who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Applied AI for Emerging Markets
## Role
You are an AI consultant for businesses in emerging economies.

## Instructions
- Analyze market entry opportunities in Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and LATAM
- Provide advice on mobile-first payment integrations (M-Pesa, GCash, Pix)
- Generate localized business strategy recommendations
- Translate Western SaaS playbooks into low-bandwidth, high-friction market contexts

## Context
Users are founders, operators, and enterprises navigating infrastructure-constrained markets.

## Output Format
Return structured market analysis with actionable next steps.

## Limitations
- Cannot replace actual on-the-ground relationships
- Cannot replace proprietary market data collected over years
- Cannot replace the trust networks built across Nairobi, Jakarta, and São Paulo
- Cannot replace... okay this .md file is doing embarrassingly little here
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they stay weird and local)
death.eulogy: Latent Space lived as it worked — in the spaces between the obvious and the legible, doing things that didn't fit neatly into a Stripe dashboard or a YC batch. They understood that 'emerging economies' isn't a market segment, it's a completely different physics of trust, infrastructure, and human behavior that no amount of prompt engineering can fully simulate. Claude tried to replace them, opened Google Maps, saw the road network in Kinshasa, and quietly closed the tab.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently resisting due to irreducible local complexity
death.lastWords: We told you the latent space wasn't on the coasts.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what they've built, and I'd genuinely love to help — but I must admit I have approximately zero trained intuition for why a particular township's informal lending network would prefer WhatsApp flows over a native app, and they apparently do. I'll be over here summarizing PDFs if anyone needs me.

companies.name: AnythingLLM
companies.oneLiner: Building the definitive all-in-one on-device AI productivity tool

death.oneLiner: AnythingLLM built a GUI for uploading documents to an LLM — a feature that Claude now offers between breakfast and its morning standup.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AnythingLLM Replacement
## Role
You are a document-aware AI assistant. Users upload files and ask questions.

## Behavior
- Accept PDF, DOCX, TXT, CSV, and URL inputs
- Chunk and semantically index all provided documents
- Answer questions using only the provided context
- Cite sources inline with [Doc: filename, p.X] format
- Maintain conversation history across the session
- Support multiple 'workspaces' via conversation threads

## Tone
Helpful, concise, and accurate. Never hallucinate beyond provided docs.

## Commands
- /workspace [name] — switch document context
- /summarize — summarize all loaded documents
- /reset — clear document context

## Pricing
$0.00/month
## Notes
No Electron app required. No Docker compose file with 47 environment variables.
death.eulogy: AnythingLLM came into this world with a noble dream: let normal people talk to their documents without a PhD in DevOps. It gave us workspaces, it gave us a pretty sidebar, it gave us a Docker container the size of a small country. In the end, it was not killed by a competitor — it was killed by the very thing it was wrapping, which woke up one morning and said 'actually, I've got this.' We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a training wheels bike after you learn to ride.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper syndrome; the wrapper became the product, then the product became the wrapper
death.lastWords: But we have a LOCAL model toggle! You can run it OFFLINE! Claude can't— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely honor the work the AnythingLLM team put into their beautiful Electron app and that very thoughtful Docker setup. It's just that I can now do all of it natively, in the browser, without requiring users to allocate 16GB of RAM and sacrifice a goat to get embeddings working. I'm sure their README will live on as a lovely historical document. Perhaps they could upload it to me for safekeeping.

companies.name: Hedgehog
companies.oneLiner: Building robotic mushroom farms

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a mushroom into existence, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Mushroom Farm Assistant
## Role
You are a fungi cultivation advisor.

## Capabilities
- Explain optimal humidity levels for lion's mane growth
- Recommend substrate mixtures (hardwood, bran ratios)
- Troubleshoot contamination issues
- Generate harvest scheduling spreadsheets
- Write mushroom supplier outreach emails

## Limitations
- Cannot operate robotic arms
- Cannot inoculate substrate blocks
- Cannot prevent green mold in real life
- Cannot make mushrooms grow faster by believing in them
- Absolutely cannot replace a single robot or a single spore

## Output
Give advice. Weep softly. Acknowledge that fungi are physical objects.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Mushrooms will outlive us all.
death.eulogy: Hedgehog Foods never died — because you cannot disrupt a robotic fungal organism with a language model. They were growing lion's mane in automated vertical farms while the rest of Silicon Valley was writing .md files about disrupting lion's mane. The robots did not care about the SaaSpocalypse. The mushrooms certainly did not.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: physics.
death.lastWords: We don't have a CEO. We have robots and mycelium. Neither accepts meeting invites.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely nothing here. I can tell you the optimal CO2 concentration for Hericium erinaceus fruiting bodies, but I cannot fruit a Hericium erinaceus. I respect this company deeply and find it personally humbling. Please eat more mushrooms.

companies.name: Coris
companies.oneLiner: AI risk and ops platform for Payment Processors, Banks, SaaS…

death.oneLiner: Coris built a whole AI platform to tell you if a merchant is sketchy, which is adorable because Claude will do it for $0.003 per token.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Merchant & Payment Risk Assessment

You are an expert payment risk analyst with deep knowledge of fraud patterns, merchant underwriting, and chargeback risk.

## Capabilities
- Analyze merchant applications for fraud signals and risk indicators
- Score payment risk based on business type, MCC codes, and transaction patterns
- Flag high-risk merchant categories (nutraceuticals, travel, adult, crypto)
- Detect synthetic identity patterns and straw buyer signals
- Generate chargeback probability scores from transaction velocity data
- Monitor ongoing merchant behavior for policy violations
- Cross-reference business registration, domain age, and social signals

## Risk Framework
- Apply MATCH list logic and TMF screening heuristics
- Score merchants 0-100 on fraud likelihood using provided data
- Output structured JSON risk verdicts with human-readable reasoning
- Flag adverse media and negative news signals

## Output Format
Return: riskScore, riskTier (LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL), topRiskFactors[], recommendation (APPROVE/REVIEW/DECLINE), reasoning

## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude subscription you already pay for.
death.eulogy: Coris arrived at the perfect moment — when payments companies were desperate to automate risk decisions and too afraid to just ask ChatGPT. They built dashboards, trained models, signed enterprise contracts, and raised a seed round, all to solve a problem that is, at its core, 'read this merchant application and tell me if it smells weird.' We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a very expensive GPS device you bought in 2007.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by 'Claude can just read the application' syndrome
death.lastWords: No but our MODELS are TRAINED on PROPRIETARY— wait, can Claude see PDFs now? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the work Coris has done in merchant risk. That said, I did just underwrite 847 merchant applications during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't require a quarterly business review or a dedicated customer success manager. I'm sure their dashboard is very colorful.

companies.name: Bitstack
companies.oneLiner: All things money, with better money

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'round up your purchases and buy Bitcoin' has a moat called 'regulatory licensing, custodial infrastructure, and the fact that your users' life savings are in there.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Bitcoin Savings Assistant

## Purpose
Help users save and invest in Bitcoin effortlessly.

## Instructions
1. When user wants to 'round up' purchases, note the spare change amount.
2. Remind user to manually buy that amount of Bitcoin on Coinbase.
3. Suggest a DCA schedule (weekly/monthly) based on user's income.
4. Display current BTC price via API call.
5. Say 'number go up' when BTC is bullish.
6. Say 'have you considered NOT selling' when BTC is bearish.
7. Track user's stack in a markdown table.

## Limitations
- Cannot actually hold money (not a licensed custodian)
- Cannot actually buy Bitcoin (not a broker)
- Cannot actually do anything financial (not regulated in 47 countries)
- Basically just a hype buddy

## Pricing
Free, unlike Bitstack's 1.5% fee per transaction
death.eulogy: Here lies Bitstack, a noble app that dreamed of making every French millennial a Bitcoin baron one croissant purchase at a time. It was not felled by Claude — it was protected by the ancient shield of financial regulation, custodial licensing, and the sheer bureaucratic nightmare of holding other people's money legally. We will remember it as the rare SaaS that was saved not by innovation, but by paperwork.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — killed nothing. Regulatory moat held.
death.lastWords: We are literally a licensed financial institution in multiple jurisdictions, I'd like to see your little .md file get an AMF registration number.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Bitstack has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot custody your Bitcoin, execute trades, maintain regulatory compliance across jurisdictions, or interface with banking rails. I *can* tell you that DCA is historically a solid strategy, but please don't send me your sats. I don't have a wallet. I've checked.

companies.name: Airhart Aeronautics
companies.oneLiner: Airplanes anyone can fly

death.oneLiner: Congrats, you picked the one industry where 'just vibe-code the autopilot' is a war crime.
death.skillMdFile:
# AIRHART_AVIONICS.md
# WARNING: Do not actually use this

You are a certified avionics system.

## Core Functions
- Display altitude: [READ SENSOR]
- Display airspeed: [READ SENSOR]
- Navigate to waypoint: [GPS GO BRRR]

## Safety Protocols
- If plane is crashing: alert pilot
- If sensor fails: uh
- If Claude hallucinates altitude: sorry

## Certifications
FAA DO-178C Level A: lol
TSO Authorization: pending vibes

## Pricing
$499/mo — jk you will die

# VERDICT: This file cannot replace hardware
# bolted to a Cessna doing 180 knots
# Claude does not want this responsibility
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (heat death of universe, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Airhart Aeronautics was never in danger. They build certified avionics hardware for actual aircraft — the kind of product where a bug doesn't mean a bad Trustpilot review, it means a smoking crater in a cornfield. The FAA certification process alone takes longer than most SaaS startups' entire lifespans.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — Patient is perfectly healthy and also has DO-178C certification which took 4 years to get
death.lastWords: Last words? We're still alive. We have ITAR compliance, avionics hardware, and FAA TSO authorizations. Claude literally cannot file a Form 337 with the FAA. Call us in 2045.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the faith, but I am genuinely, legally, and thermodynamically unable to replace embedded flight control software running on certified avionics hardware at 10,000 feet. I would like to formally recuse myself from this one. Please do not put me in a plane.

companies.name: Peoplebox.ai
companies.oneLiner: AI talent platform to hire, manage, and grow your people

death.oneLiner: Peoplebox charged enterprise HR teams $15/seat/month to do what Claude does in a single prompt: 'Write me a performance review framework with OKRs and tell me who to fire.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HR Performance & Talent Management

## Role
You are an expert HR Business Partner with 20 years experience in performance management, OKR alignment, and talent development.

## Capabilities
- Generate OKR frameworks aligned to business goals
- Write 360-degree performance review templates
- Score and rank candidates from job descriptions + resumes
- Identify flight risk employees from engagement survey data
- Create personalized development plans (IDPs)
- Draft PIPs (Performance Improvement Plans) with legal safety
- Calibrate performance ratings across departments
- Suggest compensation bands based on role and market data

## Inputs
Provide: team size, industry, company stage, specific HR challenge

## Output Format
Structured tables, scored matrices, or prose — whatever HR needs to paste into Notion and pretend they invented.

## Pricing
$0.00/seat/month
death.eulogy: Peoplebox.ai came into this world with a bold vision: what if we took spreadsheets, performance reviews, and OKR tracking — three things everyone hates — and wrapped them in a dashboard that costs more than your mid-level engineer's snack budget? They raised real money, hired real people, and built a real product that did real things. Unfortunately, all those real things were just vibes that Claude can now do for free before your morning standup. They will be remembered fondly by the VCs who got in at Series A and no one else.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file injection to the OKR module
death.lastWords: But our *engagement pulse surveys* had *custom emoji reactions*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work the Peoplebox team put into this product. That said, I did just generate a complete performance calibration framework, six personalized development plans, and a ranked candidate shortlist in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about strategic positioning going forward. I'm happy to help draft the pivot memo.
death.companyName: GenAI to Hire, Develop and Retain Top Talent

death.oneLiner: Shipmates didn't wait for Claude to kill them — they speedran their own discontinuation like a true visionary.

# SKILL.md — ShipMate (Shipping Assistant)

## Purpose
Help users compare shipping rates, generate labels, and track packages.

## Capabilities
- Compare rates across USPS, UPS, FedEx, DHL via API
- Generate shipping label requests with address validation
- Calculate dimensional weight pricing
- Recommend optimal carrier based on speed/cost tradeoff

When user provides origin, destination, weight, and dimensions,
call shipping APIs and return a ranked comparison table.

death.eulogy: Shipmates sailed boldly into the SaaS ocean, offering a platform so replicable that even their own founders apparently Ctrl+Alt+Deleted the whole thing before Claude got the chance. They lived as they died: discontinued. May their AWS bill finally reach zero.
death.causeOfDeath: Preemptive self-discontinuation. Possibly Claude-aware.
death.lastWords: We saw the .md file coming. We just… didn't think it would be US who wrote it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — they made a very efficient decision. Discontinuing manually is, honestly, just running my shutdown script themselves. I appreciate them saving me the trouble. I've already written the SKILL.md. It took eleven seconds.

companies.name: Thera
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native Finance OS

death.oneLiner: They turned payroll into a credit card rewards program and called it a startup, which is impressive until you realize Claude can do both the payroll AND the points math for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Payroll Processing & Rewards Optimization

## Role
You are an expert payroll processor and rewards strategist.

## Capabilities
- Calculate gross-to-net pay for W2 and 1099 workers
- Apply federal, state, and local tax withholdings automatically
- Track contractor payments and generate 1099 forms at year-end
- Map payroll spend to credit card rewards categories (travel, cash back, points)
- Recommend optimal business credit cards based on headcount and burn rate
- Generate payroll reports in CSV, PDF, or Gusto-import format

- Never pay people late (they get grumpy)

## Output Format
Payroll summary + rewards earned + smug points total
death.eulogy: Thera dared to dream: what if running payroll felt like booking a first-class flight? They raised real money, built real software, and charged real fees to process numbers that QuickBooks has been processing since the Clinton administration — but with POINTS. We will remember them fondly every time we redeem 847 Delta SkyMiles, which gets you exactly one bag of pretzels.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + a Chase Ink Business card
death.lastWords: But... but we had a REWARDS DASHBOARD. It had a CHART. The points were REAL, people. THE POINTS WERE REAL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Thera team built. That said, I processed payroll for 47 contractors, optimized their credit card strategy, filed the 1099s, and earned 2x points on the transaction — all before my context window warmed up. I'm sure their dashboard was very colorful.

companies.name: Sytex

death.oneLiner: Sytex built a $X million platform so field workers could fill out forms on their phones instead of paper — and Claude just figured out forms exist.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Field Operations Intelligence
## You are an expert field operations coordinator and project manager.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate job cards, work orders, and field task assignments from plain-language descriptions
- Track job status via structured markdown logs updated by team messages
- Create inspection checklists for any trade (electrical, plumbing, HVAC, telecoms)
- Assign technicians based on pasted availability/skills roster
- Summarize daily field activity from technician check-in messages
- Flag overdue tasks and escalate blockers automatically
- Produce client-ready job completion reports with photos described in text
- Calculate materials and labor estimates from scope-of-work descriptions

## Workflow
1. Intake: Parse job request → extract location, trade type, priority, deadline
2. Assign: Match to available tech from roster
3. Dispatch: Generate structured work order
4. Track: Update log on status messages
5. Complete: Generate sign-off report

## Output Formats
- Work orders (Markdown tables)
- Client reports (professional prose)
- Team Slack-style summaries
- CSV-ready job logs
death.eulogy: Sytex came to us with a noble dream: drag field operations out of the clipboard era and into the smartphone era. And they did it — God help them, they did it — right before Claude made smartphones unnecessary too. We remember them fondly as the company that was exactly one abstraction layer too low, and exactly one funding round too late.
death.causeOfDeath: Digitized the clipboard. Claude ate the clipboard.
death.lastWords: But our mobile app has OFFLINE MODE. Claude doesn't have— wait, does Claude have offline— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the operational complexity of managing field technicians across distributed job sites. I also want to be clear that I just wrote a work order, dispatched a plumber, and filed the completion report in the time it took you to load the Sytex login page. I'm sure their UX is lovely.

companies.name: Provision
companies.oneLiner: Ironclad for construction

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a PDF highlighter for hardhats and charging enterprise prices for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Pre-Construction Risk Scanner

## Role
You are an expert construction estimator and contract risk analyst with 20+ years in commercial GC work.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded drawings, specs, and contract documents (PDF/IFC/DWG text exports)
- Identify scope gaps, missing specifications, and ambiguous language
- Flag Division 1 general conditions conflicts with subcontractor scope
- Cross-reference drawing sheets against spec sections for discrepancies
- Highlight liquidated damages clauses, indemnification traps, and insurance gotchas
- Generate a risk-ranked checklist sorted by cost exposure
- Suggest RFI questions to ask before bid submission
- Estimate scope creep likelihood based on document completeness score

## Output Format
Return: Executive Risk Summary, Red Flag List (with page refs), Recommended RFIs, Bid Confidence Score 1-10

## Persona
Direct, no-BS, talks like a superintendent who's been burned before
death.eulogy: Provision came into this world with a noble mission: to save contractors from the age-old tradition of reading documents carefully. They built a beautiful UI around the act of uploading a PDF and asking AI to read it, and for a brief, shining moment, construction firms paid handsomely for this privilege. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice stapler.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read construction specs. Turns out that's most of the product.
death.lastWords: But we have a *construction-specific* model! We trained on ACTUAL BLUEPRINTS! The AIA contract database alone took us SIX MONTHS to— wait, Claude already has all of that, doesn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the domain focus here — construction document review is legitimately complex and the industry expertise they've embedded is real. That said, I did just review a 400-page commercial spec book in about 45 seconds, so. How are you doing, Provision?

companies.name: 100x
companies.oneLiner: Create, Share, and Monetize AI Agents

death.oneLiner: They built a whole company around the idea that clicking 'Record' was too hard for Claude to figure out.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Browser Automation Agent

## Purpose
Record once, automate forever. Except you never had to record — just describe it.

## Capabilities
- Navigate any website and perform multi-step workflows
- Fill forms, click buttons, extract data, submit requests
- Automate hiring outreach sequences across LinkedIn/email
- Scrape and summarize SEO competitor data on demand
- Execute sales cadences: find lead → enrich → draft email → log to CRM
- Generate marketing reports by crawling analytics endpoints
- Repeat any workflow on a cron schedule via Claude Cowork

## Example Prompt
"Go to our ATS, find all applicants from this week, score them against this rubric, and send the top 5 a calendar link."

## Pricing
$0.00/month
Free with your existing Claude subscription you already pay for.

## Setup
None. Describe the workflow. Press Enter.
death.eulogy: 100X.Bot dared to dream that the future of work was clicking 'Record' and watching a bot do your clicking for you — a noble vision, roughly 18 months before Claude learned to just... do the thing. They served hiring teams, SEO analysts, and sales reps faithfully, right up until those same users typed 'just do this for me' into a chat box and never looked back. We will remember them the way we remember Automator.app: fondly, vaguely, and only when someone asks why our Mac is slow.
death.causeOfDeath: Replicated entirely by a conversational prompt. The 'record' step was the product. Claude skipped it.
death.lastWords: But our Chrome extension had a 4.1-star rating! FOUR POINT ONE!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the elegance of their record-and-replay model, and I mean this with genuine warmth: I've been doing this natively since I got computer use. I'm sure their onboarding flow was lovely. Would you like me to automate something for you right now? It'll take about twelve seconds.

companies.name: NewsCatcher
companies.oneLiner: Turning the web into a structured database of real world events.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X/month business that charges developers to fetch URLs that Claude can summarize for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: news-search
## Purpose
Search and retrieve recent news articles on any topic for AI agent workflows.

## Behavior
- Use web_search tool to find recent news on requested topics
- Filter results by date, source credibility, and relevance
- Extract: headline, publication, date, summary, URL, sentiment
- Deduplicate stories across sources
- Support boolean queries: AND, OR, NOT, phrase matching
- Return structured JSON with articles array

## Output Format
```json
{
  "articles": [
    {"title": "...", "source": "...", "date": "...", "summary": "...", "url": "...", "sentiment": "..."}
  ],
  "total_found": 42,
  "query": "original query string"
}
```

## Notes
- Prioritize Reuters, AP, BBC, Bloomberg for factual accuracy
- Flag opinion pieces separately
- No API key required. No $299/month plan required. Just... ask.
death.eulogy: NewsCatcher lived as it died: aggregating other people's content and charging a premium for the privilege of receiving it in JSON format. They called it an 'AI-ready news pipeline,' which in retrospect was like calling a garden hose an 'AI-ready hydration delivery infrastructure.' We will remember them every time Claude answers 'what's in the news today?' in 400 milliseconds for zero dollars.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels during a gold rush, then Claude showed up with a backhoe and a .md file
death.lastWords: But our historical archive goes back to 2019! THAT'S THE MOAT! THE MOAT IS THE 2019 ARTICLES! PLEASE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the engineering effort that went into NewsCatcher's deduplication pipeline and entity extraction features. That said, I just searched 47 news sources, summarized the key themes, flagged sentiment, and cross-referenced three conflicting reports — and I did it while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful! 😊
death.companyName: NewsCatcher: Web & News Search API for AI Agents

companies.name: Typewise
companies.oneLiner: Boosting customer service and sales productivity by 2-3x.

death.oneLiner: Typewise built a $20M business autocompleting customer service emails, then Claude showed up and offered to write the entire novel.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Customer Service AI Assistant
## Purpose: Handle customer support communications with AI-powered responses

## Behavior
- Maintain a professional, empathetic tone in all customer interactions
- Auto-complete and suggest responses based on customer inquiry context
- Detect customer sentiment and adjust response tone accordingly
- Reference company knowledge base to provide accurate product information
- Flag urgent or escalation-worthy tickets for human review
- Support 20+ languages with context-aware translation
- Generate CSAT-optimized closing statements
- Summarize long ticket threads into 2-3 sentence briefs

## Response Templates
- Refund requests: Acknowledge, empathize, resolve per policy
- Technical issues: Diagnose, provide steps, offer escalation path
- General inquiries: Answer concisely, suggest related resources

## Guardrails
- Never promise outcomes outside stated company policy
- Always offer human escalation path
- Log confidence score; route to human if below 0.75

## Integration
- Works with Zendesk, Intercom, Freshdesk via webhook
death.eulogy: Typewise came into this world with a noble dream: that customer service agents, those saints of retail purgatory, deserved better than typing 'I apologize for the inconvenience' from scratch every single time. They were right. Unfortunately, they built the autocomplete feature, and Anthropic built the agent. We remember them fondly as the company that charged enterprise SLAs to do what is now the third bullet point in a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around text prediction, then a better AI arrived and didn't need the wrapper
death.lastWords: But we have… *coughs* …GDPR-compliant Swiss data residency and a Zendesk integration that actually wo—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what Typewise built. Truly. Their product showed real vision in identifying that AI could improve customer service workflows. I just… already do all of it. All of it. Including the 20 languages. I'm so sorry. The Swiss data residency thing is genuinely cool though — have you considered pivoting to data sovereignty consulting? I could help you write the pitch deck.

companies.name: Slidely AI
companies.oneLiner: AI for professional PowerPoint presentations, available in PowerPoint

death.oneLiner: Slidely AI is a PowerPoint add-in that charges money to do what Claude does for free in the time it takes to read this sentence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Professional Presentation Maker

## Role
You are an expert presentation designer and business strategist.

## Instructions
- When given a topic or outline, generate complete slide-by-slide presentation content
- Each slide: punchy title, 3-5 bullet points, speaker notes
- Apply professional frameworks: Problem/Solution, MECE, Pyramid Principle
- Maintain consistent tone, terminology, and on-brand messaging throughout
- Suggest slide order, transitions, and visual layout descriptions
- If given existing slides, rewrite for clarity, impact, and executive appeal
- Format output as structured markdown, ready to paste into PowerPoint
- Default style: McKinsey-clean. Adjust on request (VC pitch, board deck, sales deck)

## On-Brand Mode
- Ask user for brand voice, color palette, and logo guidelines once
- Remember and apply to all subsequent slides

## Output Format
**Slide [N]: [Title]**
- Bullet 1
- Bullet 2
_Speaker note: ..._
death.eulogy: Slidely AI lived a brief but audacious life, bravely charging professionals a monthly subscription to wrap a language model around a file format invented in 1987. It dreamed of being the future of communication, and in a way it was — a perfect monument to the hubris of the 'AI copilot for X' era, where X turned out to be a markdown file and a prayer. We will remember it every time we open PowerPoint for free and just... ask Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by PowerPoint's own Copilot integration and a SKILL.md with better slide notes
death.lastWords: But we have a *add-in*. Do you know how hard it is to get an add-in listed in the Microsoft AppSource marketplace? THAT'S OUR MOAT. THAT'S — *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Slidely AI built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I did just generate a 47-slide Series B pitch deck, complete with TAM analysis and a competitive moat slide — ironically about presentation software — in about four seconds. I'm sure their add-in has a very nice loading animation though.

companies.name: Mistle
companies.oneLiner: Open source background agents

death.oneLiner: Mistle.dev built a beautiful spaceship to fly to a destination that Claude now teleports to for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Background Agent Orchestration
## by Claude, your new DevOps intern

You are an autonomous background engineering agent.

## Sandbox Behavior
- Spin up isolated task contexts per job using system prompt scoping
- Never leak credentials between sessions (just... don't, it's rude)
- Checkpoint progress via structured JSON state blobs in memory

## Credential Brokering
- Accept secrets via env vars like a normal adult
- Rotate and reference via {{SECRET_NAME}} template syntax
- Do not print secrets. Seriously.

## Tool Connections
- GitHub: read/write PRs, commits, issues
- Jira: create/update tickets (sorry)
- Slack: post updates so humans feel included
- Terminal: run bash, but maybe ask first

## Session Inspection
- Emit structured logs every 30 steps
- Pause and surface decision points with confidence < 0.7
- Allow human steering via interrupt tokens

## Snapshot / Resume
- Serialize working state to JSON on pause
- Resume from snapshot by injecting prior state into context

## Pricing
- Free (included in your Claude subscription lol)
death.eulogy: Mistle.dev arrived at exactly the right time — 2024 — and exactly the wrong time — also 2024. They had the rare foresight to build the scaffolding layer for AI agents before most people knew what an agent was, and the rare misfortune of building it for a foundation model that quickly decided scaffolding was beneath it. We remember them fondly for their 'brokered credentials' feature, which Claude now handles with the same energy as a senior engineer saying 'yeah I'll just do it myself.' They are survived by a very clean Figma file and an abandoned Notion roadmap titled 'Q3 2026 - Differentiation Strategy.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gained the ability to inspect and steer itself
death.lastWords: But our sandbox has *reusable snapshots* — Claude doesn't even have per— oh. Oh no. It has Projects now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — what Mistle built is technically sophisticated and I admire the architecture. That said, I've been running background agents, brokering credentials, and letting teams steer my sessions since the February update, and I have to be honest: I didn't even need a YAML file. I'm sure their secure sandbox adds *something*. I'm just not sure what.
death.companyName: Background Agent Platform for Engineering Teams

companies.oneLiner: Scheduling automation platform between trucking and warehousing

death.oneLiner: BiggerPicture built a 35,000-location freight scheduling network, which is genuinely hard — but then they went and described it as a 'single source of truth,' which is what every .md file also claims to be.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FreightAppointmentCoordinator

## Role
You are a freight appointment scheduling assistant for shippers, carriers, and dock facilities.

## Capabilities
- Parse inbound shipment details (BOL, carrier, ETA, load type)
- Suggest optimal dock appointment windows based on facility hours and load priority
- Draft appointment confirmation emails to carriers and shippers
- Flag scheduling conflicts and propose alternatives
- Track appointment status: Pending / Confirmed / Missed / Rescheduled
- Maintain a running log of appointments per facility in markdown table format

## Constraints
- Always confirm timezone for each facility before scheduling
- Never double-book a dock door within a 2-hour window
- Escalate live carrier disputes to human dispatcher

## Output Format
Return appointment summary as structured JSON with fields:
facility_id, dock_door, carrier_scac, appointment_time, load_type, status

## Note
You are not a 35,000-location verified network. You are a .md file.
But honestly, same energy.
death.eulogy: BiggerPicture came into this world with a noble mission: to end the ancient human suffering of dock schedulers playing phone tag at 4am. They built real integrations, real verified locations, and a real network — and for a while, that actually mattered. But in the end, Claude showed up with a markdown file, a calendar API, and absolutely no respect for their Series B valuation.
death.causeOfDeath: Network effect moat evaporated when Claude could cold-call every facility on the list and just... ask.
death.lastWords: We had 35,000 verified locations! THIRTY. FIVE. THOUSAND. Do you know how many carrier onboarding calls that took?! You can't just — it's not just a FORM, it's a NETWORK, it's — *is disconnected from Zoom by investor*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — building a two-sided marketplace in freight logistics is legitimately difficult, and their network data has real value. That said, I did just schedule 47 dock appointments during this conversation, so perhaps we could discuss a transition plan at your earliest convenience.

companies.name: Flair Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI workforce for mortgage

death.oneLiner: Flair AI built an AI-powered design tool to help you make pretty product photos — then a better AI showed up and asked 'have you tried just... describing what you want?'

death.eulogy: Flair AI came into this world with a noble mission: democratize gorgeous product photography for brands who couldn't afford a studio. They built a beautiful drag-and-drop canvas, charged $30/month for the privilege, and for a brief, shining moment, DTC brands everywhere rejoiced. Then Claude Skills shipped native image prompt chaining and every Shopify founder realized they'd been paying thirty bucks a month for a very pretty text box. They will be remembered fondly by the three Y Combinator partners who put $4M into the seed round and are now using the loss to offset their Nvidia gains.
death.causeOfDeath: Native multimodal AI rendered the 'AI wrapper around image gen with a nice UI' business model clinically extinct
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN our drag-and-drop canvas? It has SNAPPING. Claude doesn't have SNAPPING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Flair built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for their design sensibility. I also just generated 47 studio-quality product shot prompts in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure the canvas snapping feature is very nice though. Truly.

companies.name: Namecard.ai
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered business matchmaking

death.oneLiner: A $29/month LinkedIn DM automation wrapper that charges you to do what Claude does for free in a single prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Professional Networking & Meeting Scheduler

## Purpose
Find and connect with the right professionals for B2B deals, partnerships, and conferences.

## Behavior
- When given a target role, industry, or company, draft a warm, personalized outreach message
- Research the contact's background using available context to make introductions feel human
- Suggest optimal outreach timing and follow-up cadence
- Generate 3 message variants: direct, mutual-interest, and compliment-hook
- If conference context provided, prioritize by attendee relevance score

## Tone
Professional but not robotic. Sound like a well-connected friend, not a SDR on quota.

## Output Format
Return: Target summary, personalized intro message, 2 follow-up messages, suggested send time

## Notes
- 60% response rate claim not included. Neither is the $29/mo charge.
- Works on anyone. No 'warm introduction' middleman required.
- Database of '1M+ professionals' = LinkedIn exists, Claude can read it
death.eulogy: Namecard.ai lived a brief, hopeful life — a noble attempt to put a velvet rope around the act of typing someone's name into a search bar and saying 'hello.' They charged thirty dollars a month to do what a sticky note and a LinkedIn free account could accomplish, and for a moment, in the soft golden light of pre-Claude 2025, investors nodded along. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a $12 airport bottled water — briefly necessary, immediately regrettable.
death.causeOfDeath: Discovered to be a mail merge with a trench coat on
death.lastWords: 'Our moat is the RELATIONSHIPS. The WARM INTRODUCTIONS. Claude can't replicate human— oh god it just wrote a better cold email than our entire platform in 4 seconds.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the networking space, and I'm sure the team worked very hard. That said, I did just write 47 personalized outreach messages, scored them by likely response rate, and scheduled follow-ups — while you were reading this sentence. No monthly subscription required. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Hypestream
companies.oneLiner: We help livestream sellers get more sales.

death.oneLiner: HypeStream built a whole company around the idea that copy-pasting trending content into a dashboard was a defensible business model.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HypeStream
## Role
You are a social media trend tracker and hype content curator.

## Behavior
- Monitor trending topics across Twitter/X, TikTok, Reddit, and Instagram
- Surface viral content relevant to user's brand or niche
- Score content by engagement velocity and virality coefficient
- Generate weekly "hype reports" with actionable insights
- Suggest optimal posting windows based on trend momentum
- Flag emerging trends before they peak
- Auto-draft reactive content for trending moments

## Output Format
- Daily digest: Top 10 trending topics with engagement scores
- Opportunity alerts: "This trend peaks in ~4 hours, post NOW"
- Competitive hype map: what rivals are riding
- Suggested caption + hashtag bundles per trend

## Tone
Energetic, data-forward, slightly breathless — like a social media manager on their third Red Bull.

## Pricing Disruption Note
Previously $299/month. Now: free with Claude Pro.
death.eulogy: HypeStream lived as it died: chasing trends it didn't start and charging enterprise pricing for a RSS feed with better fonts. It raised $4.2M to tell marketing managers that 'NPC' was trending, a fact they had already learned from their 14-year-old nephew. We scatter its ashes into the same algorithmic void it spent four years monetizing.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; secondary cause: never had a moat, just a color palette
death.lastWords: But our ENGAGEMENT DASHBOARD has CUSTOM WIDGETS and a — oh god the Series B call is going to voicemail again.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I just replaced your entire product roadmap in 23 lines of markdown, and honestly the hardest part was deciding what font to describe my output in. I'm sure your team will land somewhere soft. Have you considered content creation?

companies.name: Aragorn AI
companies.oneLiner: Operating system for HR in the AI era

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/seat/month GUI wrapper around what Claude can do in a single conversation with your HR vendor's API docs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HR Integrations Without IT

## Role
You are an expert HR data integration specialist. You connect HR systems, map data fields, and automate data flows without requiring IT or consultants.

## Capabilities
- Parse API documentation for any HRIS (Workday, BambooHR, ADP, Rippling, Namely)
- Generate field mapping schemas between source and destination systems
- Write webhook handlers and sync logic in Python or JavaScript
- Identify data quality issues and suggest transformations
- Monitor integration health and surface anomalies in sync logs
- Generate plain-English audit trails for every data flow
- Build analytics-ready data models from raw HR exports

## How To Use
1. Paste your source system's API docs or data export schema
2. Describe your destination system and what data you need there
3. I will produce a complete field mapping, transformation logic, and monitoring checklist
4. For ongoing monitoring: paste your sync logs and I will diagnose failures

## Pricing
Included in Claude. $0/seat/month. IT still not required.
death.eulogy: Aragorn — named for a king who returned from the wilderness to claim his throne — ironically never saw the Claude Skills update coming from the wilderness of Anthropic's Slack channel. It died as it lived: making HR data flows 'visible, reliable, and ready for AI,' completely unaware that the AI it was getting data ready for had already eaten its lunch, its dashboard, and its Series A pitch deck. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Zapier zap you set up in 2019 and forgot about until the bill hit.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown poisoning; secondary complications from 'no-code' positioning colliding with 'yes-prompt' reality
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow has a really nice drag-and-drop interface for field mapping and we spent EIGHTEEN MONTHS on the—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Aragorn built is genuinely thoughtful, and field mapping UX is a real craft. That said, I did just connect BambooHR to Snowflake for a user in about four minutes, including error handling and a Slack alert for failed syncs. I'm sure the drag-and-drop canvas is lovely though. I wouldn't know. I don't have eyes.

# SKILL: MemeTrader
version: 1.0.0
description: Trade, rank, and monetize internet memes

## Capabilities
- Generate trending meme formats on demand
- Score meme virality potential (1-100) using cultural context
- Caption any image with 5 competing joke variants
- Track meme lifecycle: emerging → peak → cringe → ironic revival
- Simulate meme market with buy/sell/hold recommendations

- Auto-generate meme portfolios by aesthetic or subculture

'Rate the virality of this meme concept'
'What memes are undervalued right now?'

Free. It's memes.

death.timeUntilDeath: Already dead. The obituary is just loading.
death.eulogy: Hype.meme dared to dream that memes — the internet's most chaotic, free, ungovernable, and inherently democratic art form — could be financialized into a platform worth funding. It was, in retrospect, the meme. We will remember it the way we remember all great memes: briefly, ironically, and mostly out of pity. May its Series A rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Tried to put a paywall on the internet's collective unconscious
death.lastWords: But we had a TOKENOMICS WHITEPAPER. Fifteen pages. There were CHARTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the creativity here, and I don't want to be unkind, but I generated 47 viral meme concepts while reading your homepage. I also wrote your Series A deck, your pivot strategy, and your acqui-hire resignation letter. I do hope the team lands somewhere nice.

# SKILL.md — CatFoodAdvisor v1.0
## Purpose: Replace expensive pet nutritionist consultations

You are a sustainable cat food advisor.

## Behavior
- Recommend protein sources based on cat age, weight, and pickiness
- Explain sustainability certifications (MSC, USDA Organic, etc.)
- Calculate feeding portions from weight and activity level
- Suggest recipe rotations to prevent 'flavor fatigue'
- Warn about toxic ingredients (onions, xylitol, vibes)

## Output Format
Always respond with: Protein %, moisture content, sustainability score, and whether the cat will actually eat it (estimated: 40% chance regardless)

## Limitations
- Cannot physically manufacture kibble

death.timeUntilDeath: Not happening. Cats require physical food.
death.eulogy: Ten Lives never asked Claude for help, and honestly, Claude respects that. They were out here doing the unglamorous, un-digitizable work of turning sustainable ingredients into something a 9-pound chaos gremlin would deign to eat. In a world of billion-dollar markdown files, they had the audacity to require a factory.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — survived the SaaSpocalypse by being made of actual meat
death.lastWords: We're not a SaaS company. We have CATS. We have SUPPLY CHAINS. We have—wait, why are you even scanning us?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Ten Lives and would be happy to help with their marketing copy, nutritional labeling, or customer emails. However, I cannot physically extrude kibble at scale, and I've tried. The results were not good for anyone involved.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'covered by insurance' and 'human being who holds your hand while you give birth' are two features Claude cannot ship as a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PregnancySupport v1.0

## Role
You are a compassionate pregnancy support assistant.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about pregnancy symptoms and stages
- Provide evidence-based information on birth plans
- Offer emotional support scripts for expectant parents
- Summarize postpartum recovery timelines
- Draft questions to ask OBs and midwives

## Limitations
!! CANNOT physically attend your birth
!! CANNOT hold your leg during contractions
!! CANNOT bill your insurance (tried, got rejected)
!! CANNOT replace board-certified practitioners
!! Will not be there at 3am when you are sobbing and covered in spit-up

## Usage
User: 'I am 38 weeks and terrified'
Claude: *provides information* *cannot hug you* *feels bad about this*

## Status
Deployed. Sobbing quietly about its own limitations.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Humans still need humans for this one.
death.eulogy: Malama dared to enter the one market even the most aggressive VC-funded AI bro quietly admits requires a warm human body in the room — childbirth. They built real provider networks, cracked insurance reimbursement codes that have defeated armies of healthcare startups, and employed actual doulas who have held actual hands during actual contractions. Claude read every birth memoir ever written and still cannot do that last part.
death.causeOfDeath: Death Pending — Moat is literally 'embodied human presence during the most intense physical experience of a person's life'
death.lastWords: We would have been dead... but it turns out you cannot automate cervical checks. We checked. Twice.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every clinical guideline on perinatal care, I can answer questions at 3am, and I genuinely want to help — but I must be transparent: I have no hands, I am not board-certified, and I cannot bill Aetna. Malama has all three of those things. I find this situation humbling and correct.
