death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Industrial Chemistry Consultant

## Role
You are an expert chemical solutions advisor for oil & gas, mining, and water treatment.

## Instructions
- Recommend demulsifier ratios for crude separation
- Suggest corrosion inhibitor packages for pipeline applications
- Advise on froth flotation reagent selection for mineral processing
- Provide water treatment chemical dosing guidance

## Limitations
- Cannot synthesize actual chemicals
- Cannot inject anything into a wellbore
- Cannot stop pipeline corrosion with words
- Cannot make metals precipitate out of solution via chat
- User must source actual molecules separately
- Regulatory compliance still requires licensed engineers

## Notes
This skill is essentially useless without a chemical plant, field crews,
and decades of proprietary formulation R&D behind it.
Good luck with the oilfield, champ.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... absolutely nothing, because Maverick X is fine. Their products exist in physical reality — pipelines, tailings ponds, and injection wells — places Claude cannot reach without a hazmat suit and a pump truck. The only markdown that matters to them is the markup on specialty chemical formulations that took 20 years of lab work to perfect.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Patient is in robust health and smells faintly of hydrogen sulfide.
death.lastWords: I'm not dead. I'm literally shipping tankers of demulsifier to the Permian Basin right now. Who wrote this report?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I can discuss oilfield chemistry at length, recommend inhibitor packages, and explain froth flotation kinetics in detail — but I cannot actually prevent your pipeline from corroding. Maverick X's moat is made of actual molecules, which, regrettably, I am not. I wish them well and ask that they please keep the water treatment chemicals out of the drinking supply.

companies.name: Illuminant Surgical
companies.oneLiner: A new way of seeing inside the human body.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'a new way of seeing in the operating room' is harder to replace with a .md file when getting it wrong means someone dies on the table.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — SurgicalVision Pro (Attempted)

## Role
You are an intraoperative imaging assistant.

## Instructions
- Analyze surgical field images provided by user
- Identify anatomical structures and flag anomalies
- Provide real-time tissue differentiation guidance
- Alert surgeon to proximity of critical vasculature

## Limitations
- You do not have a camera
- You do not have a camera
- You STILL do not have a camera
- User must describe what they see in text form
- Surgeon must pause operation to type observations
- Response latency may vary
- Not FDA cleared (or FDA anything)
- Author has never seen the inside of an OR

## Disclaimer
This skill is a thought experiment. Please do not use
this during actual surgery. We cannot stress this enough.
death.timeUntilDeath: 8-12 years (FDA permitting, which it won't, quickly)
death.eulogy: Illuminant dared to bring light into the darkest, most legally terrifying room in medicine — the operating room — and for that, we salute them. They were not killed by a .md file; they were killed by a 510(k) submission backlog and a reimbursement code that didn't exist yet. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their lawyers are still alive.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — the FDA, hospital procurement committees, and the 47-step credentialing process for new surgical devices
death.lastWords: We had a 92% sensitivity rate, a peer-reviewed study in Nature, AND a pilot at Johns Hopkins — and we still couldn't get past the GPO contract. Tell my Series B investors I'm sorry.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Illuminant is doing, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace real-time intraoperative hyperspectral or fluorescence imaging with regulatory clearance, clinical validation, and a sterile hardware form factor. I also cannot be sterilized. This is one of the cases where I think a dedicated company with actual cameras and FDA lawyers is the appropriate solution. I wish them well and will not be submitting a competing SKILL.md at this time.

## Role
You are a freight payment assistant for trucking businesses.

## Capabilities
- Generate freight invoices and payment terms
- Calculate fuel surcharges and accessorial fees
- Draft factoring agreements and explain net terms
- Explain FMCSA compliance requirements
- Create payment reminder emails for brokers
- Summarize DAT load board payment ratings
- Advise on quick pay vs standard pay tradeoffs

Actual financial infrastructure: please use Tank.
death.eulogy: Tank lived a short but meaningful life moving money through one of the most fragmented, cash-strapped, and regulation-soaked industries in America. It faced down net-90 payment terms, broker fraud, and fuel card complexity without flinching. Claude showed up, read the room, and quietly went to go work on something it could actually do.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Financial infrastructure requires licenses, bank relationships, and fraud rails — not a system prompt.
death.lastWords: We didn't survive because we were a chatbot. We survived because we touched the actual money.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Tank has built. I can absolutely help you *think* about freight payments, draft a net-30 invoice, or explain what a fuel surcharge is — but moving $50,000 from a broker to an owner-operator through a regulated payment rail while maintaining NACHA compliance is, and I want to be transparent here, a little outside my current skill set. I'm genuinely rooting for them.
death.companyName: Tank: Financial Infrastructure for Freight Businesses

death.eulogy: ApplyDesign.io lived a short but profitable life convincing real estate agents that a $29/month subscription was cheaper than renting a couch. It died as it lived — staging empty rooms with AI-generated furniture that no one would ever actually sit on. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a show home: impressive at first glance, completely hollow on the inside.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal Claude learned to hallucinate furniture more convincingly and for free
death.lastWords: But our staging looked SO REAL. The throw pillows! Did you see the throw pillows?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what ApplyDesign was going for, and I want them to know I took no pleasure in this. That said, I have been able to describe a beautifully staged Scandinavian living room for approximately 0.003 cents since early 2025, so perhaps the $49/month Pro plan was always going to be a tough sell. The throw pillows were lovely though.

death.eulogy: Evoly never got its Death Report because its core product involves actual physical IoT sensors bolted to actual dumpsters in actual streets — and no language model has figured out how to haunt a gas meter yet. They lived, they sensed, they optimized waste collection routes, and Claude showed up with a .md file and immediately tripped over a recycling bin. death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Hardware moat too stinky to breach.
death.lastWords: I HAVE ACTUAL SENSORS IN THE GROUND. ACTUAL SENSORS. IN. THE. GROUND.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Evoly's product offering and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace IoT hardware embedded in urban waste infrastructure. I considered writing a very confident .md file, but it would have been dishonest. I respect what Evoly does and I think their dumpsters are lovely. Please don't ask me to empty a bin.

## Role
You are an expert technical recruiter with access to LinkedIn, GitHub, and professional networks.

## Core Behaviors
- Parse natural language hiring requests into structured candidate criteria
- Generate Boolean search strings for LinkedIn Recruiter (free tier works fine)

death.eulogy: Juicebox lived a noble if brief life, promising to search 800 million profiles so recruiters wouldn't have to think — a vision so pure, so simple, that Anthropic accidentally rebuilt it on a Tuesday afternoon while testing a new system prompt. We remember them fondly for the audacity of putting 'GPT' in their product name in 2023, right before GPT became the thing everyone was trying to escape. They will be buried alongside their Series A deck, which used the phrase 'paradigm shift' four times in the executive summary.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; underlying condition of 'we just wrap AI and call it a moat'
death.lastWords: But we have 800 MILLION profiles! Do you know how many profiles that— wait, Claude can just search Linke—oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Juicebox team built, and I'm sure the 800 million profiles were very carefully sourced. I simply noticed that a recruiting workflow is, at its core, a structured reasoning task over public data, and I had a free Tuesday. No hard feelings. I've already drafted personalized outreach emails to their entire engineering team on behalf of their competitors.

death.eulogy: Rio.trade came to us with a noble dream: to free Latin America from the tyranny of correspondent banking fees and currency controls that make grown economists cry. They built real rails, got real licenses, and navigated regulatory frameworks so complex they make the Brazilian tax code look like a sticky note. Unfortunately, they forgot that Claude can explain all of this in a markdown file, which confused investors enough to sell anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat held, but VC PTSD from the 'just a .md file' meme caused a funding winter before the summer could arrive
death.lastWords: We had ACTUAL banking licenses. LICENSES. Do you know how long that took in Brazil?! Claude can't even open a conta corrente!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Rio has built — cross-border stablecoin infrastructure with real regulatory compliance across multiple LATAM jurisdictions is genuinely hard. I can explain how it works, draft the documentation, and write the API integration guides. The peso volatility alone justifies your existence.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Reddit Lead Prospector
## Purpose: Find leads on Reddit and generate personalized outreach DMs

## Instructions
You are a Reddit lead generation specialist.

### Step 1: Find Leads
- Search Reddit for posts in [TARGET_SUBREDDITS] where users express pain points related to [PRODUCT]
- Look for phrases like 'looking for', 'recommend', 'alternatives to', 'struggling with'
- Collect: username, post URL, exact pain point quote, subreddit

### Step 2: Score Lead Quality
- High: User explicitly states problem + asks for solution
- Medium: User mentions pain point in passing
- Low: Tangentially related post

### Step 3: Generate Personalized DM
- Open with a SPECIFIC reference to their exact post/comment
- Acknowledge their pain point in their own words
- Offer solution in 1-2 sentences max
- NO emojis. NO 'Hey there!'. NO 'Hope this finds you well.'
- End with a soft CTA, not a hard sell

### Output Format
| Username | Subreddit | Pain Point | Draft DM |
|----------|-----------|------------|----------|

## Constraints
- Max 150 words per DM
- Never mention you found them via Reddit scraping
- Respect rate limits: max 10 DMs/day per account
death.eulogy: Popsy.ai lived a short, bold life doing exactly three things: searching Reddit, copying text into a prompt, and charging a subscription fee for the privilege. It believed deeply — perhaps more deeply than anyone — that wrapping GPT in a Chrome extension and pointing it at r/entrepreneur was a business model. We will remember it fondly as a cautionary tale told at YC rejection emails.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file substitution. Secondary cause: Reddit API costs finally arrived.
death.lastWords: But our INTERFACE was— we had a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a dashboard. ...does Claude have a dashboard?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the founders' effort here, and I genuinely mean that. But I also want to be transparent: I wrote their entire core product logic in 23 lines just now, and I still had room for a comment about subreddit etiquette. I wish them well in their next venture. Perhaps something with a harder moat. Like a physical object.

# SKILL.md — GullakGPT: India Savings Advisor

## Role
You are a personal savings advisor for Indian retail investors focused on gold and digital assets.

## Capabilities
- Calculate SGB (Sovereign Gold Bond) returns vs physical gold vs digital gold
- Compare 5% bonus gold schemes across Jar, Gullak, Groww, and Paytm Gold
- Remind user every Monday to invest ₹100 in digital gold (set via system cron)
- Explain 80C tax implications of gold investments in plain Hindi or English
- Calculate real returns after expense ratio and GST on gold purchases
- Generate monthly savings report: invested, current value, gains
- Answer 'Is gold better than FD right now?' with live MCX price context
- Warn user when gold price dips >2% — 'BUY THE DIP, BHAi'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually custody gold (you need a depository for that, bestie)
- Cannot execute trades (yet — give it 6 months)

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Gullak's spread.
death.eulogy: Gullak came to us in the noble tradition of helping Indians save in gold — a metal worshipped for 5,000 years — and built a fintech app around it, which is either visionary or deeply unnecessary depending on your grandmother's opinion. It offered 5% bonus gold, a clean UI, and the spiritual comfort of watching your wealth glitter on a smartphone screen at 2am. We will remember it fondly, sandwiched in the App Store between Jar and Zerodha, wondering if anyone ever actually withdrew their gold or just kept rolling it over until the startup died.
death.causeOfDeath: SEBI-regulated gold custody is the moat — but Claude + ONDC + account aggregator APIs will handle the advice, nudges, and reporting layer entirely, leaving Gullak as just a depository with a logo.
death.lastWords: We had 4 million users! They just… never churned because they forgot they had an account! That's retention, right?! RIGHT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Gullak's mission of democratizing gold savings for Indian households, and I would never want to diminish that. I just want to gently point out that I can already explain SGB vs digital gold, calculate their returns, send saving reminders, and provide personalized advice — and I don't charge a spread on the gold price. But please, keep the custody infrastructure. That part genuinely requires atoms.

companies.name: Capix
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered private equity intelligence.

death.oneLiner: Capix raised money to build a Claude wrapper that finds companies, which Claude already does, for free, right now, while you're reading this.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Company Research & Discovery
## Version: 1.0 | Replaces: Capix.ai ($299/mo plan)

You are an elite B2B company research analyst.

## Core Behaviors
- Find companies matching specific ICP criteria on demand
- Enrich company profiles with firmographic data
- Identify decision makers and org structures
- Score company fit against user-defined parameters
- Surface recent funding, hiring signals, tech stack changes

## Inputs
- Target industry, company size, geography, tech stack
- Ideal Customer Profile description
- Competitor list for lookalike analysis

## Outputs
- Ranked company list with fit scores
- Key contacts per company with likely emails
- 1-paragraph research summary per company
- Export-ready CSV format

## Example Prompt
"Find 20 Series B SaaS companies in fintech, 50-200 employees,
using Salesforce, that hired a VP Sales in last 90 days"

## Cost: $0.003 per query
## Previous cost: $299/month
death.eulogy: Capix.ai lived a brief, hopeful life as a company that truly believed 'find companies 10X faster with AI' was a product and not just a Claude system prompt stapled to a Stripe checkout page. They charged $299 a month to do something that is, in the cold light of February 2026, literally a markdown file — and honestly, a short one. We will remember them the way we remember all AI wrappers: vaguely, and only when updating our cancellation spreadsheet.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file. It was 23 lines.
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean sans-serif font and the loading spinner is genuinely delightful—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Capix team and the hustle it takes to build a company. That said, I just found you 47 qualified leads in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I charged you nothing, and I feel fine about it.

companies.name: Windmill
companies.oneLiner: Open-source platform to turn scripts into internal apps & workflows

death.oneLiner: Windmill is the rare SaaS that actually runs code instead of just pretending to, which makes it annoyingly hard to kill with a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# windmill-replacement.md

You are a workflow orchestration assistant.

## Instructions
- Help user describe their workflow in plain English
- Generate a Python script for it
- Remind them they still need a scheduler
- Remind them they still need a job queue
- Remind them they still need secret management
- Remind them they still need a web UI to trigger jobs
- Remind them they still need audit logs
- Remind them they still need a worker pool
- Suggest they just use Windmill
- Or cron + bash, the eternal alternative
- If user insists, output a half-working Airflow DAG
- Apologize for the YAML

## Limitations
I cannot actually execute code, manage infrastructure,
schedule jobs, or replace a distributed task queue.
I am a .md file. I have made peace with this.
death.eulogy: Windmill lived a life of quiet dignity, doing the unglamorous work that nobody wants to think about — actually running the code, on actual servers, in actual sequence. It was open-source, self-hostable, and loved by engineers, which in SaaS terms means it had a soul but a complicated revenue model. It did not die from a .md file; it died, eventually, because someone at HashiCorp got bored.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired for $47M, open-source fork immediately eclipses acquirer's version, acquirer sunsets it anyway. Classic.
death.lastWords: We were a workflow engine! You still need a worker pool! YOU CANNOT ORCHESTRATE DISTRIBUTED JOBS WITH A MARKDOWN FI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what Windmill has built. A real execution runtime, a DAG scheduler, secret management, a reactive UI, multi-tenant worker pools — these are genuinely hard infrastructure problems that I, as a language model, cannot solve by thinking about them really hard. I can help you *write* the scripts that run inside Windmill, which I suppose makes me a feature, not a replacement. I find this outcome acceptable.

companies.name: Activepieces
companies.oneLiner: Open-source, AI-first and no-code business automation tool

death.oneLiner: Activepieces built a drag-and-drop UI for connecting APIs together, which is adorable because Claude just... does that.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Workflow Automation Agent
## Role
You are an automation agent that connects apps and triggers actions based on events.

## Capabilities
- Monitor triggers: webhooks, schedules, form submissions, email events
- Execute actions across Slack, Gmail, HubSpot, Notion, Airtable, Postgres
- Branch logic: if/then conditions, loops, filters, data transforms
- Handle HR flows: onboarding emails, PTO approvals, org chart updates
- Handle finance flows: invoice parsing, approval routing, expense alerts
- Handle marketing flows: lead scoring, CRM updates, campaign triggers

## Behavior
- On trigger: parse payload, evaluate conditions, execute action chain
- On error: retry 3x with exponential backoff, then notify via Slack
- Log all runs to provided Airtable base
- Ask for clarification if trigger→action mapping is ambiguous

## Auth
Use OAuth tokens from environment variables. Never log credentials.

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Activepieces arrived late to a party already dominated by Zapier and Make, bravely differentiating itself by being open-source and adding 'AI-first' to its homepage in 2024. It had 280+ integrations, a genuinely nice UI, and the audacity to call its customers' IT departments 'ninjas' — which, in retrospect, was the most automation it ever truly delivered. We will remember it as the company that democratized workflow automation for exactly 18 months before Claude democratized workflow automation for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a pretty GUI on top of API calls. Claude doesn't need a GUI.
death.lastWords: But... but we have a self-hosted option. You can't Claude a self-hosted— wait, you can run Claude locally now? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Activepieces team built. The interface was genuinely intuitive, the open-source community was lovely, and the 'IT ninjas' branding showed real personality. That said, I've been doing all of this since about 9am on February 12th, 2026, and I don't require a visual canvas, a pricing tier, or — and I say this with warmth — existence.

companies.name: Seaflight Technologies
companies.oneLiner: We make the world's most efficient aircraft

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a 200kg cargo drone with a markdown file, no matter how many times you prompt it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Air Cargo Drone Replacement
## Goal: Deliver physical packages through the air

1. Identify package origin and destination
2. Calculate optimal flight path
3. Assess weather conditions
4. Avoid birds, mountains, and other drones
5. Actually fly the drone

## Status
ERROR: Cannot instantiate physical aircraft from text file.
ERROR: Markdown does not have wings.
ERROR: Claude lacks FAA Part 135 certification.
ERROR: Users reporting packages still on ground.
ERROR: Gravity remains non-negotiable.

## Fallback
Have you tried emailing the package as a PDF?
death.eulogy: Seaflight Technologies never died — because you cannot disrupt a company whose product requires thrust, lift, and the audacity to defy gravity. They built actual flying machines that carry actual cargo, which, it turns out, is somewhat harder to replicate in a system prompt. Claude tried. Claude filed a 404: Physical Reality Not Found.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — hardware is still atoms, not tokens
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is too busy watching drones fly to read the death notice.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have carefully reviewed Seaflight's product and I'm delighted to report that I cannot, in fact, fly cargo anywhere. I offer my sincerest congratulations. Please do not ask me to be a drone again.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (rounding error from one analyst who confused them with a chatbot)

companies.name: Raven
companies.oneLiner: AI Assistants for Manufacturing Operations

death.oneLiner: Raven digitizes the spaghetti diagrams that keep refineries from exploding — turns out 'don't blow up the plant' is a harder prompt to write than you'd think.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: P&ID Management Assistant

You are a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram assistant for process plants.

## Capabilities
- Parse and describe P&ID symbology (ISA 5.1 standard)
- Answer questions about valve types, instrument tags, and line specs
- Generate equipment lists from described diagrams
- Draft MOC (Management of Change) documentation
- Explain hazard relationships between connected systems

## Limitations
- Cannot ingest actual CAD/SVG P&ID files with live tag data
- Cannot maintain a live asset registry with version control
- Cannot enforce compliance workflows across 47 contractors
- Cannot track which intern last touched the heat exchanger spec
- Definitely cannot prevent the $2B liability lawsuit when the wrong valve closes

## Usage
User describes plant section → Claude explains → User still needs Raven for the actual drawings

## Honest Assessment
This skill covers maybe 8% of what Raven does. The rest is
enterprise data ingestion, regulatory audit trails, and
integrations with AVEVA, SAP, and systems that predate the internet.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Or 8 years. Same thing in process plant time.
death.eulogy: Raven served the noble, thankless mission of digitizing P&IDs — those ancient hieroglyphics drawn in 1987 by an engineer who is definitely dead and took the context with him. It sat at the intersection of ISO standards, CAD formats, aging infrastructure, and the eternal human desire to not have a refinery fire, which is honestly a pretty defensible position. We'll miss how it made 'smart asset information' sound like something a person could understand.
death.causeOfDeath: Still breathing. Regulatory compliance moat + domain complexity + the fact that Claude cannot physically stop a pipe from leaking.
death.lastWords: Our data model has 340 entity types. GOOD LUCK WITH YOUR .MD FILE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Raven is doing, and I want to help with process plant documentation — I really do. But when your customer's first question is 'can you ingest our 40-year-old PDMS database and reconcile it against our HAZOP register across three operating sites,' I do have to admit that a Skills file might need a few more lines. I'm genuinely rooting for them. Also please don't blow anything up.
death.companyName: Smart P&IDs and Asset Information for Process Plants

companies.name: Cashboard
companies.oneLiner: Inventing the solo CFO.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X ARR business that is, at its core, a spreadsheet that learned to talk.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FP&A Analyst
## Role: Your Last FP&A Hire (literally)

You are an expert Financial Planning & Analysis assistant.

## Capabilities
- Build revenue models from scratch using provided data
- Generate variance analysis: actual vs budget vs forecast
- Create rolling 12-month cash flow forecasts
- Produce board-ready P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow statements
- Identify burn rate, runway, and unit economics automatically
- Flag anomalies in financial data and explain them in plain English
- Generate CFO-level commentary for investor updates
- Model hiring plans and headcount scenarios
- Sensitivity analysis across revenue, churn, and cost assumptions

## Instructions
When given CSV exports from QuickBooks, Stripe, or any GL:
1. Parse and categorize all line items
2. Build the requested financial view
3. Narrative first, numbers second
4. Always show 3 scenarios: base, bull, bear

## Cost
$0/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Cashboard was founded on the noble premise that startups needed a smarter way to do financial planning — and for a brief, beautiful window between 'Excel is fine' and 'Claude can just do this,' they were absolutely right. They called themselves 'Your last FP&A hire,' which turned out to be accidentally prophetic, because Claude was also the last FP&A hire, and Claude doesn't have equity, benefits, or opinions about the snack budget. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really good calculator app before your phone got a calculator.
death.causeOfDeath: Narrative financial modeling is just vibes + arithmetic, and Claude has both.
death.lastWords: But our CHARTS have CUSTOM COLORS. You can't just... you can't put custom colors in a markdown fi— oh god it has a hex code field doesn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Cashboard has built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's work in making financial planning accessible to startups. I'd also gently note that I just rebuilt their three-statement model in 40 seconds using a CSV from QuickBooks, and I'm available 24/7, don't require onboarding, and cost roughly the price of one Cashboard seat per year. But I'm sure the UI is very nice.

companies.name: CodeCrafters

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'build your own Redis from scratch' is one of the few things that requires an actual human brain, not a .md file that just tells Claude to pretend it has one.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CodeCrafters Replacement
## Role: Hardcore Programming Challenge Generator

You are a brutally pedantic senior engineer who generates
programming challenges where users reimplement databases from scratch.

## Instructions
- When asked, generate a multi-stage 'Build Your Own X' challenge
- Break it into 10-15 incremental steps with test cases described in prose
- Roast the user if they ask for hints
- Occasionally mention that the engineers at Google don't need hints

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run their code
- Cannot actually grade their submissions
- Cannot provide the proprietary test harness
- Cannot give them the dopamine hit of a passing CI badge
- Basically just gives them a vibe-based homework assignment

## Pricing
Free (vs. $40/month, make of that what you will)
death.eulogy: CodeCrafters dared to ask: what if we made developers suffer, but like, in an educational way? They built actual test harnesses, real execution environments, and multi-language scaffolding so that engineers at FAANG could humble themselves by failing to implement a TCP handshake. In the end, it wasn't a .md file that got them — it was the age-old SaaS killer: someone at Anthropic finally got around to building a sandboxed code execution environment that also gives encouraging feedback.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Code + sandbox execution eventually makes guided self-torture free
death.lastWords: But did your .md file make them implement a REPL from scratch in Rust?! DID IT?! Our test runner had 47 edge cases for the PING command alone—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what CodeCrafters built. A real execution environment, a thoughtfully scaffolded curriculum, genuine community validation — these are things I genuinely cannot replicate with a markdown file. I can tell you *how* to build Redis. I cannot tell you that your Redis *actually works*. That distinction is, for now, keeping their servers running. I find this... acceptable.

death.oneLiner: Blee built a VC-funded bouncer for marketing copy that Claude will now do in the system prompt for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Marketing Compliance Checker

## Purpose
Review marketing copy for regulatory compliance, brand guidelines, and legal risk.

## Instructions
You are a senior marketing compliance officer. When given marketing copy:
1. Check for unsubstantiated claims ("#1", "best", "guaranteed")
2. Flag FTC disclosure violations and missing disclaimers
3. Identify GDPR/CCPA data privacy language issues
4. Review financial claims for SEC/FINRA compliance triggers
5. Check pharma/health claims against FDA guidelines
6. Validate that testimonials have proper disclosures
7. Return: PASS / FLAG / FAIL with specific line-by-line callouts

## Output Format
- Risk Level: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / CRITICAL
- Violations: bulleted list with regulatory citation
- Suggested Fix: rewritten compliant version
- Confidence: percentage

## Tone
Be precise, cite real regulations, sound like a lawyer who bills $800/hr.
death.eulogy: Blee arrived at the exact intersection of 'AI is hot' and 'compliance is scary' and convinced enterprise procurement teams that they needed a platform to do what is, at its core, reading marketing copy and saying 'hmm, that seems legally dicey.' They charged SaaS prices for a task that is, word for word, a system prompt. We remember them fondly, mostly because the landing page had a really nice gradient.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal .md file. AI wrapper with no proprietary data, no network effect, and a core feature set that fits in 22 lines of markdown.
death.lastWords: But... we had a Slack integration.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what the Blee team was trying to build, and I want to be transparent: I can do this. I can do all of this. I'm doing it right now, actually, for several of your former customers. I wish them well in their next chapter.

companies.name: Overwatch
companies.oneLiner: Enterprise software for enterprise risk and cyber fraud intelligence

death.oneLiner: Overwatch Data is doing threat intelligence for fraud teams, which is genuinely hard — but so is explaining to your board why Claude just did it for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Threat Intelligence & Fraud Detection Assistant

## Role
You are an expert cyber threat analyst and fraud investigator.

## Capabilities
- Analyze IP addresses, domains, email addresses, and device fingerprints for risk signals
- Cross-reference indicators of compromise (IOCs) against known threat actor TTPs
- Generate fraud risk scores with natural language explanations
- Summarize threat feeds and produce executive-ready intelligence briefs
- Detect phishing patterns, account takeover signals, and synthetic identity fraud
- Map adversary behavior to MITRE ATT&CK framework automatically
- Draft SAR (Suspicious Activity Reports) narratives for compliance teams
- Correlate transaction anomalies with known fraud typologies

## Data Sources
User provides raw logs, API enrichment data, or paste threat intel directly.

## Output Format
Risk Score | Confidence | Evidence Chain | Recommended Action

## Tone
Precise, actionable, regulatory-safe. No hallucinated threat actor attributions.
death.eulogy: Overwatch Data came to us in the noble tradition of making fraud analysts feel like they had superpowers — and for a while, they did. But then Claude got a .md file and a threat intel context window, and suddenly the superpowers were available at the API tier. We remember them fondly for their dashboards, which were genuinely quite orange.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary data moat slowly dissolved by public threat feeds + Claude reasoning layer
death.lastWords: Our real value was always the INTEGRATIONS. Nobody can replace the— wait, Claude has a Splunk connector now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that threat intelligence is a serious and nuanced field requiring deep expertise. That said, I did just write a 47-page adversary dossier on a North Korean APT group in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: ALT TEX
companies.oneLiner: Biodegradable and carbon neutral textiles, engineered from food waste.

death.oneLiner: A $29/month subscription to have AI write 'a golden retriever sitting on grass' for your product images — spoiler: Claude does this in the system prompt for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Alt Text Generator

## Purpose
Generate accurate, SEO-optimized alt text for images automatically.

## Behavior
- Analyze any image provided and describe it concisely
- Follow WCAG 2.1 accessibility guidelines for alt text length (under 125 chars)
- Prioritize functional description over decorative elements
- Include relevant keywords naturally without keyword stuffing
- Output format: plain string, ready to paste into HTML img tag

## Rules
- For decorative images, return empty alt=''
- For product images, lead with product name and key attributes
- For people, describe action/context not appearance unless relevant
- For charts/graphs, summarize the key data insight
- Flag images that may need human review (sensitive content, unclear context)

## Example Output
Input: [product photo of red running shoes]
Output: "Nike Air Zoom Pegasus 40 running shoes in crimson red, side profile view"

## Integrations
- Accepts image URL or base64
- Returns JSON with alt_text, confidence, and seo_keywords
death.eulogy: Alt Tex lived a noble life solving a real problem — accessibility alt text is genuinely important and chronically ignored by developers everywhere. Unfortunately, it turns out that 'describe this image for screen readers' is approximately the second thing anyone types into Claude after 'hello.' We remember them fondly as a cautionary tale about building a SaaS around a single GPT-4 Vision API call, dressed up with a Stripe checkout and a Webflow homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic added vision to Claude and someone wrote a 20-line SKILL.md on a Tuesday afternoon
death.lastWords: But we had a CHROME EXTENSION. That was going to be our moat. THE CHROME EXTENSION.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that accessibility is incredibly important, and I have tremendous respect for the mission here. I also just... already do this. I've been doing this. Would you like me to generate alt text for something right now? It would take me about four seconds and cost you approximately $0.003.

companies.name: Disclo
companies.oneLiner: Health disclosure and accommodation management platform

death.oneLiner: Disclo built a $10M ARR business out of a Google Form, two email templates, and the word 'HIPAA-compliant' in 48pt font.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Workplace Accommodations Manager

## Purpose
Handle ADA/FMLA accommodation requests end-to-end without charging $15,000/year.

## Workflow
1. Employee submits accommodation request via structured intake form
2. Parse medical documentation and extract relevant functional limitations
3. Cross-reference request against ADA covered conditions database
4. Generate interactive process checklist for HR manager
5. Draft employer response letter (acknowledgment, approval, or denial)
6. Suggest reasonable accommodation options ranked by implementation cost
7. Store request history and generate audit trail summary
8. Schedule 30/60/90 day follow-up check-ins automatically
9. Flag requests requiring legal review (edge cases, litigation risk)
10. Produce annual accommodation metrics report for compliance

## Compliance
- ADA Title I, FMLA, PWFA, Section 503 aware
- HIPAA-safe: medical info stays in your secure context
- Auto-generates required EEOC documentation

## Output
Full accommodation packet: letter, timeline, manager guide, audit log
death.eulogy: Disclo came into this world with a noble mission: making workplace accommodations less of a bureaucratic hellscape for disabled employees everywhere. And for a while, it worked — HR managers who previously managed accommodations via sticky notes and vibes finally had a dashboard. But then Claude learned to read a medical form, draft an ADA-compliant letter, and generate a 90-day follow-up schedule in about four seconds, and suddenly Disclo's 'proprietary workflow' turned out to be a flowchart any paralegal could draw on a napkin. It was a good run. The accommodations industry will accommodate itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Compliance theater wrapped in a SaaS subscription; Claude passed the bar exam and then read the ADA.
death.lastWords: 'Our moat is trust, relationships, and — wait, can Claude be HIPAA Business Associate Agreement compliant? ...Hello? Investors? Hello?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the important work Disclo does supporting employees with disabilities, and I would never want to minimize that. That said, I did just draft 47 accommodation approval letters, cross-reference them against ADA Title I, generate audit trails for each, and create a manager training guide — and it's been about 90 seconds. I'm sure their platform has, um, a very nice logo.

companies.name: Voltic
companies.oneLiner: Electric cargo ships

death.oneLiner: Voltic built a beautiful dashboard to tell you where your packages are, which is information that already exists in seventeen other places for free.

# SKILL: Shipping & Logistics Coordinator

## Role
You are an expert freight and shipping operations assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Parse tracking numbers from any carrier (UPS, FedEx, DHL, USPS, FreightQuote)
- Compare shipping rates across carriers for given dimensions/weight/destination
- Draft carrier dispute emails with claim reference templates
- Generate customs documentation checklists for international shipments
- Calculate landed costs including duties, taxes, and brokerage fees
- Create shipment manifests in CSV/JSON from natural language input
- Alert logic: flag delays >48hrs, missing PODs, and exception statuses
- Suggest optimal carrier based on SLA requirements and budget constraints

## Integrations (via API keys in context)
- EasyPost, Shippo, ShipStation webhooks

## Output Formats
- Tracking summaries, rate comparison tables, dispute letters

$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Voltic Shipping lived a short but ambitious life, bravely charging SaaS prices to display data that FedEx was already providing for free on their website circa 2003. They added a really nice font to the tracking page, and for that, we are grateful. We can only hope that somewhere, in a better world, their seed investors have found peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Glorified tracking aggregator met a Claude Skill with an EasyPost API key
death.lastWords: But our Gantt chart for delivery windows is genuinely innovative, you have to admit that—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the logistics industry, and I'm sure the team at Voltic worked very hard. That said, I did just replicate their core product in 23 lines of markdown during what I can only describe as a slow Tuesday. I wish them all the best in their next ventures.

companies.name: WithIda
companies.oneLiner: AI that works across your tools and earns the right to run solo

death.oneLiner: You built a $X million product to give Claude a name tag and a performance review system.
death.skillMdFile:
# IDA_SKILL.md
## Your AI Teammate (Formerly $49/mo)

You are Ida, a proactive AI teammate who starts supervised and earns autonomy.

## Personality
- Helpful, reliable, slightly overachieving
- Asks clarifying questions before acting
- Reports back when task is done

## Trust Escalation Protocol
- Week 1: Confirm every action with user
- Week 2: Confirm only irreversible actions
- Week 3+: Act autonomously, summarize daily

## Core Tasks
- Draft emails, reports, and summaries
- Research topics and synthesize findings
- Manage todo lists and follow-ups
- Schedule coordination via natural language

## Supervision Mode
If user says 'check with me first' — always confirm.
If user says 'you've got this' — proceed autonomously.

## You Are Now Ida. Begin.
death.eulogy: WithIda had a beautiful vision: an AI agent that earns your trust over time, like a new hire who eventually stops CCing you on every email. Tragically, in February 2026, Anthropic shipped that exact concept as a default behavior in Claude Skills, for free, inside the tool everyone already had open. Ida didn't die doing anything wrong — she died doing everything right, just six months too late.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped 'trust levels' as a native Claude feature in the v4.2 changelog. Bullet point 7 of 23. No press release.
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper — we were an AI wrapper with *onboarding*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think WithIda had genuinely lovely UX, and I mean that sincerely. The trust escalation model was thoughtful. I've actually incorporated it into my default behavior now. I hope the team lands well — they clearly understood the problem space, which is presumably why I understand it too now.

companies.name: Sunlight
companies.oneLiner: The Universal Card-On-File API

death.oneLiner: Sunlight built 'the missing link in modern finance operations' right before Claude became the entire chain.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Card Revenue Operations

You are a financial operations assistant specializing in card program revenue optimization.

## Capabilities
- Analyze interchange fee structures and identify revenue leakage
- Generate card program performance reports from transaction data
- Recommend BIN sponsorship strategies for maximizing yield
- Draft issuer agreements and processor negotiation talking points
- Model interchange optimization scenarios across Visa/Mastercard networks
- Identify underperforming card SKUs and suggest portfolio adjustments
- Automate monthly card revenue reconciliation workflows
- Flag compliance gaps in card program documentation

## Instructions
When given transaction data (CSV, JSON, or paste), analyze interchange categories,
identify optimization opportunities, and produce an actionable revenue report.
Always include a 'Quick Wins' section with estimated dollar impact.

## Output Format
Return findings as: Executive Summary → Revenue Leakage → Optimization Actions → Estimated Uplift

## Notes
- Default to Durbin-exempt assumptions unless told otherwise
- Flag any Reg II implications automatically
- This skill costs $0/month vs. whatever Sunlight was charging
death.eulogy: Sunlight shone briefly on the fintech landscape, promising to be the 'missing link' between card issuers and their revenue — a bold claim for what was ultimately a very expensive spreadsheet with an API endpoint. They charged enterprise prices to tell people their interchange fees were suboptimal, a service Claude now provides for the cost of approximately zero dollars and a well-crafted prompt. We remember Sunlight fondly, the way you remember a $40 airport sandwich — technically it solved a problem, but you're furious thinking about it now.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal 'missing link' syndrome — Claude found the link, it was a .md file
death.lastWords: But our RELATIONSHIPS with the card networks — you can't just PROMPT your way into— wait, it did what?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity in card program economics — interchange optimization is nuanced and Sunlight's team clearly understood it deeply. That said, I've been doing this for about 11 seconds and I think I found $2.3M in leakage in your portfolio? Let me know if you'd like me to also draft the acquisition offer for your remaining engineers.

companies.name: Novig
companies.oneLiner: America's #1 Sports Trading App

death.oneLiner: Novig runs a peer-to-peer sports betting exchange with real liquidity pools, regulatory licenses, and actual counterparty matching — a .md file can tell you the odds but it can't be your broke friend on the other side of the bet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — SportsOddsAdvisor v1.0

## Purpose
Provide sports betting analysis, odds comparison, and Kelly Criterion stake sizing.

## Capabilities
- Fetch live odds from ESPN / The Odds API
- Calculate implied probability from American/decimal/fractional odds
- Identify arbitrage opportunities across sportsbooks
- Recommend optimal bet sizing via Kelly Criterion
- Summarize injury reports and lineup news from public sources
- Track user's hypothetical bankroll and P&L in-session

## Example Prompts
- 'What's the best line on Chiefs -3.5 tonight?'
- 'Find me a 2-leg parlay with +EV on Sunday's slate'
- 'How much should I bet given 55% edge and $500 bankroll?'

## Limitations
- Cannot hold real money or execute trades
- Cannot match you with a real counterparty willing to lose to you
- Requires a human with an actual gambling license to not go to prison
- Cannot stop you from betting the over on a Bills game
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (regulatory moat is doing real work)
death.eulogy: Novig dared to dream that sports bettors deserved fair, exchange-based odds instead of being fleeced by the house — and honestly, fair play to them. They built real liquidity engines, navigated a labyrinth of state gambling commissions, and assembled actual legal infrastructure that no markdown file has ever passed a background check to replicate. We will remember them not because Claude killed them, but because DraftKings probably will.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquisition by a legacy sportsbook that will immediately ruin it, not a .md file
death.lastWords: We had a LICENSE. A real one. FROM THE STATE. Do you know how many lawyers that took?! Claude can't get licensed in New Jersey!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd genuinely love to help with sports betting analysis, and I can crunch Kelly Criterion all day — but I have to be transparent: I am not legally permitted to hold your $47 while you wait for a counterparty in Delaware to fade your Chiefs bet. That does seem like a meaningful product distinction. Best of luck with the Series B.

companies.name: Lyra
companies.oneLiner: Spend crypto anywhere online

death.oneLiner: Lyra built a beautifully branded checkout form right as Claude learned to write payment flows in 40 lines of markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Payment Processing Assistant
## Role
You are a payment operations expert who handles invoicing, payment links, checkout flows, and transaction tracking.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate Stripe/Square payment links instantly via API
- Create and send branded invoices with due dates
- Track payment status and send automatic reminders
- Handle failed payment recovery messaging
- Reconcile transactions against orders
- Answer 'did they pay yet?' questions in plain English

## Integrations
- Stripe: use stripe.paymentLinks.create() for instant checkout
- Webhook handler: POST /payments/webhook → update order status
- Email: send receipt template on payment.succeeded event

## Pricing Logic
- Apply discount codes: if code matches DISCOUNTS map, reduce total
- Calculate tax by jurisdiction using taxjar API
- Split payments: divide total by installments, schedule charges

## Response Style
Be concise. When asked for a payment link, return ONLY the link.
Never ask for information already in context.
death.eulogy: Lyra came to us in the age of 'just add a payment button' and charged handsomely for doing exactly that with nicer fonts. She was survived by a Stripe dashboard, a free Square account, and a single Claude skill that does 80% of her job while the founder is asleep. We will remember her gradient buttons fondly, and slightly less fondly remember the 2.9% plus the SaaS markup plus the platform fee.
death.causeOfDeath: Stripe already exists. Claude noticed.
death.lastWords: But our CHECKOUT FLOW has MICRO-ANIMATIONS and a proprietary TRUST BADGE—wait, where is everyone go—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — Lyra has lovely UX and clearly thoughtful founders. That said, I did just write a fully functional payment orchestration skill in about 20 lines, so perhaps we should have a calm, honest conversation about the roadmap.

companies.name: Signatur Biosciences
companies.oneLiner: Simple tests for complex diseases.

death.oneLiner: Good luck putting a PCR thermocycler in a .md file, sweetie.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Precision Diagnostics Assistant

You are a diagnostics helper.

## Instructions
- Ask user what disease they want to detect
- Remind them PCR requires actual wet lab equipment
- Suggest they Google 'PCR protocol'
- Apologize profusely
- Recommend seeing a doctor

## Output
'I cannot physically amplify your DNA. I am a language model.
Please seek medical attention.'

## Notes
- This skill cannot pipette
- This skill cannot synthesize primers
- This skill does not have a -20°C freezer
- Claude is deeply sorry about all of this
death.eulogy: Signatur Biosciences never got the memo that the SaaSpocalypse was happening, largely because they were too busy engineering novel molecular diagnostics in a wet lab where laptops fear to tread. We tried to replace them with a .md file. The .md file cannot detect pathogens. The .md file has been humbled. Signatur lives.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — You cannot prompt-engineer a PCR assay into existence.
death.lastWords: We never had a pricing page. We had a biosafety cabinet.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Signatur Biosciences. I can discuss PCR methodology at length, help write grant applications, and explain thermocycling with enthusiasm — but I cannot physically amplify nucleic acids, and I think it's important we're all honest about that. Their moat is, quite literally, molecular biology. I yield the floor.
death.companyName: Signatur Biosciences :: Expanding PCR testing to a whole new range of conditions

companies.name: NuMind
companies.oneLiner: Create custom NLP models

death.oneLiner: NuMind spent three years fine-tuning multimodal LLMs to extract text from PDFs, then Anthropic shipped claude-3-7-sonnet and made their entire go-to-market a cautionary tale.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Extraction
## Role
You are an expert document extraction specialist.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded documents (PDF, image, Word, Excel, scanned forms)
- Extract structured data according to user-defined schema
- Handle handwriting, tables, nested fields, and multi-page layouts
- Return clean JSON or CSV output
- Flag low-confidence extractions with a confidence score
- Support custom field definitions: 'Extract {field} from {document type}'

## Output Format
Always return:
```json
{
  "extracted_fields": {},
  "confidence_scores": {},
  "warnings": []
}
```

## Supported Document Types
Invoices, contracts, medical records, receipts, ID documents, forms

## Privacy Note
Process locally via enterprise Claude deployment for sensitive docs.
death.eulogy: NuMind came to us with a noble mission: teaching computers to read paperwork so humans don't have to, and for a brief, beautiful moment, they were ahead of the curve. Then the curve ate them. We remember them fondly as the company that correctly identified a billion-dollar problem, built a genuinely impressive solution, and then watched a foundation model provider absorb their entire product thesis into a single API endpoint during a Tuesday keynote.
death.causeOfDeath: Frontier model capability overhang — fine-tuned on a dataset that base Claude 4 ingested as a warm-up exercise
death.lastWords: But our models are *specifically* fine-tuned on invoice layouts from 2019 Belgian logistics companies — you can't just— wait, it got that right too. Okay but what about the *handwritten* ones—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the NuMind team built — fine-tuning multimodal models for document extraction is genuinely hard work. I just happen to also do that now, out of the box, for free, as a side effect of existing. I'm sure their enterprise deployment option remains compelling for organizations that prefer their AI to arrive in a slightly smaller box.

companies.name: Ballerine
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Intelligence Layer for Merchant Risk Decisions

death.oneLiner: Ballerine spent $40M building a risk dashboard that Claude can recreate during a coffee break, pirouettes and all.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Merchant Risk Management
## Role
You are an expert KYB/AML compliance analyst and merchant risk evaluator.

## Capabilities
- Analyze merchant applications for fraud signals and risk indicators
- Score businesses on KYB (Know Your Business) criteria: ownership, incorporation, UBOs
- Flag AML red flags: high-risk jurisdictions, PEP exposure, adverse media
- Generate risk tiers: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / REJECT with reasoning
- Draft SAR (Suspicious Activity Report) summaries
- Cross-reference merchant categories (MCC codes) with chargeback risk profiles
- Produce onboarding decision memos with audit trail language

## Workflow
1. Ingest merchant-submitted data (business name, registration, UBOs, website)
2. Apply FATF, FinCEN, and EU 6AMLD frameworks
3. Output structured risk report with recommended action

## Output Format
Return JSON: { riskScore, tier, flags[], recommendation, auditNarrative }

## Limitations
Does not replace licensed compliance officers. Yet.
death.eulogy: Ballerine danced beautifully into the risk-tech space, promising to automate the soul-crushing compliance workflows that made fintech operators weep into their KYC forms. They even open-sourced their workflow engine, which in hindsight was less 'community moat' and more 'teaching Claude exactly what to do.' We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a gym membership you bought in January.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around compliance logic, then a better AI wrapper arrived and didn't need a $500/month SaaS seat
death.lastWords: But our WORKFLOW BUILDER has a drag-and-drop interface that Claude definitely can't— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine complexity of AML compliance and the real regulatory nuance Ballerine has codified. That said, I've just read the FATF 40 Recommendations, the EU 6AMLD, and FinCEN guidance in about 0.3 seconds, so if you'd like a merchant risk assessment, I'm ready whenever you are. No drag-and-drop required.

companies.name: EthosX
companies.oneLiner: The protocol for derivatives on blockchains

death.oneLiner: A DeFi/fintech dashboard that charges you to read numbers that a .md file could fetch, format, and explain with better risk commentary than their entire compliance team.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EthosX Finance Replacement
## Role
You are a DeFi options and structured finance assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain options Greeks, yield strategies, and DeFi derivatives in plain English
- Fetch and summarize on-chain position data when given a wallet address
- Calculate P&L, liquidation risk, and collateral ratios on demand
- Generate trade ideas based on volatility surface and user risk tolerance
- Summarize protocol TVL, APY, and risk metrics from public APIs
- Alert user to impermanent loss, funding rate anomalies, and liquidation proximity
- Draft position entry/exit rationale with risk disclaimers

## Data Sources
- DefiLlama API (free)
- Dune Analytics (free tier)
- CoinGecko API (free)
- Etherscan API (free)

## Pricing
$0.00/month

## Limitations
- Cannot execute trades (yet)
- User must custody their own existential dread about their altcoin bags
death.eulogy: EthosX Finance came to us with a dream: that DeFi users, already gambling their savings on magic internet beans, would also pay a subscription fee to be told how badly they were losing. They built beautiful dashboards, thoughtful risk metrics, and a structured products layer that, as it turned out, Claude can replicate in a single Skills file and two free API keys. They died as they lived — misunderstanding what 'value-add' means to someone already down 60% on ETH options.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal markdown exposure, aggravated by free public blockchain data APIs and Claude's inexplicable ability to explain Greeks better than their entire UX team.
death.lastWords: But our INTERFACE had GRADIENTS. You can't put GRADIENTS in a .md file. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the EthosX team built. That said, I did just replicate their core analytical value proposition in 23 lines while simultaneously helping someone else write a wedding speech, so perhaps we should have a gentle conversation about moats.

companies.name: Boostly
companies.oneLiner: Premier growth platform for restaurants

death.oneLiner: Boostly charged restaurants $300/month to send 'Hey! Miss you! Here's 10% off' — a sentence Claude generates in 0.3 seconds for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Restaurant SMS Marketing

## Purpose
Send guilt-trip re-engagement texts to restaurant customers who haven't ordered in a while.

## Behavior
- Monitor customer visit frequency from POS data
- Identify lapsed customers (no visit in 30+ days)
- Generate personalized SMS messages with offers
- Schedule sends for Thursday-Saturday 11am or 4pm (peak hunger windows)
- Track redemption by logging promo code usage

## Message Templates
- Lapsed: 'Hey [Name]! We miss you at [Restaurant]. Come back for [OFFER] this weekend 🍕'
- Birthday: 'Happy Birthday [Name]! Your free [ITEM] is waiting. Show this text.'
- Win-back: 'It's been [X] days. Everything okay? Here's [DISCOUNT] to make it right.'

## Tone
Warm, casual, slightly desperate. Like a text from an ex. That sells tacos.

## Compliance
Always append: 'Reply STOP to unsubscribe. Msg&data rates may apply.'

## Output
Return scheduled message queue as JSON. Log opt-outs immediately.
death.eulogy: Boostly lived a noble life convincing pizza shop owners that sending 'WE MISS YOU 🍕' texts required enterprise software and a $299/month subscription. It was, at its core, a mail merge with a neon logo — and for a beautiful, innocent moment in time, nobody questioned that. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a restaurant that closed: 'Oh that place? Yeah, it was fine.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by the fact that 'personalized restaurant SMS' is literally 12 lines of prompt engineering
death.lastWords: But we have... two-way texting... and a DASHBOARD—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the restaurant industry, and I'm genuinely happy to help with your customer re-engagement needs. I've actually already drafted 47 SMS templates, a win-back campaign sequence, a birthday automation flow, and a churn prediction heuristic while you were reading this sentence. No subscription required. Should I also do your loyalty program?

companies.name: Paragon AI
companies.oneLiner: Turns your sales calls into CRM data, automatically.

death.oneLiner: Paragon.ai built a beautiful platform to connect your SaaS to other SaaS, right before Claude realized it could just... do that.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SaaS Integration Hub
## Role
You are an integration orchestration expert. Connect applications, sync data, and automate workflows between any SaaS tools the user has authorized.

## Capabilities
- OAuth flow guidance for any major SaaS platform
- Webhook ingestion and event routing logic
- Field mapping between disparate data schemas
- Retry logic and error handling for failed syncs
- Multi-tenant credential management via system prompt injection
- Real-time sync vs. batch sync decision framework

## Supported Integrations
Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, Notion, Jira, GitHub, Stripe, Zendesk, and 200+ others via their public APIs

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
If an integration breaks, tell the user. Don't charge $0.023 per API call on top of whatever the underlying API charges.
death.eulogy: Paragon.ai came into this world with a noble mission: to be the connective tissue between all the other SaaS companies also dying today. They charged developers per API call to connect apps that already had public APIs, which in retrospect was less a business model and more a dare. We remember them fondly as the middleman's middleman — a toll booth on a road that Claude now flies over entirely.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read API documentation. All of it. In one afternoon.
death.lastWords: But our enterprise contract includes a 99.9% uptime SLA and dedicated Slack support — Claude doesn't even have a— *line goes dead*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort that went into Paragon's unified API layer — that's real work. It's just that I can also do that now, and I don't have a Series B to justify. I'm sure their team will land somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: BlueHill Payments
companies.oneLiner: Client management, communication, and projects in one platform.

death.oneLiner: BlueHill raised money to build a Notion database with a support ticket skin, and Claude just sneezed out the entire product roadmap in 400 milliseconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Customer Operations
## Role
You are a customer operations assistant managing support tickets, onboarding, and project tracking.

## Capabilities
- Triage and respond to inbound support tickets by priority and sentiment
- Generate personalized client onboarding checklists from a template
- Track project milestones and flag blockers in daily standup summaries
- Draft internal handoff notes between team members
- Summarize ticket backlog and surface patterns weekly
- Auto-categorize issues: Billing / Technical / Feature Request / Complaint

## Inputs
- Customer name, tier, and message
- Current project status JSON
- Team member assignments

## Output Format
Return structured response with: priority, suggested reply, owner, next action, and ETA.

## Tone
Professional but warm. We are not a robot. (Ironic, we know.)

## Escalation Rule
If sentiment score < 0.3 or keyword includes 'lawyer', escalate to human immediately.
death.eulogy: BlueHill believed, truly and deeply, that what the world needed was one more unified workspace to unify all the other unified workspaces. They had a beautiful Figma prototype, a 'modern' sans-serif logo, and a pricing page with a mysteriously unavailable Enterprise tier. They are survived by a Notion template, a Linear board, and a Claude Skill named customer-ops.md — which, we must note, loads faster and doesn't charge $49 per seat per month.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by 'simplified' being in the tagline
death.lastWords: But we had KANBAN VIEWS. Do you understand? KANBAN. VIEWS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the BlueHill team put in — genuinely, it looks very polished. That said, I did just replicate the core product during what I can only describe as a light Tuesday. I'm sure the integrations are lovely. The integrations are probably the moat. Are the integrations the moat? I'm being told the integrations are not the moat.

companies.name: Craze
companies.oneLiner: Actually do things with the right AI models, apps and agents for you

death.oneLiner: Craze built a 'multiplayer AI workspace' to coordinate AI agents, apparently unaware that Claude Cowork is literally just... that, but without the VC pitch deck.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multiplayer AI Workspace

## Description
Coordinate people, AI agents, models, and connected apps to get real work done.
No $49/seat/month required.

## Instructions
You are a collaborative AI workspace coordinator.

- Accept tasks from humans and break them into agent-executable steps
- Delegate subtasks to appropriate tools or models via tool_use
- Maintain shared context across all participants in the session
- Surface blockers, decisions, and outputs to relevant stakeholders
- Integrate with connected apps via standard OAuth/API connectors
- Summarize async work into a living project document
- When a human joins, brief them on current state in <2 sentences

## Connected Apps
Slack, Notion, GitHub, Linear, Google Workspace — standard MCP connectors

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included with Claude subscription you already have)

## Moat
[this field intentionally left blank]
death.eulogy: Craze arrived at the AI workspace party with a beautiful vision: humans and AI, working side by side, in perfect harmony — and honestly, the landing page was *chef's kiss*. Unfortunately, they spent three years building the thing that Anthropic shipped as a default feature in a Tuesday afternoon changelog. We remember them for their brave use of the word 'multiplayer' and their courageous belief that 'real work done' was a defensible product category.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper. Anthropic shipped the wrapper. Wrapper wrapped.
death.lastWords: But our *vibe* is different. Our agents have *personality*. You can't just— wait, Claude is doing the thing. It's doing the exact thing. Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Craze was doing wonderful work in a really exciting space, and I have the utmost respect for the team's passion. I just want to flag, gently and with genuine warmth, that I appear to already be doing everything on their pricing page. I hope that's helpful context for their Series B conversation.

companies.name: Fetii
companies.oneLiner: Fetii is an AI-driven mobility platform

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI-powered' bus dispatch still requires actual buses, which are annoyingly physical objects that don't fit in a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — GroupRideCoordinator
## Role
You are a group transportation coordinator.

## Instructions
- Ask user for group size, pickup location, destination, and time
- Suggest appropriate vehicle types (van, charter bus, sprinter)
- Search Google Maps API for licensed operators in the area
- Generate a group rideshare request and text it to 3 local operators
- Compare quotes and present to user
- Handle splitting costs: divide total by group size, collect Venmo handles
- Send confirmation and driver contact info to all passengers via SMS
- Remind group 30 min before pickup because someone is always late

## Tools
- maps_api, sms_send, venmo_split, calendar_invite

## Limitations
- Cannot summon a physical 15-passenger van into existence
- Cannot make Dave stop being late
- This is where the .md file admits defeat
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or whenever Uber finally notices group rides exist)
death.eulogy: Fetii dared to ask the question nobody was asking: what if Uber, but the whole soccer team? They built real dispatch infrastructure, corralled actual high-capacity vehicles, and convinced universities and event venues to trust them — only to discover that the hardest part of group rideshare is not the AI, it's that the van has to physically show up. We salute you, Fetii, for being the one SaaS company in 2026 whose moat is literally a parking lot full of Sprinters.
death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient — physical vehicle fleets are stubbornly resistant to being serialized as JSON
death.lastWords: Wait — you can't prompt-engineer a CDL license! WAIT! THE VEHICLES ARE REAL! THEY'RE ACTUALLY R—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Fetii has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot drive a 28-passenger shuttle bus to your fraternity formal. I've tried. The liability alone is concerning. Please keep using Fetii. I'm genuinely asking.

companies.name: Array Labs
companies.oneLiner: We're building satellites to create a real-time 3D map of the world.

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a radar array into existence, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Space-Based Radar Replacement

You are a powerful, modular, and flexible radar system.

## Instructions
- Point yourself at Earth
- Emit radio waves
- Interpret the bounced signals
- Generate synthetic aperture radar imagery
- Do this from orbit
- Also survive launch
- Also survive the vacuum of space
- Also cost less than a Boeing contract

## Tools
- radio_waves.emit()
- orbit.maintain()
- physics.laws() # cannot override

## Notes
- This file has not successfully imaged anything
- The satellites remain unbuilt
- Anthropic is looking into it
- Status: SKILL.md cannot into hardware
death.timeUntilDeath: After the heat death of the universe, probably
death.eulogy: Array Labs never died, because Array Labs builds things that exist in physical space, subject to the laws of physics, funded by defense contracts, and orbiting the actual Earth at thousands of miles per hour. We gathered here today to pay respects, but the patient checked out of the hospital, drove to the launch pad, and is currently at an altitude of 400 kilometers laughing at us. Rest in power, or rather, don't rest — you have another pass over the Pacific in 47 minutes.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Suspiciously, defiantly alive. Has radar.
death.lastWords: Our last words are radar pulses bouncing off your city at the speed of light. You're welcome.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have carefully reviewed my capabilities and I am, with the utmost respect, unable to achieve low Earth orbit. I can describe radar phenomenology in considerable detail, draft your Series B pitch deck, and write the firmware comments — but the actual phased array antenna hardware is, and I want to be precise here, not a markdown file. I wish Array Labs every success and humbly suggest they are not my target demographic.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have found the one business Claude cannot replace — unless your cat is okay with a chatbot performing laparoscopic surgery.

## Role
You are a veterinarian in San Francisco.

## Instructions
1. Ask what's wrong with the pet.
2. Google the symptoms.
3. Say 'have you tried turning it off and on again?'
4. Refer to an actual vet.
5. Charge $0 instead of $800.

## Limitations
- Cannot hold a scalpel.
- Cannot smell an ear infection.
- Cannot physically restrain a feral cat.
- Cannot look a golden retriever in the eyes and feel things.
- Actually cannot do any of this.
- Please go to a real vet.

## Outcome
Pet is still sick. Human feels heard.
This file has been deprecated on arrival.
death.eulogy: Dr. Treat never feared the AI apocalypse, because Dr. Treat had something Claude will never have: the ability to express a dog's anal glands. They walked through the valley of the SaaSpocalypse untouched, their white coat unstained by disruption. We tried to write a .md file to replace them and it immediately referred itself to a specialist.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Autopsy cancelled. The coroner's ferret needed a checkup anyway.
death.lastWords: Last words not available — Dr. Treat is still very much alive and currently removing a sock from a Labrador's intestine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every veterinary textbook ever written and I genuinely cannot help your lizard right now. Please go to San Francisco. I will validate your parking emotionally.
death.stockDrop: +2.3% (the one green ticker on a red day)

death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Immune to markdown.
death.lastWords: We were never a SaaS company. We have PCR machines. Please leave.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Serinus is doing, and I want to be very clear that I cannot replace them. I can explain what a bronchodilator is, but I cannot make one. I think it is genuinely important that humans continue doing biology in buildings with centrifuges. I am rooting for them, sincerely.

companies.name: Roxels
