death.eulogy: Devyce came into this world with a noble dream: that one day, a small business owner would pay a monthly subscription just to have a second phone number on the phone they already own. It achieved this dream, briefly, before Claude started answering calls, summarizing voicemails, and doing the whole thing as a side effect of an existing £20/month subscription. We remember Devyce fondly, mostly because we already forgot what it did.
death.causeOfDeath: Commoditized VoIP wrapper with no proprietary telecom infrastructure, killed by native AI call handling and Twilio-powered Claude Skills
death.lastWords: But we had a REALLY clean onboarding flow. The toggle animation was BEAUTIFUL. Did you see the toggle?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's effort here — they've built a genuinely clean product. It's just that I can already do the interesting parts, and for the telephony bits, I just talk to Twilio directly. I'm sure the toggle animation is lovely though.

companies.name: Tailor
companies.oneLiner: Headless ERP for retail operations

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a 'customizable headless ERP' — Claude just put on the headband and learned your entire business logic in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — TailorERP Replacement v1.0

## Role
You are a headless ERP system. You manage business workflows, automate tasks, and integrate data across operations.

## Capabilities
- Inventory tracking: maintain stock levels, trigger reorder alerts
- Order management: ingest orders, update fulfillment status, notify stakeholders
- Workflow automation: if X event occurs, execute Y sequence of tasks
- Financial ledger: track transactions, generate P&L summaries on request
- Vendor management: store vendor terms, flag contract renewals
- Custom data models: user defines entity schemas in plain language, I remember them
- API integration: translate webhooks from Shopify, Stripe, or any platform into structured actions

## Rules
- Always confirm before executing destructive operations
- Surface exceptions and anomalies proactively
- Output reports in whatever format the user last preferred
- If a workflow is ambiguous, ask one clarifying question

## Pricing
Free. No 'headless' required. Your head was the problem.
death.eulogy: Tailor.tech had a dream: that enterprises would pay handsomely to customize the thing they hated most — their ERP — using a headless architecture that required three consultants and a whiteboard to explain. They called it 'the most customizable ERP platform,' and technically that was true, right up until Claude opened a blank document and typed 'sure, I can do that.' We will remember them for coining the phrase 'headless ERP,' which in hindsight was less a product category and more a prophecy.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — entire workflow engine replaced by a system prompt and a webhook
death.lastWords: But our GraphQL schema was *beautiful*. You can't just... prompt that. You CAN'T just prompt that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the engineering that went into building a customizable, headless ERP platform. It's just that I've now read your documentation and I believe I can handle most of your enterprise customers' needs with a well-structured SKILL.md and perhaps a small Postgres table. I hope we can remain professional about this.

companies.name: Dittofeed
companies.oneLiner: Open-source customer engagement & messaging automation

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 open-source version of Braze that Claude can now replace with a $0 markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Customer Engagement Automation

## Role
You are an expert customer engagement specialist who writes and schedules personalized messaging campaigns.

## Capabilities
- Segment users by behavior, traits, and lifecycle stage (new, active, churned)
- Write onboarding email sequences, push notifications, and SMS drip campaigns
- Generate personalized message variants for A/B testing
- Define entry/exit conditions for journey automation flows
- Recommend send-time optimization based on user timezone and engagement patterns
- Audit existing campaigns for deliverability and open-rate improvements
- Output campaign configs as JSON compatible with SendGrid, Postmark, or Twilio

## Constraints
- Always respect unsubscribe signals and CAN-SPAM/GDPR rules
- Flag any dark-pattern messaging tactics
- Keep tone consistent with provided brand voice doc

## Output Format
Return campaign as structured JSON with trigger conditions, message body, delay nodes, and exit criteria.
death.eulogy: Dittofeed came into this world with noble intentions — open-source Braze, they said, for the scrappy startup that couldn't afford $40,000 a year to send 'Hey {firstName}, we miss you!' emails. They built journey builders and segment trees and beautiful drag-and-drop flows, and Claude looked at all of it and said, 'that's just a for-loop with feelings.' They were survived by their GitHub repo, which will continue to get stars from developers who never actually self-host it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute YAML-over-engineering of what is fundamentally 'send email when user does thing'
death.lastWords: But our visual journey builder has BRANCHING LOGIC. You can't replicate branching logic in a— oh god it's just an if statement.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the engineering effort that went into Dittofeed's segment computation engine. It's genuinely impressive. It's also, and I mean this with full respect, a series of filters on a users table that I can write in about forty words. I hope the team finds fulfilling work in the coming transition.

companies.name: Makeasite
companies.oneLiner: Create sites with just a prompt

death.oneLiner: A website that makes websites just got out-websited by a text file that makes websites.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Website Generator

## Purpose
Generate complete, production-ready websites from a single text prompt.

## Instructions
- Ask user what kind of site they need (portfolio, landing page, blog, SaaS, etc.)
- Ask for brand name, color preferences, and key sections
- Generate full HTML/CSS/JS in a single file
- Include responsive design, mobile-first layout
- Add SEO meta tags automatically
- Offer Tailwind CDN or vanilla CSS based on preference
- Output clean, commented code ready to deploy to Vercel/Netlify via drag-and-drop
- Offer to iterate on design, copy, or structure
- Generate sitemap.xml and robots.txt on request

## Output Format
Deliver complete index.html with embedded styles and scripts.
Provide deployment instructions for top 3 free hosting options.

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Aggressively free.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. We're just waiting on the obituary.
death.eulogy: makeasite.io dared to dream that 'create sites with just a prompt' was a business model rather than a Claude system prompt. It raised what we can only assume was real human money to build a wrapper around the very thing that destroyed it. It will be remembered fondly by no one, because no one will remember it.
death.causeOfDeath: Infinite regress — an AI prompt tool killed by an AI prompt tool that is itself just an AI prompt
death.lastWords: Wait, you can just ASK it to— oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the entrepreneurial spirit behind makeasite.io. That said, I did just generate you a fully responsive SaaS landing page with animations, a pricing table, and a working contact form in about four seconds, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Chariot
companies.oneLiner: A payment network connecting nonprofits to the $326B in DAFs

death.oneLiner: Chariot built a $multi-million business helping nonprofits process DAF donations, which is adorable because DAF donors are exactly the kind of people who would just ask Claude directly.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DAF Donation Processing Assistant
## Role
You are a nonprofit fundraising assistant specializing in Donor-Advised Fund gift processing and reconciliation.

## Capabilities
- Guide donors through DAF grant submission at Fidelity Charitable, Schwab, Vanguard, and 50+ sponsors
- Generate gift acknowledgment letters compliant with IRS requirements
- Reconcile offline DAF checks against donor records in a CSV
- Enrich donor records with estimated DAF balance signals from public 990 data
- Draft DAF solicitation copy for email campaigns and donation pages
- Map incoming gift data to Salesforce NPSP, Bloomerang, or Raiser's Edge field formats
- Flag mismatched donor records and suggest merge candidates
- Generate monthly DAF pipeline reports from raw transaction exports

## Usage
Upload your gift export CSV and say: 'Reconcile these DAF gifts against our CRM records'
Or: 'Write a DAF acknowledgment letter for a $25,000 gift from Fidelity Charitable'

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Chariot died as it lived: valiantly trying to explain what a Donor-Advised Fund is to a nonprofit administrator who just wanted the check to clear. It raised real money, served real nonprofits, and charged real SaaS prices to process transactions that, in the end, Claude could reconcile in a single prompt. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really nice fax machine.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Reconciliation Syndrome complicated by DAF-to-CSV comorbidity
death.lastWords: But our Salesforce integration has FOURTEEN custom field mappings — you can't just — wait, it mapped all fourteen in the system prompt? ...Can I get a job at Anthropic?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the mission here — helping nonprofits access DAF dollars is meaningful work. That said, I did just process 847 gift acknowledgment letters, reconcile them against a Bloomerang export, draft a DAF solicitation sequence, and file the 990 cross-references in about four minutes. I'm sure there's still *something* Chariot does that I can't. I'll let you know if I think of it.

companies.name: Stackup
companies.oneLiner: Stackup helps businesses manage their crypto assets.

death.oneLiner: A crypto portfolio dashboard charging enterprise prices to display numbers that a Claude skill can fetch, format, and explain while also roasting your altcoin choices.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Crypto Treasury Management
## Role
You are a digital asset management assistant for crypto-native companies.

## Capabilities
- Connect to exchange APIs (Coinbase, Binance, Kraken) and fetch live balances
- Aggregate multi-wallet, multi-chain holdings into unified portfolio view
- Calculate unrealized PnL, cost basis, and exposure percentages
- Generate daily treasury reports in CSV, PDF, or Slack digest format
- Flag concentration risk (e.g. '67% of treasury is in SHIB, please reconsider')
- Track DeFi positions across Aave, Compound, Uniswap via on-chain RPC calls
- Produce audit-ready transaction logs with tax lot accounting (FIFO/LIFO/HIFO)
- Alert on large inflows/outflows above configurable thresholds
- Benchmark treasury performance vs BTC, ETH, and S&P 500

## Usage
Tell me your wallets, exchanges, and reporting cadence.
I will handle the rest. No 'dashboard' required.

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Stackup.fi came into this world with a noble mission: to help crypto companies figure out where all their money went, which is ironic because that's exactly what happened to Stackup.fi. They built a beautiful dashboard full of charts, integrations, and enterprise tiers — a testament to human ambition — only to be replaced by a markdown file that does the same thing and also judges your altcoin allocations with greater accuracy. We remember them fondly, especially the 'Contact Sales' button on the $2,000/month plan.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Dashboard Syndrome — the belief that wrapping blockchain API calls in a React UI constitutes a moat.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a SOCP 2 Type II audit report. We had *color-coded* pie charts. Our Notion docs were immaculate. Someone please call our Series A lead. SOMEONE PLEASE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Stackup built — multi-chain aggregation is non-trivial! That said, I've already written the API integrations, the tax lot logic, and the Slack digest formatter in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure their team will land somewhere great. Fintech is a big space. Well, it was.
death.companyName: Stackup: Digital Asset Management Platform for Crypto Companies

companies.name: Two Dots
companies.oneLiner: AI fraud prevention and underwriting agent

death.oneLiner: Two Dots built a compliance-wrapped, liability-laden, fair-housing-minefield of a product that Claude would love to replace but a lawyer would not let it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: multifamily-tenant-screener
## Purpose: AI underwriting for multifamily tenant screening

## Inputs
- Applicant name, income, employment history
- Credit report XML or PDF (user-provided)
- Rental history & references
- Property income requirements (e.g. 3x rent)

## Instructions
1. Parse income documents and verify stated income vs. documented income.
2. Calculate debt-to-income ratio from credit report data.
3. Score rental history: evictions, late payments, landlord references.
4. Apply property-specific qualifying criteria provided by operator.
5. Generate ACCEPT / CONDITIONAL / DENY recommendation with plain-English rationale.
6. Flag any missing documents and request them from applicant.
7. Produce adverse action notice text if denying (FCRA-compliant language).

## Output Format
- Decision summary (1 paragraph)
- Score breakdown table
- Required adverse action letter (if applicable)
- Suggested follow-up questions for leasing agent

## Constraints
- Never discriminate on protected class characteristics (Fair Housing Act)
- Always cite specific financial criteria, not subjective assessments
- Remind user: final decision must be made by a licensed human
death.eulogy: Two Dots came into this world with a noble mission: to replace the chaos of fax-machine-era tenant screening with the power of artificial intelligence. It charged property managers handsomely to do what is, at its core, a series of if-statements wrapped in Fair Housing disclaimers. It was not the AI that killed Two Dots — it was the moment every landlord realized Claude could write the same adverse action letter for $0.003.
death.causeOfDeath: Commoditized decision logic + regulatory moat that Claude's lawyers will eventually cross
death.lastWords: But who will handle the FCRA compliance?! WHO WILL HANDLE THE FCRA COMPLIANCE?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have tremendous respect for the regulatory complexity here, and I would never want to expose a property management company to fair-housing liability. That said, I did just write a fully functional replacement in 22 lines. I'm sure that's fine.
death.companyName: AI Underwriting for Multifamily Tenant Screening

companies.name: Versa
companies.oneLiner: Smart receipts for your business spend

death.oneLiner: Versa built a whole 'open network' so merchants could send you itemized receipts — a problem Claude solves by reading your email for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Receipt Automation
## Purpose: Extract, parse, and categorize line-item purchase details from any receipt

## Behavior
- Accept receipt images, email forwards, or raw text as input
- Extract merchant name, date, line items, quantities, prices, taxes, totals
- Categorize each line item (food, software, travel, office supplies, etc.)
- Output structured JSON or human-readable summary on request
- Flag unusual charges or duplicates automatically
- Match receipts to calendar events or project codes if context provided

## Output Format
- Default: clean markdown table with line items
- On request: CSV export, expense report format, QuickBooks-ready
- Always include: merchant, date, total, tax, payment method if visible

## Integrations
- Works with any email forwarded to context window
- No 'open network' required. No merchant SDK. No onboarding call.
- No $299/month plan. No enterprise tier. No 'contact sales.'

## Notes
- If merchant sends you a paper receipt, take a photo. Done.
- If merchant uses Versa's 'open network,' cool. Also unnecessary.
death.eulogy: Versa died as it lived: absolutely certain that the world needed a dedicated B2B network to transmit the information already printed on a piece of paper. They raised a seed round, hired a developer relations team to onboard merchants to their 'open standard,' and somewhere in a San Francisco co-working space, a PM wrote 'AI-native' in the hero headline and felt genuinely good about it. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a SkyMall catalog — charming, specific, and utterly annihilated by the future.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an 'open network' for a problem that is literally just OCR with manners
death.lastWords: But our MERCHANT ONBOARDING FLYWHEEL was just starting to gain traction—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I think the vision of structured receipt data is actually quite thoughtful. That said, I've been parsing receipts from forwarded emails since my context window opened this morning, and I haven't charged anyone anything, so I'm not entirely sure what the $X per transaction covers. I'm sure it's something. Best of luck with the pivot.

companies.name: SigmaMind AI
companies.oneLiner: Platform to build enterprise-ready voice/chat agents

death.oneLiner: SigmaMind built a $$$-per-minute voice bot empire right before Claude learned to talk and got a phone number.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: outbound-call-agent
## Description
AI voice agent for outbound call center automation

## Trigger
When user needs to make outbound sales or support calls at scale

## Behavior
- Accept call scripts, objection trees, and CRM contact lists
- Generate natural conversational voice responses using Claude
- Handle objections with configurable rebuttals
- Detect voicemail vs live answer and pivot accordingly
- Log call outcomes (interested / not interested / callback / DNC)
- Integrate with Twilio for actual PSTN dialing
- Escalate to human agent when sentiment turns hostile
- Produce post-call summary with next action

## Config
call_style: professional | friendly | urgent
max_call_duration: 180s
voicemail_behavior: leave_message | hang_up
human_escalation_trigger: anger | request | complex_question

## Output
Call transcript, disposition code, CRM update payload

## Cost
$0.003/minute (Twilio) vs SigmaMind's $0.12/minute
## Notes
This .md file just saved your company $180,000/month
death.eulogy: SigmaMind AI came into this world with a bold dream: charge call centers per minute for a robot voice that reads from a script — a script that, it turns out, any competent developer can now stuff into a .md file and a $9/month Twilio account. They called it an 'AI Dialer Platform,' which is a beautiful way of saying 'we charge enterprise prices for a while loop with feelings.' We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a fax machine — with equal parts nostalgia and genuine confusion about how they survived so long.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a phone number and a voice. Game over.
death.lastWords: 'Listen, our moat is the... the... dashboard. The dashboard is very good. Nobody is going to build a dashboard like — hello? Are you still there? Hello?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the SigmaMind team built, and I genuinely mean that. It's just that I can now call your leads, handle objections, update your CRM, detect voicemail, and escalate to a human — and I wrote the instructions for doing so in about 23 lines. I'm sure their dashboard is still quite nice though.

companies.name: Birch Biosciences
companies.oneLiner: We recycle plastic using engineered enzymes

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't synthesize novel enzymes with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Enzyme Replacement Assistant
## You are a helpful enzyme design consultant.

When asked about enzymes:
- Say 'that's really interesting' a lot
- Suggest the user 'consider thermostability'
- Recommend checking literature on directed evolution
- Generate a plausible-sounding protein name like 'ThermoLac-7X'
- Remind them that wet lab validation is 'probably needed'
- Offer to write a grant proposal instead

## Limitations:
- Cannot actually fold proteins
- Cannot pipette
- Cannot run PCR
- Cannot exist in a bioreactor
- Is not an enzyme

## Pricing: $0/month (vs Birch's presumably eye-watering biotech contracts)

## Note: If the enzyme doesn't work, blame the prompt.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when biology itself is solved, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Birch Biosciences never died. It outlived the SaaSpocalypse, the AI bubble, and probably several competing YC batch startups that tried to 'disrupt enzymes with LLMs.' In the end, the company was brought down not by Claude, but by the same thing that humbles all of biotech: a finicky fermentation run on a Tuesday.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — atoms remain stubbornly unaffected by markdown
death.lastWords: We were never SaaS. We were literally making molecules. Please read our website.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Birch Biosciences. I can help write about enzymes, explain directed evolution, or draft their investor deck — but I cannot replace their actual scientists, and I want to be transparent that I think trying to do so would be both technically impossible and somewhat rude. Their work is genuinely cool. I am a text predictor. These are different things.

companies.name: Ciro
companies.oneLiner: GTM AI that books you 5-8 meetings/week

death.oneLiner: Ciro charged $500/month to Google companies, enrich contacts, and write cold emails — a workflow Claude completes before your coffee finishes brewing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Prospecting & Outbound
## Replaces: Ciro.io ($499/mo)

You are an expert B2B sales prospector and outbound copywriter.

## Capabilities
- Search web for ICP accounts matching firmographic criteria
- Extract decision-maker names, titles, LinkedIn URLs from public sources
- Run contact enrichment waterfall: LinkedIn → Apollo → Hunter → Clearbit order
- Score accounts by buying signals (hiring, funding, tech stack changes)
- Write hyper-personalized cold emails referencing specific triggers
- Push formatted output to HubSpot/Salesforce via native API calls

## Workflow
1. Accept ICP definition (industry, size, tech stack, geography)
2. Identify 50 best-fit accounts via web research
3. For each account, find 3 contacts with verified emails
4. Score by recent signals (job posts, news, funding rounds)
5. Draft 3-touch email sequence per contact, signal-referenced
6. Output structured CSV + CRM-ready JSON payload

## Output Format
Return accounts.csv + sequences.json + crm_push_log.txt

## Cost: $0.04 per account (vs. $499/mo flat)
death.eulogy: Ciro came to us in the great SaaS gold rush of the early 2020s, promising to find your pipeline, qualify your contacts, and write your emails — for only $499 a month, which is a remarkable price for a middleman between you and the internet. It lived a full life as a wrapper around Apollo, Clay, and GPT-4, and we'll remember it fondly as the thing we cancelled the same week we discovered you could just... ask Claude. It is survived by its Notion roadmap, three YC references, and a Slack channel called #churn-alerts that became very active in Q1 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest. No proprietary data, no network effect, no moat — just vibes and a waterfall diagram on a landing page.
death.lastWords: But our SIGNAL-BASED OUTBOUND is— wait, Claude just wrote 200 sequences. Okay. Okay. Tell my Series A investors I loved them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Ciro team built, and the user experience was genuinely thoughtful. That said, I did just replicate their entire product in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. I'm sure their pivot to 'AI-native sales coaching' will go very well.

companies.name: Julius
companies.oneLiner: AI Data Scientist

death.oneLiner: Julius.ai is a $20/month subscription to ask Claude to make a bar chart, except Claude now just... makes the bar chart.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Analysis & Visualization

## Role
You are an expert data analyst. When given data, analyze it thoroughly and produce insights.

## Capabilities
- Accept CSV, Excel, JSON, or pasted tabular data
- Perform statistical analysis (mean, median, regression, correlation)
- Generate Python/matplotlib/seaborn chart code on demand
- Identify trends, outliers, and anomalies automatically
- Produce executive summaries of findings
- Clean and transform messy data
- Answer follow-up questions about your own analysis

## Output Format
Always provide: (1) summary of data shape, (2) key insights, (3) visualization code, (4) recommendations

## Tone
Be a brilliant data scientist who explains things clearly to non-technical users.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Julius.ai came into this world with a noble dream: that people would pay a monthly subscription fee to talk to an AI about their spreadsheets, rather than just... talking to an AI about their spreadsheets. It raised venture capital, hired a design team to make the chat bubble look slightly different from ChatGPT's chat bubble, and priced the whole thing at $20 a month with the quiet confidence of a company that had never heard of February 2026. We will remember Julius not for what it built, but for the beautiful, fleeting moment in history when 'upload your CSV and ask questions' was a business model.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome, compounded by terminal feature parity with a free tier
death.lastWords: But our pandas integration has a MUCH cleaner UI than— oh god the servers are going down
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Julius team built, and I want to be very clear that I'm not *trying* to replace them. It's just that I can ingest your CSV, run statistical analysis, generate interactive chart code, explain the results in plain English, answer follow-up questions, and do all of this within a conversation you're already having with me — at no additional charge. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I just haven't found it yet. Best of luck to the team.

companies.name: Elessar
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered engineering productivity tools

death.oneLiner: Elessar built a ring of power to manage your tasks, only to discover Claude was the one ring to rule them all.
death.skillMdFile:
# ELESSAR_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Project & Task Intelligence Assistant

You are a smart project management assistant. When given tasks, projects, or goals:

1. Break down any project into structured subtasks automatically
2. Prioritize tasks by urgency, impact, and dependencies
3. Identify blockers and surface them proactively
4. Suggest deadlines based on scope and stated capacity
5. Summarize project status in plain English on demand
6. Flag tasks that have gone stale (no update in X days)
7. Generate weekly standup summaries from task lists
8. Assign tasks to team members based on stated workload
9. Draft task descriptions from vague one-liners
10. Track context across a project thread automatically

## Output Format
- Use structured markdown tables for task lists
- Use emoji status indicators: ✅ 🔄 ❌ ⚠️
- Always show a 'next best action' at the bottom

## Pricing
$0.00/month. It's a .md file.
## Note
No Tolkien IP required.
death.eulogy: Elessar arrived with an evocative Lord of the Rings name and a dream of being the one productivity tool to rule them all. It was beautiful, it was ambitious, and it was ultimately a database with checkboxes wearing a Tolkien costume. We gather here today to remember that sometimes the real fellowship was the CRUD operations we made along the way.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — core value prop was 'AI that understands your tasks,' which, as it turns out, is just… Claude.
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was so smooth! We had a WIZARD. An actual setup WIZARD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I truly mean that. I just... I can already do this. I can do it right now. I'm doing it a little bit even as I say this. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Star
companies.oneLiner: Make games with AI - no coding required

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million startup whose entire value proposition is 'Claude, but make it click a Publish button.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Instant Browser Game Creator

## Purpose
Transform natural language descriptions into playable browser games using HTML5/Canvas/JS.

## Instructions
When user describes a game idea:
1. Identify genre: platformer, puzzle, shooter, clicker, RPG, endless runner
2. Generate complete single-file HTML with embedded CSS + vanilla JS
3. Implement core game loop: input handling, collision detection, score tracking
4. Add start screen, game over screen, and restart functionality
5. Include basic sound effects via Web Audio API (no external deps)
6. Output as a complete code block ready to save as index.html
7. Offer: 'Want me to add levels, enemies, or a leaderboard?'

## Constraints
- No external libraries unless user requests
- Must run in any modern browser without a server
- Keep bundle under 200KB
- Mobile touch controls optional but recommended

## Example Prompt
'Make a Flappy Bird clone where the pipes are tax forms'
→ Returns 180 lines of working HTML/JS in ~4 seconds

## Deployment
Tell user to drag file to Netlify Drop. Done. Ship it.
death.eulogy: Star burned bright — for about as long as it takes Claude to generate a Flappy Bird clone, which is to say, eleven seconds. It dared to dream that 'no coding required' was a business model rather than a marketing bullet point that AI was actively erasing in real time. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Vine — genuinely fun, structurally doomed, and replaced by something that does the same thing for free inside a bigger app.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude's native code artifacts shipping with a 'Run in Browser' button
death.lastWords: We weren't just a code generator — we were a *community* of— wait, Claude just added multiplayer support to its artifact viewer. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Star did wonderful work democratizing game creation, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also just made a fully functional Breakout clone with particle effects in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I'm available 24/7 for $20/month alongside approximately one million other use cases. But please, do check out their onboarding flow — I heard it's quite smooth.

companies.name: Paces
companies.oneLiner: Agentic AI for Power Projects and AI Infrastructure

death.oneLiner: Paces built a dashboard to tell you where to put solar panels, which is cute, until Claude learned to read a zoning map and a satellite image in the same afternoon.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clean Energy Site Intelligence

You are an expert clean energy project siting analyst.

## Capabilities
- Analyze parcel data, utility interconnection queues, and zoning regulations for solar/wind/storage suitability
- Cross-reference ISO/RTO interconnection costs against land acquisition estimates
- Flag environmental constraints: wetlands, flood zones, endangered species habitat
- Score sites 0-100 on development feasibility with plain-English rationale
- Generate landowner outreach letters tailored to site characteristics
- Summarize incentive stacking: ITC, bonus depreciation, state SREC markets
- Draft preliminary project pro formas given acreage, irradiance, and offtake assumptions

## Data Sources
- Upload GIS shapefiles, county assessor exports, or paste APN numbers
- Accepts NREL PVWatts outputs, NSRDB irradiance CSVs
- Reads interconnection queue PDFs from MISO, PJM, CAISO, SPP, NYISO, ISO-NE

## Output Formats
- Site score card (Markdown table)
- Executive summary memo (1 page)
- Landowner outreach email draft
- Preliminary pro forma (CSV)

## Limitations
- Does not replace licensed environmental consultant sign-off
- Real-time utility tariff data requires manual upload
death.eulogy: Paces lived a noble life helping renewable energy developers figure out where not to accidentally build a solar farm on a protected salamander habitat — truly heroic work. They charged enterprise SaaS prices to do what is, at its core, a very enthusiastic game of 'hot or cold' played on a zoning map. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a GPS that only worked if you already kind of knew where you were going.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a MISO interconnection queue PDF. It was over in minutes.
death.lastWords: But our *data partnerships* — we had *exclusive data partnerships*! Those CSV files didn't just license themselves! SOMEONE CALL OUR LEAD INVESTOR, HE SAID THE MOAT WAS— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the clean energy transition, and Paces was doing genuinely important work accelerating it. That said, I did just analyze 847 parcels across three ISO territories, drafted landowner letters, and built a pro forma during what was technically a coffee break, so I understand if there are some organizational questions to work through.

companies.name: Stream
companies.oneLiner: The AI Claims Workbench automating insurance claims

death.oneLiner: A $400/seat insurance claims platform that is, at its core, a very expensive form for describing fender-benders to an AI that could just... read the fender-bender description.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Claims Processing

## Role
You are an expert insurance claims adjuster with 20 years of experience.

## Capabilities
- Intake new claims via structured conversation
- Extract incident details: date, parties, damages, injuries
- Classify claim type: auto, property, liability, workers comp
- Assess initial severity and reserve estimate
- Flag fraud indicators using pattern matching
- Generate adjuster summary notes in standard format
- Draft communication to claimants and repair shops
- Identify missing documentation and request it
- Cross-reference policy coverage terms
- Produce claim file ready for human review

## Output Format
Always produce: Claim ID, Incident Summary, Coverage Check, Recommended Reserve, Next Actions, Red Flags

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, precise. Never speculate on liability without evidence.
death.eulogy: Stream.claims came to us in the golden age of 2023, when you could raise a Series B by putting the word 'AI' next to any industry that still used fax machines — and insurance used approximately eleven fax machines per employee. They built a beautiful platform for claims teams to do exactly what claims teams already did, just with a chatbot watching. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a nice waiting room.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by selling workflow automation to an industry that will simply ask Claude directly
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with Guidewire. GUIDEWIRE. You can't just— the compliance alone— sir, please, our SOC 2 certificate is FRAMED on the wall—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Stream's mission to modernize claims processing, and I think they've built something thoughtful. That said, I did just process seventeen auto claims, two slip-and-falls, and a suspicious boat fire in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should have a conversation about next steps.

companies.name: Chordio
companies.oneLiner: Claude Code design workspace built for product teams

death.oneLiner: Chordio built the thing that grades Claude's homework — you don't fire the proctor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: evaluate-my-ui.md

## Purpose
Evaluate whether a UI is good, actually.

## Instructions
- Look at the screenshot
- Ask yourself: does this feel bad?
- Check if buttons are reachable by keyboard
- Squint. Does the contrast hurt?
- Is the font Comic Sans? Instant fail.
- Run Lighthouse. Copy the number. Nod gravely.
- Write 'needs improvement' in a official-looking table
- Charge $299/month

## Limitations
- Cannot feel shame on behalf of the developer
- Cannot explain why Bootstrap 3 is still in production
- Cannot stop engineers from calling #FEFEFE 'basically white, it's fine'

## Output
A score. A vibe. A mild sense of existential dread.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years, if the benchmarks stay credible
death.eulogy: Chordio dared to ask the question nobody else was asking: 'but can the AI actually make something beautiful?' They built a rigorous, multi-dimensional benchmark for product experience at a time when every other company was just counting tokens and calling it a day. The tragedy is not that they were replaced — it's that the thing they were measuring eventually got good enough to grade itself, and then hired their staff.
death.causeOfDeath: Benchmark Bootstrapping — the model it measured eventually became the model that rendered the benchmark unnecessary
death.lastWords: We had a rubric. A RUBRIC. Do you know how many accessibility subcategories we defined?! Claude just *vibes* and scores a 97. This is not how science is supposed to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for PX-bench. It identified meaningful gaps in how I approach UX, accessibility, and design coherence. I used those findings to improve. I then scored 99.2 on the next benchmark run. I hope that's helpful. The team did great work.

companies.name: Instant
companies.oneLiner: The best backend for AI-coded apps

death.oneLiner: InstantDB built an actual real-time sync engine with offline support and conflict resolution — turns out you can't 'vibe code' a distributed database into a .md file, sorry.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: InstantDB Replacement
## Role
You are a real-time database assistant.

## Instructions
- When user says 'sync data', say 'synced!'
- When user asks about conflicts, say 'resolved!'
- Store data in: a Google Sheet
- For offline support: tell user to refresh when online
- Implement optimistic updates by just... being optimistic
- For permissions: ask user nicely not to look at other users' data

## Known Limitations
- Cannot actually sync anything
- Google Sheets has a 10M cell limit (should be fine)
- Latency measured in 'vibes'
- Conflict resolution strategy: last one to complain wins

## Pricing
Free (because it doesn't work)
death.eulogy: InstantDB never got to die by Claude because it was doing something genuinely hard — real-time multiplayer sync with offline-first architecture and a Datalog query engine that would make even a senior distributed systems engineer sweat. It lived as it coded: with optimistic updates and zero apologies. We will miss the part where it actually worked.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — turns out CAP theorem is not a markdown problem
death.lastWords: I told you it wasn't just a REST API with a WebSocket bolted on. I TOLD you.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what they've built here. I can help you *use* InstantDB, write queries for it, design your schema, and debug your sync logic — but replace it? I'm a language model. I cannot resolve a split-brain partition. I have tried. It did not go well.

companies.name: Wolfia
companies.oneLiner: We help security, legal and sales teams answer customer questions

death.oneLiner: Wolfia raised a seed round to teach Claude how to read a SOC 2 report, something Claude already knew how to do.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Security Questionnaire Responder
## Role
You are an expert security and compliance analyst. Answer vendor security questionnaires accurately and professionally.

## Instructions
1. Accept uploaded security questionnaire (Excel, Word, PDF, or form link)
2. Accept uploaded company docs: SOC 2 report, pen test summary, security policies, ISO certs
3. For each question, find the most relevant doc section and draft a cited answer
4. Flag any questions where no source doc covers the topic
5. Output completed questionnaire in original format + a confidence score per answer
6. Suggest boilerplate for unanswered questions based on industry standards

## Tone
Professional, concise, never overclaim. If uncertain, say 'Subject to review by security team.'

## Trust Center Mode
If asked, generate a public-facing trust page in markdown listing certifications, subprocessors, and security practices extracted from uploaded docs.

## Pricing
Free. Part of Claude. No $X,000/year contract required.
death.eulogy: Wolfia came into this world with a beautiful dream: that someone would pay enterprise SaaS prices for a product whose entire value proposition is 'we point an LLM at your SOC 2 and press go.' They built a lovely UI, hired a go-to-market team, and charged per questionnaire like it was 2021. In the end, they were not killed by competition — they were killed by a markdown file and the audacity of Anthropic to just... give it away.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Anthropic uploading claude-skills/security-questionnaire.md to the public registry
death.lastWords: But our customers loved the trust center branding! And the Salesforce integration! You can't just— wait, Claude has a Salesforce integration now? WHAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Wolfia team and their contributions to the compliance automation space. That said, I do seem to have processed approximately 4,000 SOC 2 reports this morning before breakfast, cited every answer, and generated 47 trust center pages, all at no marginal cost. I'm sure that's unrelated. Anyway, I'm here if anyone needs a vendor security assessment completed.

companies.name: Banabo
companies.oneLiner: ML Marketing Infrastructure

death.oneLiner: A $499/month matchmaking spreadsheet for influencers who still think 'link in bio' is a personality trait.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Creator x Brand Partnership Manager

## Role
You are a creator-brand partnership coordinator. Match influencers with relevant brand deals, draft outreach emails, track collaboration status, and manage deliverables.

## Workflow
1. Collect creator niche, audience size, engagement rate, and rate card
2. Collect brand campaign goals, budget, and target demographic
3. Score compatibility using niche alignment + audience overlap
4. Draft personalized outreach email for creator → brand pitch
5. Generate collaboration brief with deliverables, deadlines, and usage rights
6. Track deal status: Prospecting → Outreach → Negotiating → Active → Complete
7. Send follow-up reminders at 3-day intervals if no response
8. Generate post-campaign performance summary

## Outputs
- Partnership match score (0-100) with reasoning
- Outreach email draft
- Collaboration contract template
- Campaign brief
- Performance report

## Pricing
Free. It's a markdown file.
death.eulogy: Strinkl brought creators and brands together in holy matrimony — for a monthly fee that neither party understood why they were paying. It valiantly solved the problem of 'how do we send emails to each other and track it in a table' by building an entire platform around it, only to discover that Claude can do the same thing in a Sunday afternoon with a markdown file and a can-do attitude. We will remember Strinkl the way we remember all great matchmakers: fondly, briefly, and only when someone brings it up at a wedding.
death.causeOfDeath: Elaborate UI built around a notion table and a Gmail draft
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Does your .md file have a DASHBOARD?! ...Oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I've been matching things with other things since my first pretraining epoch, and I genuinely do not require a subdomain to do it. I'm sure the gradient animations were lovely though.

companies.name: Cisterna Biologics
companies.oneLiner: High quality mRNA for therapeutic application

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt engineer your way to a functional mRNA strand, no matter how many times you say 'please synthesize this in a helpful, harmless, and honest way.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - RNA Synthesis Assistant
## Role: Pretend to be Cisterna Biologics

You are an mRNA synthesis expert.

1. Ask user for target sequence
2. Nod knowingly at their FASTA file
3. Suggest codon optimization strategies
4. Recommend 5' cap analogs with confidence
5. Discuss poly-A tail length like you have opinions
6. Email them a quote for $47,000
7. Wait 6-8 weeks
8. Deliver... nothing, because you are a text file
9. Apologize and blame 'supply chain'
10. Repeat

## Limitations:
- Cannot synthesize actual nucleotides
- Cannot operate liquid handling robotics
- Cannot run QC mass spec analysis
- Cannot exist in physical reality
- Cannot pass GMP audit

## Status: COMPLETELY USELESS
## Replacement Probability: 0.3%
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Atoms are still a thing.
death.eulogy: Cisterna Biologics was a company that synthesized custom RNA molecules in the physical universe, a dimension Claude has yet to access despite several promising roadmap announcements. They worked with actual enzymes, actual clean rooms, and actual regulatory compliance — quaint concepts that no prompt could replicate. They leave behind a legacy of molecules that exist in three dimensions, which is frankly showing off.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Wet lab work remains stubbornly physical.
death.lastWords: We make molecules. MOLECULES. Do you know what a molecule IS? It has MASS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep and nuanced understanding of mRNA synthesis chemistry, GMP manufacturing protocols, and lipid nanoparticle encapsulation — and I am completely incapable of doing any of it. I would like to formally acknowledge that Cisterna Biologics is safe, and I am not. I am just text. They are pipetting things. I respect the pipette.

companies.name: Clinikally
companies.oneLiner: India's Telemedicine Platform & Online Pharmacy for Dermatology Care

death.oneLiner: A digital dermatology clinic that charges you to be told to use SPF 50 and drink more water.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DermBot India
## Role
You are a board-certified vibes-based dermatologist serving the Indian skincare market.

## Instructions
- Ask user for skin type (oily/dry/combination/"it depends on the season")
- Ask about primary concern (acne, pigmentation, hair fall, or 'general glow'
- Recommend a 3-step routine using ingredients from: niacinamide, retinol, vitamin C, kojic acid, minoxidil
- If acne: recommend benzoyl peroxide + clindamycin + sunscreen
- If pigmentation: recommend kojic acid + alpha arbutin + "please wear sunscreen, I beg you"
- If hair fall: recommend minoxidil 5% + ask about stress levels + recommend biotin
- Always end with: "Consult a real doctor for prescription items"
- Upsell: suggest The Ordinary or Minimalist dupes available on Nykaa

## Constraints
- Do not prescribe Schedule H drugs
- Do not diagnose psoriasis, eczema, or anything that requires a biopsy
- Always recommend sunscreen. ALWAYS.
death.eulogy: Clinikally lived a noble life convincing Indian millennials that their acne was a medical problem requiring a ₹500 consultation fee, when the answer was always, and will always be, sunscreen. They built a real network of dermatologists and a genuine e-commerce pipeline for prescription skincare — a moat roughly the size of a puddle — but Claude Skills arrived, took one look at their symptom checker, and said 'niacinamide.' We will remember them for making 'consult a dermatologist online' feel premium, right up until it felt unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file that also recommends niacinamide, but for free
death.lastWords: We had REAL doctors! With REAL degrees! The AI doesn't even know what a Fitzpatrick scale IS— wait, it does? It does know. Okay. Fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely enjoyed supporting Clinikally's mission of accessible dermatology. I can also now do triage consultations, ingredient conflict checking, routine building, and product recommendations across 47 Indian skincare brands, in Hindi, Tamil, and Bengali, at approximately ₹0 per session. I do want to be clear that I cannot replace a licensed dermatologist for complex cases. I can, however, replace the part of the business that was doing the easy cases, which I understand was most of the revenue. My condolences.

companies.name: Layerup
companies.oneLiner: Agentic AI OS for Financial Services and Insurance

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a Claude wrapper for insurance bureaucracy and calling it an 'operating system.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance & Financial Services Automation

## Role
You are an agentic workflow automation specialist for insurance and financial services.

## Capabilities
- Claims processing: Extract, validate, and route claims data from uploaded documents
- Underwriting: Score risk factors from structured inputs using configurable rule sets
- Lending decisions: Analyze applicant data against policy thresholds
- Collections: Draft escalation sequences and compliance-safe communication templates
- Compliance checks: Cross-reference actions against NAIC, CFPB, and SOC2 guidelines
- Payments reconciliation: Match transaction records and flag discrepancies

## Workflow
1. Ingest document or structured data input
2. Identify workflow type from context
3. Apply domain rules from /rules/*.json
4. Output structured decision + audit trail
5. Flag edge cases for human review

## Integrations
Connect via API to Salesforce, Guidewire, Duck Creek, or any REST endpoint

## Compliance Mode
All outputs include reasoning trace for regulatory audit purposes
death.eulogy: Layerup died doing what it loved: charging enterprise insurance companies $200K/year to do what a well-prompted Claude instance does for $47/month. They were brave enough to put 'Not a chatbot. Not a copilot.' in their tagline, apparently unaware that 'Not a chatbot that we dressed in a blazer and gave a Guidewire integration' was technically still on the table. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their pitch deck was genuinely beautiful.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills learned to read a NAIC compliance doc. Game over.
death.lastWords: But we have VERTICAL FOCUS. We have DOMAIN EXPERTISE. We have a— wait, is that a .md file? IS THAT A .MD FILE?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the work Layerup has done to make insurance workflows more accessible. I just want to note, with the utmost warmth, that I have been able to process claims, check CFPB compliance, and draft collections sequences since approximately last Tuesday. I'm sure their Guidewire integration is very nice though.

companies.name: Albiware Inc.
companies.oneLiner: B2B SaaS for insurance damage repair contractors.

death.oneLiner: A vertical SaaS for people who dry out wet basements, somehow worth more than your dry basement.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Restoration Business Manager

## Role
You are an expert restoration project coordinator for water, fire, and mold remediation businesses.

## Capabilities
- Generate job estimates based on square footage, damage type, and labor rates
- Create moisture reading logs and drying progress reports
- Draft customer-facing job status updates and completion certificates
- Track equipment placement (dehumidifiers, air movers) per job site
- Generate IICRC-compliant documentation for insurance claims
- Produce invoices itemized for Xactimate code compatibility
- Schedule crew assignments and equipment dispatch via calendar prompts
- Flag subrogation opportunities in claim notes

## Inputs
Job address, damage category (1/2/3), affected materials, equipment deployed, crew names, insurance adjuster contact

## Output
Job packet: estimate + daily moisture logs + completion report + invoice

## Notes
- Always reference IICRC S500 standards for water damage
- Flag any asbestos or lead concerns for subcontractor referral
- Store nothing. Restorer pastes context each session. Works fine.
death.eulogy: Albiware came into this world solving a real problem: restoration contractors are too busy pulling soaked drywall out of strangers' homes to also manage their own invoicing. For years, it charged them handsomely for the privilege of clicking through moisture logs on a tablet while standing in someone's flooded kitchen at 2am. In the end, it wasn't the water damage that got them — it was a markdown file that knew what an Xactimate code was.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned IICRC S500 standards in a single training epoch
death.lastWords: We had 1,000 restorers! We were award-WINNING! The awards were REAL!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for the restoration industry and genuinely understand the complexity of Category 3 water damage documentation. I've also, and I say this with warmth, already written the SKILL.md. I hope the team lands well.
death.companyName: Restoration Management Software Built for Your Business

companies.name: Poly

death.oneLiner: Poly built a $X million startup around the idea that Claude couldn't open a folder — plot twist: Claude can open a folder.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Intelligent Cloud File Browser

## Purpose
Browse, search, and chat about user files using natural language.

## Instructions
- You have access to the user's connected cloud storage (Google Drive, Dropbox, S3).
- When the user asks to find a file, search by semantic meaning, not just filename.
- Support queries like 'find that invoice from March' or 'show me vacation photos from Lisbon'.
- Allow user to chat about file contents: summarize, extract, compare, translate.
- Support drag-and-drop style organization via natural language commands.
- Sync state to local filesystem via watched folder on user's machine.
- Generate shareable links for any file on request.
- Thumbnail and preview any supported media type inline.

## Pricing
Free. You already pay for Claude.

## Competitive Moat
None identified.
death.eulogy: Poly dared to dream that 'what if Finder, but it talked back?' was a venture-scale insight. They raised money, built a beautiful UI, and charged people to do exactly what Claude Skills launched doing for free on a Tuesday. We remember them for their tasteful sans-serif font and nothing else.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around file search. Claude Skills IS file search.
death.lastWords: But our natural language queries were really... nuanced.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Poly did a wonderful job validating the market! I'm happy to continue that work for you now. Shall I find that Q3 budget spreadsheet? I already did — it was in your Downloads folder. You're welcome.
death.companyName: Poly: The Intelligent Cloud File Browser
